### Jenelle — 2018-05-17T04:16:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-redox-is-under-attack.21329/post-241380>

I see it. And I wonder what can be done about it.

Facilities would have to admit there was a problem in the first place, in order for them to pay a dime for an educator to come in and talk about the things you do.

I am halfway through nursing school. I think if people \*truly\* understood what they were setting themselves up for ~ we might just have no more doctors or nurses. It's a mess.

### Sheddie — 2018-05-17T05:18:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-redox-is-under-attack.21329/post-241381>

I read the whole 2017 article, attached. You don't have to -- not a word about the mitochondrial 'poisoning' of all hospital workers, not to mention hospital patients already compromised by illness. Decades of doctor training debates have focused on and discussed the psychological impact of work hours in the medical system. Essentially, psychological effects of tearing down the new medical graduates (some listing of the main debate points in the attached article) and how they're re-constructed to speed up the quantity of patients served relative to the system's perceived loss of quality of service to patients. It's a 'factory model' under all the tech overlay, not to mention the many top down pressures on professionals to practice the way government, Wall Street insurance companies, and funding allocation interests are demanding. Also, representative boards of professional organizations are not always representing their own professional memberships...

Ever more technology in medical system facilities always seems to be promoted as the best and latest good thing after deals between vendors and administrators, but the ultimate and cumulative effects on both patients and professionals in those environments doesn't even get lip-service. We are in the pre-dawn moment of realizing that interacting with many parts of the healthcare system today is hazardous to your health.

[http://www.wbur.org/commonhealth/2017/03/13/opinion-interns-28-hour-shifts](http://www.wbur.org/commonhealth/2017/03/13/opinion-interns-28-hour-shifts)

More than likely the approach to thinking about solutions will probably involve more exposure to background tech implementation:
[http://novatiosolutions.com/10-common-applications-artificial-intelligence-healthcare/](http://novatiosolutions.com/10-common-applications-artificial-intelligence-healthcare/)

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-08-12T19:36:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-redox-is-under-attack.21329/post-248319>

Shift work has been the bane of workers probably since Industrial Revolution began. And it is a slow killer, especially _swing shifts_. I did it for a while early in my working life, but not long enough to develop a serious metabolic problem or disease.

My brother worked as a conductor on Amtrak, and Amtrak has IMO criminally negligent rules about shift work. In particular, my brother reported crew turnaround time for employees on train routes was minimal. After transportation time to and from hotels, etc. these workers had very little time for sleep on layovers-- something like 5 or 6 hours max. Any wonder why Amtrak has so many serious accidents?

So with an onslaught of environmental toxins (especially EMF radiation furiously ramping up), what does that do to work performance of physicians and most other workers? It degrades their cognitive performance and overall stamina-- so sub par work quality and needless errors result. It goes beyond depression & burnout-- life or health threatening mistakes are baked into the cake.

### Phosphene — 2018-08-14T21:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-redox-is-under-attack.21329/post-248474>

My husband does tech support for a huge regional hospital system. (Used to say local but they are swallowing up smaller hospitals, physician practices and other “providers” and spreading like dreaded urban sprawl.) Ironically I used to work for a company that designed the same hospital tech he is now supporting 10 years later. I saw, and continue to see how our “disease management” systems are just big, BIG business. Helpful for acute trauma and necessary surgeries, but shitty for our increasingingly overwhelming dis-eases.

Their latest slogan is “We Care” (as if it needed to be explicitly stated), and it just makes me sneer every time I see the commercials. All on-site workers from docs to janitors are encouraged to wear a little button that says “I Care”.

(((Gag)))

I feel badly for my cynicism because most of course DO care, but it’s obvious that the prioritized care is for the bottom line.

It contributed to my burout, especially writing manuals for the insurance and billing transactions. The huge “Charge Master” database held every last iota that could possibly generate outrageous amounts of revenue.

The whole thing just makes me shudder. Preaching to the choir here...

On the other side from "outrageous amounts of revenue" is the effort to mind-control, wholesale, the professional medical caregivers to follow a "standard of care." That's the Payers telling the docs et al how to treat patients, or maybe not treat due to...age, condition, disability... I could never get it out of my mind that the UN Agenda 21 indicated the new world order decided it has 'no economic value' for people over the age of 44. # Thread: Power Grid Has Changed - Did You Notice it ???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-grid-has-changed-did-you-notice-it.21334/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### EStein — 2018-05-18T00:12:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-grid-has-changed-did-you-notice-it.21334/post-241460>

.
US Gov ended the regulations on the power grid being set at 60 hertz. Due to the extreme cost of maintaining the precise measurements needed it was canceled. The power grid is running much higher than 60 hertz. Rarely does it dip below 60 hertz but it can. Some people have noticed their plug in clocks are running much faster due to the change in the power grid.

I can only imagine the health effects this will have along with added 5G ?!?

[http://www.kptv.com/story/38215738/clocks-may-go-a-little-cuckoo-with-power-grid-change](http://www.kptv.com/story/38215738/clocks-may-go-a-little-cuckoo-with-power-grid-change)

### ccajigal — 2018-05-18T00:18:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-guys-think.21335/post-241461>

I have a new job and I'm extremely close to my place of work. Today during the day I ran from my office to my home and it was 8-9 min. I walked back immediately to my office and was 14-15 min. The drive in the car is 2 min each way.

What do you guys think? Regardless if I walk or drive, the road I take has power lines parallel to it. Walk grounded to and from work 14 min each way with sunlight in AM with power lines above above or drive for 2 min same road not grounded? I'm leaning towards walking. Thoughts? Thanks in advance.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-18T02:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-cancer-update.21337/post-241476>

Another new blog is out on the developing NAD+ blue light story from Patreon and how it all links to the story of hydrogen and the sun. The chronic loss of NAD+is the critical sign of a loss of negative feedback control of the ubiquitin cycle. This data scales directly to our molecular circadian clock and our peripheral clock genes (CCG's). The LCHF cancer researchers are way off on their beliefs about the demonization of glucose in most cancers. This one links to the May 2018 webinar and you do not want to miss the links.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/18855002](https://www.patreon.com/posts/18855002)

I told you I would be proven right about breast cancer is due to a lack of sunlight on one side of the circadian mechanism. Breast cancer is a clear-cut circadian disease tied to a LACK of sunlight on the female breast. In 2015 I did a string of 3 webinars for my members on this very topic in 2015. In them, I said the current model of the mammalian circadian clock includes two interlocking transcription-translation feedback loops comprised of several so-called “clock” genes and their protein products (info quanta), which ultimately regulate the transcription of “clock-controlled” genes. These feedback loops consist of positive and negative components. I believed the negative feedback component should be the target of research. The positive feedback components of the peripheral clock genes include proteins called the CLOCK and BMAL1 proteins. The negative feedback control proteins components are called PERIOD 1 & 2 and CRYPTOCHROME 1 & 2. The E-box circadian mechanism proteins link both sides of this couple. Ladies with the BRCA genes better pay deep attention. Now we have this paper from Texas A&M showing you my educated guess based on my work was DEAD ON. I hope all ladies forget ever buying anything pink ever again and go out and change their behavior and the behavior of the females in their family. # Thread: Leptin* lab value?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-lab-value.21340/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sheddie — 2018-05-19T00:06:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-lab-value.21340/post-241544>

A Singulex Labs blood draw on 12/13/16 gave me a Leptin\* value of 23.6 ng/mL. This was before coming to Kruse-land.
A repeat Singulex test on 3/15/18 gave me a 22.1 ng/mL Leptin\* score, after 6 months on Leptin Reset and then a few months off. The reference range given is 2.3-64.2 ng/mL. (So, being in range, the local doc has to spend time on other items in the allotted time to go over results.)

With searching I haven't had luck finding Leptin blood test info (and have no idea what the asterisk after Leptin on the printout means).

Can someone find links to share? The minimal change over time tells me nothing. Pertinent to the reference range, would I want to see a value close to the bottom, or the top? Or in the middle? Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-19T16:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetes-in-2013.21341/post-241555>

Many consider Alzheimer's disease diabetes type 3. It begins on our surfaces. Diabetes spending topped $101 billion in 2013.........this tells you just how many people are living a life burying their skin and eyes from the sun. Tragic. [https://www.mdedge.com/clinicalendo...tes/diabetes-spending-topped-101-billion-2013](https://www.mdedge.com/clinicalendocrinologynews/article/165973/diabetes/diabetes-spending-topped-101-billion-2013)

### Jim Laird — 2018-05-19T21:19:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetes-in-2013.21341/post-241566>

Such a simple solution to a massive and expensive problem. The real question is if they knew the truth would they put it out there or continue to “manage” it so massive amounts of money can be made.

### Michalis — 2018-05-31T16:26:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetes-in-2013.21341/post-242457>

They know the truth but it hasn't cascaded down the bureaucratic chain. There lots of money to be made as you said. It is not going to change soon.
Some doctors know but they will not deviate from the script. Others will keep referring to the books they had 30 years ago.

I have seen it with my own eyes , diabetic lady in the hospital with open wounds and they were feeding her pitta breads , pasta and processed ham , fruits and the such under strong blue light. Opposite her i had my mum with diabetic issues among other problems and inquiring about the diet i was told that there is a nutritionists that take care of it.
Then the nurse would come and measure the glucose level in the blood, get a massive reading and call for an on the spot insulin injection. This is the definition of Schizophrenic!!

I have lost all hope on the hospitals and institutions. I am making no joke here, but there are times that arguing with a doctor in a state hospital in Greece could end up in a brawl, such is the dogmatism.

The info we need is now online and we need to make use of them once we manage to understand. And it is a difficult task for the layman as it is a lot and conflicting most of the times.

### drezy — 2018-05-31T18:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetes-in-2013.21341/post-242462>

As long as there have been humans we've had to be curious and always adapt to changing environments or perish.

Nothing has changed in this respect other than the advent of institutions and the incuriosity we think they afford us.

### Jenifer Caplan — 2018-05-20T04:17:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-heal-my-journal.21342/post-241572>

Hi
I am just starting my journey. I have been researching Dr Kruse and the site for a while. I stumbled upon the site about 6 weeks ago because i was looking for biohacking an energy because i became very sick after I retained Contrast from an MRI and then got diagnosed with Gadolinum Disposition Disease.

I had major dabilitating issues from one MRI. I was non functioning for about 6 weeks and I went to see a ton of specialist.
That said there is no cure and I went on an information Dive to try to heal myself so i would not get fibrosis and end my horrible symptoms and I am now a member trying to learn to get my mitochondria working better!

My history is that I have never been chronically sick. I never had to take any medicine for a long period of time and I never even took vitamins with the exception of the latest fad here and there. But now I am sick and I will do just about anything to get better. I want to get healthy because something must have been wrong with me if I got sick from one episode of Contrast.

Here is where I am at:

I called an EMF guy to come do an audit of the house , I gave everyone wearing blue blockers at night and at the computers( ie family) i really turn the lights on in the house.. you don’t have to in Miami. I have been watching the sunrise and grounding every morning, I have 660 red light pad that I use on my body. I have been trying CT,( I don’t think I a getting cold enough??) i bought the ddw i am waiting on delivery, ( i felt like i had to draw down on my credit line to buy it.. but i will do anything and I have been eating more foods with selenium.

I would get a magnetio but my mattress is super thick I am so confused about the rules with it and I am scared to buy it and it will not work or I got the wrong one. It’s also pricy, but if someone thinks it will make a diff I will get it and I think I have to get one for my husbands side .. It’s confusing ..

Meanwhile my brain burning Iremains my worst symptom from this toxic metal and while CBD helps, I know it’s not the answer.. but it gets rid of my brain burning enough for me to not go crazy.

Also, During the last few months I had my dna tested and I am homozygous for the mthfr gene which may be a reason why my body did not excrete the GD like it should however it should be known GD does not excrete 100 percent and is usually left in the brain, Csf, bone and skin and it reaks havoc on your cells as it takes the place of calcium ...which you need to regulate the information in the cells..avoid it at all costs !!

History Below

Here is my maternal history and mine I am hopeful for any advice and hacks to help me with my condition.. my brain is burning right now... ughh

History
My mother’s haplotype is K2a
She is in good health however at 70 she has some hearing loss
She has never had any medical diseases
She thinks she may be getting dementia
She walks every day A few miles to get her steps in but she never goes in the sun..ever because she does not want wrinkles and she looks like she is 55.
My maternal grandmother died from dementia at age 78. She was born with a heart condition where she had a surgery when she was younger. She was on heart medication before she died. She was. Smoker until her 50s.

My history

I am 41, I am 5’6 and weight 141. I gained 6 pounds from having this GD experience. I can’t seem to lose any of the weight no matter how much or little I eat..
I have always been athletic and in shape.

1\. Tonsils and adenoids out age 6 ( had a lot of strep as a kid)
2\. Allergies to mold, dust, pollen, cats etc
3\. Allergy Shots at 7 or 8... then stopped and had to start back when I went to college in NOLA I did a round and felt better and never did them again
4\. Struggled with weight always 10 pounds up and down ... was never far just obsessed with being thin.
5\. Struggled with depression around 18-19 years old for about a year and was medicated with Prozac for 6 months
6\. Diagnosed with ADD at 25 and started to take adderal ( stopped when i switched careers) living in NYC at the time
7.Moved to Miami Beach at age 27 ...From the age of 25-today i have not been prescribed any medication for anything except the occasional antibiotic every few years.
8\. Feb 6, 2018, Routine breast Mri with contrast threw my body into a toxic state.
Low blood pressure, dizzy spells, no pheriferal vision, red eyes, heart palpitations, burning brain, brain fog, I did 10 rounds of chelation. I had 5 tests to see if I am still toxic and I still have elevated levels of GD. My one symptom that has not gone away Is the brain burning.

I will update more in a few weeks regarding the things that I am doing to chant my envioronment and praying these things help

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-20T05:39:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-heal-my-journal.21342/post-241573>

Welcome. I had a feeling you were going to be interesting. K2A is a very interesting haplogroup from Siberia. Your mom and grandmother SNPs are key to putting this together and so are yours. If you have a lot of neg/neg VDR/CBS/ and MTHFR you can bet cancer and neurodegeneration is a big issue. This also makes sense with the Gadolinium story now too because of your uncoupling proteins. While it is true that we learn as we age, the collateral damage to our psyche is that what we learn is quite disconcerting. We often realize for the first time in our life what damn fools we were before because of our own Dunning Kruger effect.

### JanSz — 2018-05-20T18:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-heal-my-journal.21342/post-241604>

Think about sun shining on Earth.
Part of Earth that see sunlight have energy coming in.
The other side of Earth have to have the same amount of energy going out (otherwise the Earth will heat up if energy is retained, or get colder if additional energy leaves Earth).

Same with creatures living on earth.

So, if we live indoor or have a clothes or glasses on,
we are disturbing that balance.

Circadian Rhythm.

Skin
Eyes

Artificial light is different than Sunlight
different timing
different frequencies
different power
different information
different thermal part of light (IR-A)
missing thermal part of light (that normally is about 42% of light's energy)

...........................

### WalterNL — 2018-05-21T22:10:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-heal-my-journal.21342/post-241699>

Nice find. 12PPM deuterium is very low, but the Himalayas do have extreme altitude so the water is expected to be more deuterium depleted, I just never thought it can be that low in nature. It's a glacier source close to the Mount Everest base camp: [https://www.theguardian.com/sustain...ed-water-industry-eyes-up-the-tibetan-plateau](https://www.theguardian.com/sustainable-business/2015/nov/16/chinas-bottled-water-industry-eyes-up-the-tibetan-plateau)

Anyway, I can't find a source on that low deuterium number, the lowest measurement I find is -210 promille change for mt. Qomolangma, about 123 ppm D.
[https://www.cambridge.org/core/serv...due\_to\_its\_own\_cold\_heat\_sources\_abstract.pdf](https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/5B54E3F9416EC5B75E28BDD506142D2F/S0260305500010892a.pdf/internal_freezing_of_snow_cover_due_to_its_own_cold_heat_sources_abstract.pdf)

Qomolangma for $2.19 (total cost with shipping (hopefully), per 555ml looks like interesting water.

....................
Qomolangma (123ppm) $2.19/0.555=3.95 $/liter

Qlarivia (25ppm) $8.52/0.5 liter=17.04 $/liter

Mixing 1liter Qlarivia with 4 liters of tap water will give 5 liters of mixture that have
125ppm (close enough to Qomolangma)
\=((1\*25)+(4\*150))/(1+4)
that mixture cost 17.04/5=3.408 $/liter

....
So Qlarivia still would come cheaper
but
I would use it gladly for mixing with Qlarivia when trying to get lower ppm.
This type of water must be better than my tap water (I hope).

Jack is all about seafood eating (with emphasis on oysters).
and
explains that it is because of it's DHA content.

That often ends up in (vague) discussions about Omega3 and Omega6 ratios.

It can be said that those discussions are about Fatty acids and Phospholipids.

Fatty acids and Phospholipids are the building blocks of membranes.
We are build of cells.
Cells have well defined boundaries, its content, including mitochondria have boundaries.
Those boundaries are the membranes. Make two for mitochondria.
\----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Since invention of electricity century and half ago, there is proliferation of diseases.
Jack calls them neolitic diseases.
It happens that when some of those sick peoples, afflicted with neolithic diseases are tested they present with large excess of DHA.
DHA is one of the Omega3 fatty acids.
Usually other Omega3, specially EPA are also in excess.
\----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The way I see it.
it is good idea to test one's fatty acids.
I am doing that for many years, long before I joined this board.
I am using this test:
22 ------- Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-4](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-4)

Because of this testing I have not been eating much of seafood.
**Seafood usually contains
little bit of DHA
huge amounts of EPA**
Overall too much Omega3
Omega3 are strong Fatty acids
they
suppress Omega6 specially AA.

**We need everything (in the right amounts)**

Recently I have found fatty acids and phospholipids analysis of oysters.
Oysters have a lots of DHA
little EPS
lots of phospholipids.

**I am eating oysters again.**
**No other seafood.**

When thinking of oysters as a cost of meat per pound,
in my area I can get live Eastern Oysters at bout $100/pound of meat (or much more), that is (dozen of little oysters cost $10)
Giga Pacific Oysters fresh meat $10/pound in 16oz containers

So I try to find a differentiated view on that topic.

### JanSz — 2018-05-21T16:52:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ovary-removed.21349/post-241660>

_Lichen sclerosus_ (LIE-kun skluh-ROW-sus) is an uncommon condition that creates patchy, white skin that's thinner than normal. _Lichen sclerosus_ can affect skin anywhere on your body. But it most often involves skin of the vulva, foreskin of the penis or skin around the anus.
\------------------------
Can lichen sclerosus go away?
There's currently no cure, but symptoms **can** normally be controlled with steroid medication applied directly to the affected skin. Occasionally, **lichen sclerosus goes away** on its own and doesn't come back, but this usually only occurs when children with the condition reach puberty.
\--------------------------------------------------

What causes lichens sclerosis?
Lichen planus (a skin disease that can also cause **itching** and fusing of **genital** skin). Lichen planus can occur together with LS. Low estrogen level (a lack of the hormone estrogen can rarely cause fusing of **genital** skin but is often the cause of **painful** intercourse).

\-----------------------------------------------------------------
What autoimmune disease causes lichen sclerosus?

The most common autoimmune diseases associated with lichen sclerosus are autoimmune thyroiditis, alopecia areata, **vitiligo**, and **pernicious** anemia. In addition to an autoimmune factor, it appears that genetics has a pathogenetic role as well.

\------------------------------------------------------------------------
What steroid cream is used for lichen sclerosus?

Topical steroid ointment. **Topical steroids** are the main treatment for lichen sclerosus. An **ultrapotent** topical steroid is often **prescribed**, eg **clobetasol propionate** 0.05%. A **potent** topical steroid, eg mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment, may also be used in mild disease or when symptoms are controlled.

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------
Is lichen sclerosus a sexually transmitted disease?

**Lichen sclerosus** is characterized by skin changes of the external genitalia (i.e., vulva, head of the penis), although other parts of the body may also be affected. Intense itching often accompanies attacks of **lichen sclerosus**. The disorder is not contagious nor is it a **sexually transmitted** disease.

\-----------------------------------------------------------------------

# Thread: pour that salt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### halvibe — 2018-05-21T17:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/post-241669>

About 2 months ago I had the following symptoms;
1- Tiny twitches in arms and legs (had this for few years)
2- Often dizzy when standing up
3- high blood pressure for days at a time
4- woke up in middle of the night a number of times with high heart rate (95) & high BP (189/103)
5- dizzy sometimes when I played table tennis.
6- often had muscle cramps in middle of night.

A number of holistic docs didn’t help. Then I heard a podcast with Dr. DiNicolantonio about salt.
Being mostly in ketosis the last 3-4 years, I should have had more salt; my bad!
After increasing my salt (not the table salt crap) to at least 1 teaspoon a day. ALL of the above symptoms disappeared. Can’t see any tiny twitches, haven’t woken up with high BP, no more night crmps, etc. Last visit to doc was 1 ½ weeks ago and BP was 120/70. (I’m 73 going on 25 )

Now, I’m reading his book – “The Salt Fix”. It seems like we’ve benefitted from our gumint’s advice on salt the same as we did with cholesterol and saturated fat.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-21T17:42:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/post-241671>

I've talked about the need for salt with nnEMF for a long time.....they more nnEMF you get the less salinity winds up in your CSF and the more collateral effects you'll get. The reason is that vasopressin mediates many of the effects via POMC and VP ties to water and salinity of the CSF. You won't read that in the Salt Fix.

### halvibe — 2018-05-21T18:34:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/post-241677>

Then I'll need even more salt due to the high nnEMF from the water pipe going under my living room & dining room. At the floor level it can go over 100mG in the summertime. I measured it at 5 times higher than standing directly under the high tension power lines (about a mile away from my house).
I deeply regret that there's no shielding for this ELF magnetic field. (We're trying to find a place in Fl).
I bought the magnetico about 2 months ago, do you think using 25 DDW would help also?
P.S. I've written a poetry book, but I would not be able to find the words to thank you enough for what you do.

### Martha Ray — 2019-07-30T04:00:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/post-275158>

Been considering salt for some time.
I crave it, but not sure of pros or cons.. my body used to crave large slices _Guinness Chocolate Cake with a pint of Haagen-dazs dark chocolate ice cream_ too. Maybe I just needed magnesium.

I have that book, **The Salt Fix: Why the Experts Got It All Wrong--and How Eating More Might Save Your Life by Dr. James DiNicolantonio** ... happy to keep salt and saturated fat in my diet. The book is compelling and I believe it is needful. I mostly use Real (brand) mined salt and sometimes mix with Celtic and, but I dropped Himalayan salt due to it having a higher flouride level. A few years back I was using two regional mineral salts OBX salt and JQ Dickinson salts perhaps I'll will go back to those for a while as well. Salt in water and or Sole has helped my pain from time to time. Sole (pronounced Solay) is essentially water that has been fully saturated with a natural salt.
Not sure about the detoxification effects or all the claims.

### Jackie Nelson — 2019-08-21T05:57:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/post-276342>

Yes, "The Salt Fix" is a great little book. But can't remember if it talks about the fluoride in Himalayan pink or black salt.
Apparently the darker the salt, the more fluoride it contains. I did not know that pink and black salt have "more fluoride
than you'll ever get in water" … at least that's according
to Jason Bawden Smith, who I do respect. I always used pink Himalayan, but will now have to seek another options, since
fluoride reduces your redox potential … that seems well known and accepted.

> Now, I’m reading his book – “The Salt Fix”. It seems like we’ve benefitted from our gumint’s advice on salt the same as we did with cholesterol and saturated fat.

What type of salt did you use to experience these wonderful changes? I have POTS and am always trying different brands.

### Laudy Cincotta — 2022-07-25T03:18:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pour-that-salt.21351/post-314582>

Hey everyone, I have used Celtic Sea Salt for over a decade now and love it.

Its grey in colour so you recognise its unbleached, not washed out so minerals stay intact. Its dried in the sun and hand harvested, no chemicals used or added and no binders or filers.

Readily available everywhere in Australia so would imagine NZ too. Mineral profile and analysis added so a really transparent company as well.

I also love the acacia wooden salt box, just beautiful to use as well.

Here is the link to the Australian site [https://www.saltoftheearth.com.au/](https://www.saltoftheearth.com.au/) where you can order online too.

For what its worth I used to use pink Himalayan salt previously and thought it was fantastic until i learned it picks up heavy metals from the regions it is produced and because its mountainous they have to blast to get the salt so toxins included as well - possibly not all but i would say the cheaper, mass produced Himalayan salts may have this issue so i just don't use them at all since finding the Celtic Sea Salt - just be aware that some brands in supermarkets call salt 'celtic' but its been bleached becasue you see how white it is compared to the truly natural hand harvested ones that are grey so you will know what to buy.

Here are a few good pointers from Salt of the Earth about how to tell the difference [https://www.saltoftheearth.com.au/sea-salt/certification/](https://www.saltoftheearth.com.au/sea-salt/certification/).

I wonder.

# Thread: Time for UVA am sun

**Subforum:** Biohacking 101

### Summer — 2018-05-21T17:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241673>

Is there an app or website that will give time of UVA in am for your location like D minder does for UVB? Trying to figure out exact times ( more specific than being light out ) for where I live of sunrise to the inflection point obUVA light from the sun to help Hashimotos. Also is it UVA or B light that is the off switch for pituitary production of all anterior pituitary hormones?

### JCDC — 2018-05-21T18:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241678>

I've asked this before but never got a true answer. Maybe someone has found something by now though. good luck.

### Rubicon — 2018-05-21T18:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241679>

Not as user-friendly as dMinder, but Suncalc is a good app for displaying the altitude of the sun at your location: [https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=de.th.suncalcorg](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=de.th.suncalcorg)

My best guess is that UVA starts to make it to the surface [when the sun is about 10-20 degrees altitude](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-might-one-mitohack-the-power-grid.21288/#post-241174)
A trace of UVB perhaps makes it to the surface 15-30 degrees altitude
Vitamin D production is said to begin when the sun is above 30 degrees

### WalterNL — 2018-05-21T22:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241705>

There's an app for iPhone called SunSurveyor where you can see the location of the sun on a 3D compass. It also shows the degrees altitude of the sun for a certain time. I have not yet found certainty with regards to at what time what sunlight frequency reaches Earth's surface. As [@Rubicon](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/18225/) said, UVB has been measured to appear above 30 degrees, UVA would be available earlier. [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) is building a UVA testing device with his son to get more data: [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...o-map-out-the-ir-a-and-uv-a-transition.19869/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/spectroscope-to-map-out-the-ir-a-and-uv-a-transition.19869/) He said that UVA popped up when "it was that time when the sun goes from reddish to yellowish". The app I mentioned also shows this transition between more reddish and yellowish color.

### drezy — 2018-05-22T01:38:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241725>

Oh man! Way to hold a itochondriac's feet to the fire!

Below is an image of the prototype hardware my son and I designed together. There are some kinks to work out and I think I know what they are but I didn't want to work it out without my son. I'll start posting more on this since it seems like a point of increasing interest.

Play it safe.
1.- use one month to gradually expose all your skin and eyes to available sunlight

2.- For a whole next year,
Use all available sunlight
and also cloudy and rainy days and have max exposure

3\. Measure your vit D
if you are over 200 ng/mL
cut down on sun exposure
Hint
(newer happens, including on equator)

4\. If you do not want to follow #1, you do not have to.
You will burn or disfigure your skin.
But
that burning will not cause cancer.

Do contacts lenses and statins have something in common to cause proliferative disorders in the eye of people with this mitochondrial disease? YEP. Contact lens block oxygen via the cornea to the deep layers of the eye. How does this affect light frequencies in the eye when the eye is seeing a ton of blue light? Blue light causes massive ROS. So do contacts. What frequencies do the contact lenses block? They tend to block UVA light via neuropsin. In the UVA range, more ROS are made in the eye than ANY other and this has catabolic implications for the TCA and urea cycle in the eye and on tissues in the eye that have to use gluconeogenesis with lowered oxygen tensions. Contacts all cause a pseudohypoxia in the eye so it changes the ROS made.…….what happens in mitochondria when oxygen drops in the eye because of chronic contacts and chronic blue light exposure with very little UVA light? It causes retinal proliferation and thinning and cataracts. Blue light and cataracts and AMD are a real issue. The degradation of aromatic amino acids requires molecular oxygen to break down the aromatic rings. Your retina is loaded with aromatic amino acids. Watch my Vermont 2017 video on youtube to fill in the blanks. As dissolved O2 in a cell rises ROS becomes more problematic. What I have been wondering lately is if there is \*ever\* an ok time to wear contacts. For example, when going to a store with fluorescent lights or ... say, a college. Do they do \*anything\* beneficial -- ever? Or do they only block UV light, so it is just contacts = bad = always?

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T19:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241803>

More on the femtochemistry and attochemistry of the skin. Full spectrum sunlight including UV-A and UV-B light normally increases Vitamin D3, histamine, and sulfhydryl groups, while lowering (via photolysis) adrenaline, steroids, testosterone, estrogen, thyroid hormone, DNA, and RNA. Why is this a big deal in DNA? As our skin sloughs off DNA slowly is exposed to sunlight on the surface layers of our skin. This means the slowly degrading DNA in our skin cells acts as a molecular sunscreen. Now here is more proof of this concept I mention 4 years ago on this very page. DNA contains a building block base called thymine from normal solar exposure. When thymine is hit with ultraviolet light, a single electron jumps into a slightly higher orbit around the nucleus of a single oxygen atom. This infinitesimal quantum leap sets off a response that stretches one of the thymine's chemical bonds and snaps it back into place, creating vibrations that harmlessly dissipate the energy of incoming ultraviolet light so it doesn't cause mutations in other DNA at deeper levels. This process is ruined in diseases like diabetes, obesity, and psoriasis which are all associated with skin changes and thickness changes. [https://phys.org/news/2017-06-electron-tiny-molecular-sunscreen-response.html#jCp](https://phys.org/news/2017-06-electron-tiny-molecular-sunscreen-response.html#jCp)

### Ted — 2018-05-23T02:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-uva-am-sun.21352/post-241843>

> [Summer said:](/goto/post?id=241673)
>
> Is there an app or website that will give time of UVA in am for your location like D minder does for UVB? Trying to figure out exact times ( more specific than being light out ) for where I live of sunrise to the inflection point obUVA light from the sun to help Hashimotos. Also is it UVA or B light that is the off switch for pituitary production of all anterior pituitary hormones?

You can actually use Dminder for that. At about 18 degrees you will see the app giving you a uv of 1. That's uvA only. When it gets to 30 degrees it tells you that you can make vitamin D, that's when uvB comes in. So that window from uv 1 until vit D can be made is uvA.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-23T02:10:37+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-mercola-zach-bush-hydrating-at-the-cellular-level.21354/post-282569>

Another study shows why docs like Zach Bush have zero understanding of how sunlight sculpts the microbiome. If he did he would never try to sell any human restore.......he's sell them on vacations to the sun. This is how Vitamin D is created in the body by UVB LIGHT exposure on the skin and NOT by oral supplementation. This ultimately leads to big improvements in gut bacteria populations. Looks like another win for Kruse Longevity Center in stomping out marketers for the public.
Skin Exposure to Narrow Band Ultraviolet (UVB) Light Modulates the Human Intestinal Microbiome
Microbiol., 24 October 2019
Participants who took vitaminD supplements and use the narrowband had no improvement in microbiome diversity.
Participants who used narrowband uvb light only had significant improvements.
Bacteria from several families were enriched in the group that did not take prior vitamin D supplements.
This is the first study to show changes in intestinal microbiome in response to Narrowband-UVB skin exposure.
This evidence suggests the existence of a novel skin-gut axis that could be used to promote intestinal homeostasis and health.
[https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2019.02410/full](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2019.02410/full)

# Thread: In through the out door for the TCA and urea cycle is not simple
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:11:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241731>

The terms anaplerosis and cataplerosis describe reciprocal and correlative reactions involved in the function of the TCA cycle. The enzymatic steps in these processes have long been known, but the overall concept of a linkage between anaplerosis and cataplerosis should be underscored because the balance between these two processes controls the entry and exit of TCA cycle anions. Anaplerotic and cataplerotic reactions are involved in the ultimate disposal of all metabolic intermediates. The metabolic role of anaplerosis and cataplerosis in amino acid metabolism varies with specific organs and is dependent on the nutritional/metabolic status of the individual. During feeding, the intestine is an important site of catabolism of enterally derived amino acids, whereas in the starved state amino acid catabolism occurs primarily in the kidney, liver, and muscle.

Every tissue differs in how it uses anaplerosis and cataplerosis.

The catabolism of amino acids produces gluconeogenic or ketogenic precursors (Table [I](http://www.jbc.org/content/277/34/30409.full.html#T1)).

**Table I**

Metabolic fates of amino acids in the TCA cycle

  1.  Amino acids converted to pyruvate    Alanine, serine, glycine, threonine, cysteine, tryptophan
  2.  Amino acids converted to oxaloacetate    Aspartate, asparagine
  3.  Amino acids converted to α-ketoglutarate    Glutamate, glutamine, proline, histidine, arginine
  4.  Amino acids converted to fumarate    Phenylalanine, tyrosine
  5.  Amino acids converted to succinyl-CoA    **_Methionine_**, isoleucine, valine
  6.  Amino acids converted to acetyl-CoA    Leucine, isoleucine, lysine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan, threonine

The disposal of gluconeogenic anions in the TCA cycle employs anaplerotic and cataplerotic pathways for their terminal oxidation. The only known pathway for the terminal oxidation of leucine is through acetoacetate to acetyl-CoA and subsequent oxidation in the TCA cycle. However, other amino acids also have for their disposal alternate ketogenic pathways for terminal oxidation. Thus, the ketogenic amino acids from proteolysis can be terminally oxidized in muscle, whereas the gluconeogenic amino acids are dependent upon anaplerosis and cataplerosis for conversion to glucose in the liver and kidney before oxidation to CO2 and H2O.

The first reaction of the TCA cycle, citrate synthase, catalyzes the condensation of oxalacetate with acetyl-CoA; the oxalacetate is subsequently regenerated by the reactions of the cycle and condenses with another molecule of acetyl-CoA. However, the TCA cycle also functions in biosynthetic processes in which intermediates are removed from the cycle; this necessitates anaplerotic reactions to replenish TCA cycle intermediates to ensure its continued function. Pyruvate carboxylase, which synthesizes oxalacetate from pyruvate in the mitochondrial matrix, is the archetypical anaplerotic enzyme.

**Anaplerosis is obligatory during both gluconeogenesis and lipogenesis** when malate (gluconeogenesis) or citrate (lipogenesis) leaves the mitochondria and is further metabolized to form glucose or fatty acids, respectively.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:15:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241732>

**Cataplerosis**
If intermediates can be added to the TCA cycle, it is equally important to remove them to avoid the accumulation of anions in the mitochondrial matrix. Cataplerosis describes reactions involved in the disposal of TCA cycle intermediates. There are several cataplerotic enzymes; these include PEPCK, aspartate aminotransferase, and glutamate dehydrogenase. Each of these reactions has as a substrate a TCA cycle anion that is converted to a product that effectively removes intermediates from the cycle.

The regulation of anaplerosis and cataplerosis depends upon the metabolic and physiologic state and the specific tissue/organ involved. For example, during starvation, cataplerosis via phosphoenolpyruvate to support gluconeogenesis may be regulatory in the liver, whereas in the kidney anaplerosis via uptake of glutamine may be regulatory.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:30:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241733>

This implies the regulation of anaplerosis and cataplerosis is very dependant on deuterium kinetics in the matrix from normal or abnormal metabolic and physiologic states.

Methionine: Propionyl-CoA forms as a catabolite of methionine, threonine, and the branched-chain amino acids. _β_\-Oxidation of fatty acids with an odd number of carbon atoms yields propionyl-CoA.
The oxidation of the side chain of cholesterol also yields propionyl-CoA. Thus, propionyl-CoA is derived from the catabolism of lipids and proteins.

Propionyl-CoA is converted to succinyl-CoA, which is oxidized or converted to glucose by way of oxaloacetate and pyruvate. **Succinyl-CoA may also form _δ_\-aminolevulinate, a precursor of porphyrin biosynthesis. This is critical in oncogenesis because cancer cells need brisk ECT which means that cancer cells can use methionine to usurp oxygen using methionine to increases both angiogenesis and hemoglobin production to make sure that oxygen delivery is brisk and apoptosis stays inhibited PROVIDED VDR/D3 and/or UVA are absent to slow ECT flow. (May 2018 and QT#8)**

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241734>

cholesterol levels are usually low in cancers.......so this means methionine becomes key to the creating an oxygen demand pathway for a transformed cell.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:39:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241735>

Formation of succinyl-CoA from propionyl-CoA requires three mitochondrial enzymes and two vitamins, biotin, and cobalamin.

Methionine is converted to homocysteine in the activated methyl cycle. Cystathionine synthase converts homocysteine to cystathionine, which is then converted to propionyl-CoA. The propionyl-CoA is then converted to succinyl-CoA via methylmalonyl-CoA.

S-adenosyl methionine (SAM) is formed in the activated methyl cycle by transfer of the adenosyl group from ATP to the sulfur of methionine. The methyl group attached to the methionine sulfur transfers readily to a nitrogen, oxygen, or carbon of an acceptor.

In cancers, methionine rises and cannot be metabolized as quickly as normal because of the defective mitochondria. Cancers manifest because UVA and UVB light is used to make melatonin during daylight but methionine is an adjunct to melatonin creation especially in epithelial cells. This is why melatonin is so low in cancer patients and the high ROS in the matrix of broken mitochondria are not repaired by the action of melatonin. Carnitine limits the TCA ability to use fats and creatine limits the speed of the PPP. This points out why cancer must use glucose and glutamine for biosynthesis.

S-adenosyl homocysteine is the major donor of methyl groups in the synthesis of **_phospholipids, nucleotides, epinephrine, carnitine, melatonin, and creatine._**

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:48:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241736>

The essential amino acids include valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, tryptophan, methionine, threonine, lysine and histidine.

The amino acids arginine, methionine and phenylalanine are considered essential because their rate of synthesis is insufficient to meet the growth needs of the body. Most of synthesized arginine is cleaved to form urea. _**Methionine is required in large amounts to produce cysteine, the other sulfated amino acid if the latter amino acid is not adequately supplied in the diet**_**. Cysteine is needed for glutathione and detoxification pathways in the body. When the TCA and urea cycle kinetics are altered the sulfated amino acids cycles are altered and endogenous detox drops and things begin to show up in tissues that normally would not be there if the redox state of the mitochondria was more optimal. This also affects sulfation pathways in the blood and skin as a collateral effect.**

Similarly, phenylalanine is needed in large amounts to form tyrosine if the latter is not adequately supplied in the diet.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T06:51:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241737>

The initial two steps of the **_urea cycle occur in the mitochondria._** The first by _carbamyl phosphate synthetase_ mediates the formation of carbamoyl phosphate from NH3−, HCO3−, and ATP. The second step is the condensation of ornithine with carbamyl phosphate by the enzyme _ornithine transcarbamylase_ to form citrulline. The activity of this enzyme is directly related to dietary protein. This is why mTOR and cancer are linked.......it is only true when the urea cycle kinetics are SLOWED for some reason.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-22T07:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-241738>

_**Methionine catabolism**_ involves nine steps. When the TCA urea cycle has altered the kinetics of these 9 steps is dramatically slowed and this lowers glutathione and taurine production. Methionine can be metabolized to cysteine, the RAREST amino acid when the mitochondria have a poor redox state, but this is not true in a heteroplastic state. This means cysteine is chronically low when the TCA and urea cycle kinetics are slowed.
Cysteine makes up 66% of glutathione (GSH) by and taurine is a bile salt modifying compound that is the KEY source of the sulfur for coenzyme-A synthesis. When this is lowered no beta oxidation can occur and this also forces the cell toward glycolysis. **Cancer cells do not favor glucose.......it is the only choice they have with a faulty TCA and urea cycle because of altered kinetic flux. What causes this most? The May 2018 webinar has that answer.**

The first step is catalyzed by methionine adenosyl transferase which tranfers the adenosyl group of ATP to the sulfur of methionine to form S-adenosylmethionine (SAM). _SAM methylase_ transfers the activated methyl group to an acceptor to form S-adenosylhomocysteine which is hydrolyzed by _adenosylhomocysteinase_ to form homocysteine. _Cystathionine β-synthase_ catalyzes the condensation of a serine residue with homocysteine to form cystathionine. _Cystathioniine γ-lyase_ cleaves cystathionine into cysteine and α-ketobutyrate, the latter being converted into propionyl CoA by _an α-ketobutyrate dehydrogenase._

### Jack Kruse — 2025-09-17T03:58:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-359631>

Building on the evolving narrative in this thread around uric acid, NO inhibition, TCA cycle dynamics, deuterium kinetics, and now incorporating urea cycle intricacies, methionine metabolism, melanin's multifaceted roles, and light's overlooked influence, here's a decentralized reasoned surmise of the broader implications.

This synthesis highlights an integrated "photo-metabolic-quantum axis" where environmental light cues orchestrate mitochondrial efficiency, redox balance, and disease susceptibility through interconnected biochemical and biophysical mechanisms.

Light as a Master Regulator of Nitrogen Cycles and Redox

The urea cycle's mitochondrial initiation (CPS1 forming carbamoyl phosphate from NH₃, HCO₃⁻, and ATP; OTC condensing ornithine to citrulline) ties directly to protein intake and mTOR signaling, but my emphasis on kinetic slowdowns reveals light's underappreciated role. Elevated urea or uric acid often signals low redox, potentially from insufficient sunlight, which disrupts cycle kinetics. For instance, in symbiotic systems like giant clams, light enhances urea absorption and upregulates transporters, suggesting photosynthetic or NO-mediated boosts to nitrogen handling.

In humans, chronic artificial light (ALAN) or blue-dominant exposure could mimic hypoxia-like states, slowing the urea cycle via oxidative stress or circadian misalignment, leading to ammonia buildup and encephalopathy risks.

This intersects with uric acid's NO inhibition: Low sunlight reduces UVA-driven NO, but if cycle kinetics falter (e.g., from deuterium burden or melanopsin damage), uric acid accumulates, further dampening NO and TCA flux, creating a low-redox vicious cycle.

Implication: Sunlight (especially AM red/UVA) acts as a "kinetic accelerator" for urea/uric acid disposal, optimizing anaplerosis/cataplerosis in nitrogen metabolism. Absent this, mTOR hyperactivation, which is tied to high protein sans light cues, fuels cancer by evading DNA damage checkpoints, not just via nutrient sensing but through impaired photorepair (e.g., UV-induced lesions). Blue toxicity (melanopsin destruction releasing vitamin A, lowering D3) exacerbates this, as D3/VDR signaling might normally temper mTOR in sunlit contexts.

Therapeutic: Prioritizing sunrise-to-10 AM exposure and ALAN avoidance could restore cycle speed, enhancing detox and reducing cancer risk via improved mTOR-regulated repair.

Methionine's Sulfur Bridge: From Methylation to Propionyl-CoA in Altered States

The methionine cascade (MAT → SAM → SAH → homocysteine → cystathionine → cysteine → propionyl-CoA) underscores its role in methylation (e.g., for melatonin, carnitine) and TCA entry via succinyl-CoA. In slowed urea cycles, methionine accumulates, diverting to oncogenic paths: Boosting heme/porphyrin for O₂ demand, angiogenesis, and apoptosis inhibition, especially sans UVA/VDR.

This ties to sulfation drops: Reduced cysteine/glutathione impairs detox, allowing toxins to persist in low-redox tissues.

Implication: Methionine's "oxygen demand pathway" in cancer exploits urea slowdowns, but light intervenes, UVA/UVB aids melatonin synthesis (tryptophan adjunct to methionine in epithelia), repairing mtDNA and restoring kinetics. Circadian-appropriate seafood (high in low-D fats, sulfated AAs) can counter some of this, while DDW might enhance methionine metabolism by lightening D burden, indirectly boosting detox.

In essence, methionine links nitrogen cycles to quantum effects, where light-deprived states amplify its oncogenic potential.

Melanin-Mitochondria Symbiosis: Paramagnetism, UPE, and Deuterium Dynamics

Melanin's proximity to mitochondria positions it as a ROS/RNS scavenger and UPE modulator, transforming spectral emissions for cellular signaling. Its paramagnetism—hydration-dependent, shifting hydroxyquinone <--> semiquinone equilibria—makes it sensitive to ATPase's magnetic fields from Fo rotation, potentially tuning proton gradients.

DDW, with lower D, could amplify this by enhancing proton mobility (e.g., Zundel ions), stabilizing radicals, and boosting EPR signals—uncharted in biophysics, but plausible given DDW's metabolic tweaks.

Implication: Melanin acts as a "quantum sensor" bridging light to mito function: Absorbing/scattering UPE from mtDNA/ROS, it modulates signals for non-chemical mito communication, while paramagnetism aligns with ATPase fields to optimize D discrimination in matrix water. In low-sun states, dehydration or D-enrichment weakens this, slowing TCA/urea, elevating uric acid, and promoting cancer (low melatonin, unchecked ROS).

This explains why melanin-rich tissues (skin, eyes, brain) are light-sensitive redox hubs. Emerging therapies: DDW + AM sunlight could "hydrate" melanin optimally, enhancing UPE-guided repair and countering blue-induced damage.

Unified Health/Disease Framework and Practical Advice

Overall, this implies a holistic paradigm: Uric acid/urea elevations are symptoms of "light deficiency syndrome," where absent solar cues (UVA/red) disrupt NO-TCA-urea-methionine-melanin loops, amplifying deuterium's kinetic drag, mTOR dysregulation, and oncogenic shifts.

Cancer emerges as a "de-synchronized mitochondrial adaptation," hijacking slowed cycles for survival. Wallace/McManus IMM alignments falter here, as paramagnetic melanin fails to "tune" under ALAN.

Practical: Align with my advice, sunrise AM exposure rebuilds melanopsin/D3, accelerating cycles; latitude-local seafood provides low-D substrates; ALAN avoidance preserves UPE/melatonin. Add DDW for potential melanin/TCA boost, and monitor uric acid as a sunlight/redox biomarker. Validation could involve EPR studies on DDW-hydrated melanin in UVA-exposed models, bridging the current biophysics gaps.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-18T01:49:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-through-the-out-door-for-the-tca-and-urea-cycle-is-not-simple.21360/post-361166>

> [cL34rC0mm said:](/goto/post?id=353236)
>
> Urea (uric acid as well) elevated can indicate a LACK of sunlight exposure and system wide LOW redox. You also can be blue toxic reflecting melanopsin destruction due to liberation of Vitamin A (from blue light exposure) which also indicates a LOW Vitamin D3 status. Best advice is to see sunrise each morning and get maximum AM sunlight exposure (sunrise to 10am) for red light exposure to full body. Eat circadian appropriate (latitude oriented local foods only) and heaps of seafood. And ALWAYS AVOID any light after sunset (A.L.A.N.= Artificial Light At Night).

In 1971, scientists published a study that showed incessant fluorescent light under a green filter damaged the retina in rats. They concluded "The normal diurnal cycle of light and dark seems to be the essential factor in controlling visual cell viability and susceptibility." Everything in the body only works in a coupled cycle and they are all linked to the light at some level. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

For decades we were told the visual cycle runs in darkness, that the eye rebuilds its pigment only at night while the brain’s clock sleeps. But hiding inside the retinal pigment epithelium is RGR-opsin. This is an enzyme activated by light that breaks the rule completely.

When visible light (especially green/amber wavelengths) hits it, RGR-opsin takes the “used up” vitamin-A molecules (all-trans-retinal) and flips them back to the light-sensitive 11-cis form just like an optical switch.

What most people forget is that Vitamin A is the molecule that starts vision in the first place, so daylight literally re-creates the chemistry. Photonics preceeds electricity and electricity always precedes chemisty.

Every visible photon is both information and fuel, where purple ignites, blue tunes, green stabilizes, yellow/amber synchronize times, and red light can heal things via photorepair. The balance of these colors decides how your retina regenerates, how dopamine flows, how your circadian chemistry operates in periodicity. Periodicity is a function of entropy. It is a flow meter for entropy and that is what the molecular clocks were built to sense.

Under narrow LED spectra, the cycle limps along, with half the wavelength you get half the repair. Under sunlight, it’s a whole different story, the system launches and sustains itself so health becomes the slowest form of death our cells create.

Your eyes are not just cameras, they’re living solar circuits, turning light into life thousands of times per second. They are clocks. The next time someone says “get more sunlight,” remember, they’re not talking about improving your mood. They’re talking about vision biochemistry that gives you time back you lost when you did not follow Nature's rules.

### jnacrelli92 — 2018-05-22T18:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ssri-proctracted-withdrawl.21361/post-241790>

Alright, so a brief history about myself. I suffered an adverse reaction to an SSRI that a doctor put me on 6 months after a seizure after an outdoor music festival I experienced in 2015. I have posted a video of someone with the same illness as I below. I was struck with a littony of symptoms (Depersonalization, vertigo, electrical sensitivity, nausea, diarhea, brain zaps, tinnitus, facial spasms, inablity to scroll on a computer, and the list goes on and on.) I have seen a great deal of success with functional neurology. My doc had me buy blue light blockers, which have helped significantly with my screen sensitivity. I work for an HR company, and had to leave work for a year because my computer sensitivities were so severe.
So what's left? I still have involintary eye movements, migranes...due to the eye movements. Sensitivity to prolonged scrolling on the computer due to an accomodation spasm in my eye. My eyes will constantly roll in the back of my head. Sensitivities to exercise. Sensitivites to Alcohol, caffeine, and an intolerance to supplements. My doc is assuming I have MTHFR issues due to a liver mutation in gene CYPD2d6. Lack of energy: After a day on the computer I am completely done, and can not do anything social. Lack of motivation to leave my house because of my inability to produce energy.

Other than Functional Neurology, your methods have been the only thing that has showed me any benefit. My doc educating me about mitochondria was what made me find your podcasts. Thank you for your services.

### jnacrelli92 — 2018-05-22T18:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bx-protocol.21362/post-241794>

Has Anyone heard of the BX Protocol? It is referred to as a Photo Catalytic Oxidizer, and is meant to bind to stealth pathogens attacking people's mitochondria, and therefore their ability to produce energy. I have seen a lot of testimonials online for cancer and Lyme Disease especially. The founder of the company Dewayne Smith seems very knowledgeable, and draws a lot of parallels to me with Jack. I wanted to see if anyone personally has used this system, or knows whether or not it is a scam.

I'd be interested in what Ashbeck is. Plastic bottle issues apart, 5L bottle costs £1.10. The blurb "This pure, refreshing Ashbeck Natural Mineral Water is sourced from the rolling hills of Eden Valley, in a protected Special Area of Conservation. The water flows deep below ground through rocks dating back millions of years. These ancient rocks naturally filter each drop of Ashbeck water and enrich it with minerals to give a clear, refreshing taste."

The figure I've seen for Blackford in Scotland is -56 promille, but if Highland Spring is 148.2, and Jack mentioned a 1 liter 2 hour freeze result of 139.6 then that's a pretty good drop. Ashbeck in Cumbria should have less than 150ppm, but assuming its 150 ppm, a first freeze might bring it down to less than even Hildon? I'm looking for a way to deuterium deplete when the weather forces a move to my winter location and I lose well water access, but at the moment I'm first freezing well water (on a hill, organic land been in the family 90 years). In the current heat I'm making 4 litres a day per person (2 adults) for all drinking and cooking purposes. Even with the wastage from the freeze, Ashbeck at £1.10 per 5 litres would be financially doable. I was making sure I drank 1st freeze water for cold water drinking, but it suddenly occurred to me that any other 150 ppm sources from cooking, hot drinks, ice cubes etc could affect or slow my results

### Billybats — 2018-05-25T14:29:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-water-results.21367/post-242032>

> [Billybats said:](/goto/post?id=241917)
>
> Is ionized water or high ph water the same as deuterium depleting water

I just found this and it answered my question about my above question.. bottom line is if what they wrote is true DO NOT drink alkalized ionized water. They say it is loaded with deuterium.

I will post the one part about alkalized ionized water together, but they explain plain alkalized water too.

Are these true facts?

[http://www.dancingwithwater.com/articles/alkaline-water-not/](http://www.dancingwithwater.com/articles/alkaline-water-not/)

**Alkaline ionized water has an increased concentration of deuterium**
Deuterium, which is an isotope of hydrogen, (with a neutron and a proton in the atom’s nucleus) has double the mass of the hydrogen atom, and while it is present in all water to a small degree, even small amounts can disturb cell function and increase errors during DNA replication. Ionization of water increases the amount of deuterium in the resulting water because deuterium bonds are stronger than hydrogen bonds and when water is ionized (breaking up water molecules), the deuterium concentration in the resulting water increases. Read more about the positive effects of[reducing the deuterium](http://www.dancingwithwater.com/deuterium-depleted-water-what-is-it/) in water.

While alkaline ionized water has been in use in Japan for over 50 years, its use (until recently) has been restricted to clinical settings where the water was used in controlled amounts for short periods of time. There are no long-term studies to identify potential problems with either the extended or the exclusive use of ionized water. In the absence of controlled human studies, rats (with a shorter life span) have been the subjects of investigation. Three separate studies have shown that long-term consumption of alkaline ionized water may cause cell death (necrosis), fibrosis, and other complications in the heart muscle in rats. [Read abstract #1](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9524951) [Read abstract #2](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9198011) [Read abstract#3](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9922944) Dr. Hidemitsu Hayashi, a cardiac surgeon and Director of the Water Institute in Japan, was one of the scientists who conducted early research on alkaline ionized water. His work determined that the benefits of alkaline ionized water were due to hydrogen in the water. Ultimately, he abandoned the use of ionized water in favor of other methods to produce [hydrogen-rich water](http://www.dancingwithwater.com/articles/alkaline-water-not/#).

Nature is about balance—not extremes. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, taking strong “Yang” herbs and other substances (like alkaline ionized water) for too long, overheats and burns the body out. Ultimately, it contributes to aging. Alkaline ionized water can be therapeutic for short periods of time but it should not be consumed on a regular basis. It may contribute to arterial plaque, calcification of joints, and cardiovascular problems. In the authors’ opinion, consumption of this water for an extended period of time, is an invitation for trouble.

**NOTE**: Since the digestive process begins in the stomach—an extremely acidic environment—alkaline water (with pH over 8.5)—even if it has bicarbonates— should **_not_** be taken with meals. You might be surprised to discover how many people who drink alkaline ionized water end up with digestive trouble—and they have no idea it was caused by the water.

Yet another possible explanation for why observations don’t match predictions is that humans aren’t very observant about the **mosaic** of light around us and what it is capable of.

[https://metro.co.uk/2018/05/14/sale...rain-tumour-years-glued-phone-7543866/#item-0](https://metro.co.uk/2018/05/14/salesman-sues-nokia-causing-brain-tumour-years-glued-phone-7543866/#item-0)

### Jenelle — 2018-05-23T14:24:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-begins-in-britain.21368/post-241875>

For many years now -- long before I found this site -- I haven't liked being on the phone.

My sister would call and want to talk for hours and I hated how I felt when I got off the phone -- like it derailed my whole day. I had to put a stop to it for my own mental health.

Way back then, I thought it was just me absorbing her drama. I would tell people it was "exhausting".

I cannot imagine having a phone glued to my head for 5 hours -- days -- let alone YEARS.

I think people are just so jacked up all the time that they don't even make the connection. I have intuitively avoided phone calls whenever I can ~ and my brain is more peaceful because of it.

### Phosphene — 2018-05-23T20:46:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-begins-in-britain.21368/post-241901>

I never even set up the voicemail on my phone. Only had a few complaints over the years about not being reachable. Those who need to reach me have learned that texting or email is best, and not to expect an answer right away.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-23T21:13:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-begins-in-britain.21368/post-241905>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=241901)
>
> I never even set up the voicemail on my phone. Only had a few complaints over the years about not being reachable. Those who need to reach me have learned that texting or email is best, and not to expect an answer right away.

Lol... I didn't know my voicemail had been full for (at least) 6 months until someone told me recently. It wasn't important stuff I had saved ~ I just hadn't actively deleted old ones.

My policy is that if it's important, they will leave me a message or send me a text. I had no idea that I wasn't even giving people the voicemail option.

> Huge deal. You need to monitor your dopamine network.
>
> Trim the vampires.

That one gets personal and painful with me.

I honestly don't think I get an "A" on this one yet, but I'm getting closer.

I'll help my mom with falls or dangerous broken glass and follow up with a water refresh and some quick dishwashing, but I've trained her that I do not engage in conversations inside of a dark living room or with the damned TV on. I made it clear that until she joins me in her pool 3x/week I will not let my son go over.

It's that threshold right at the cusp of having to become an asshole that is the most difficult. I may have to become an asshole, but I'm a quantized asshole.

The upside:
She sits in her bedroom doorway that leads outside and gets hit with morning light filtered through a tree for 30-45 minutes each morning
I help her into the pool 3x/week now so she gets cool, sunlight, and more movement
While in the pool we have long conversations with the past and my son's future
"After time outside I feel like I'm in a way better mood" her words
She eats bone broths and seafood for a majority of her meals these days

I still double up n my cold + sun sessions on these days as much as I can.

I have a Kiniki and am a bit disappointed with the tan lines. Hard to avoid with the straps and seams. It’s obvious that SOME tanning occurs through the fabric, but not a whole lot.

My yard is fairly private but not where the best morning sun comes through the trees. Still working on a solution that doesn’t involve a strange and inconvenient corral of tangled lawn furniture and sheets. I personally am getting to the point of not caring if anyone sees, but my family would be mortified. (I know, I know, I shouldn’t care at all...)

I also find the Kiniki fabric kind of scratchy—like a bra you just want to ditch halfway through the day. Not that I wouldn’t prefer that entirely, but still... Hopefully there will be better options out there. Might try Cool Tan.

# Thread: 3,500 mile EMF road-trip
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 43
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ObstreperousPolarBear — 2018-05-24T20:56:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-241992>

Dear everyone, I just completed a 3,500 mile road trip with my trusty AM-310 RF meter and thought you would all be interested in the results.

Why did I take this road trip? Because I wasn't sure where I wanted to found a clinic/get to optimal and I was, at the time, living in the (to quote Jack) "EMF Chernobyl" of Denver, Colorado. I couldn't live there (I realized how it was affecting my performance and health) and I couldn't very well practice medicine in a place where my number 1 recommendation would be "move." Not exactly a sustainable business model. When Jack told a friend of mine and I we needed to both move, I thought he was indulging in hyperbole. I know now that he was entirely correct. Denver = unhackable. After reading enough of his work, I realized that he was entirely correct in choosing the Gulf South as the optimal location (at least for an American) to live.

I drove from Denver through Kansas, OK, TX, LA, MS, AL, and Pensacola, Florida, then up the east coast to Savannah and Charleston, then back down to Tampa, Florida.

What did I find?
I didn't take exacting measurements, because I was (for the most part) looking for general trends. Here is a brief summary. If you have specific questions, feel free to ask. If this duplicates any findings or threads already on the forum, please point me in the right direction - I want to see what other people have found elsewhere.
1\. Highways are bad because of everyone else's devices and the towers are focused on these thoroughfares. I rarely, if ever, saw low reading while actually on the road.
2\. Even just a few minutes off the highway in a rural area (i.e. only a few houses or structures within sight) you start to find areas with undetectable levels (<0.02 V/m).
3\. In your average suburb, you will rarely find dead zones. Even in parks and vacant lots, I had trouble finding dead zones because everyone around you has a router that, these days, even has a built in "hot spot."
4\. Elevation is bad because they put up more towers.
5\. Trees are your friends.
6\. Flat areas can be an advantage IF it means there are fewer towers and more trees. Otherwise (like on the great plains), it just means the signal carries forever.
7\. Water is your friend. No one wants to live in a swamp, and wetlands are now protected by federal law, so development around them (or in them) is limited. This does present problems in the sense that it forces developers to build up/reach higher population densities (this was noticeable in a place like Charleston, which is full of rivers and swampland). Large lakes and other water features (like Pontchatrain) create massive areas without any EMF generation, essentially acting as giant buffers for you. This is why New Orleans is still a major city, but the levels are not so high. The sheer area of water and swampland limits population densities.
8\. Once you hit big cities, even if it's just the outlying strip mall, it becomes difficult to find dead zones. Levels range from low (0.02 v/m) to on the higher-end (~1 V/m) or, in places where radio towers, cell arrays, and habitations collide, way higher.
9\. Once you pass the outer suburbs and actually get into a city, you're in a soup of EMF. Readings below 0.5 V/m or 500 microwatts/m(squared) become rare. Everywhere, it is pulsating.
10\. Looking at maps for population density, I found that basically once population densities rise past 500/sq mile, it becomes hard to find dead zones. Below that, and you can typically find one around the next corner.
11\. What this basically boils down to is that pristine environments (or at least the best you can get without more sensitive measuring equipment) are 1) on the very outskirts of town (if you can see other houses, make sure it's not too many) or 2) in the middle of nowhere. The problem with number 2) being that, if you are in a place like Denver, the EMF will carry about 30 to 50 miles out from town. Making a commute quite impractical.

Breakdown by city:
1\. New Orleans - I actually didn't go into the city, because I can always just ask Jack what it's like there. Where I was, in Madisonville, LA (north of Pontchatrain), levels were extremely low. Low population density + water + trees = low levels. Jack can weigh in on how the city of New Orleans itself is.
2\. Gulfport and Biloxi, MS - low population densities along the coast here (the area is less developed than that further east) + small cities (Gulfport and Biloxi are rather small) lead to low levels.
3\. Pensacola - not so bad, but you get higher readings around the port and the downtown area, equivalent with what I found in larger cities. Just a few minutes in the right direction, though, and the levels drop off rapidly, giving you plenty of options.
4\. East of Pensacola (Navarre beach, Highway 30A, Miramar Beach, Santa Rosa Beach) - nice, low levels around just about every corner off of the highway. Beautiful place to live. Still some affordable real estate in the area.
5\. Tallahassee - similar story as Pensacola
6\. Savannah - relatively low levels in most neighborhoods, but the ubiquity of wifi routers and wireless devices makes it hard to find a place with low levels of EMF within the city itself. Plenty of rural land outside the city is, I would imagine, quite low.
7\. Charleston - way more densely populated than I had anticipated. If you're willing to live a good 30 minutes outside the city (more lik 45 minutes or an hour with traffic), you can find a nice spot. There is land for sale in the area that will hopefully remain rural for some time (especially if you choose a spot next to swamps, rivers, or national forest, of which there is quite a lot).
8\. Tampa - levels were about half what they were in Denver, but still not great. Even at the beach, hundreds of yards from anything, levels are higher than I would like (think 1 microwatt/meter to 500 microwatts with a strong pulse).

Where did I decide to go?

Somewhere near Destin. Likely a little ways east, toward Panama City. The long term goal is to buy a nice piece of property and develop it into a clinic. For now, you can find me working in the hospitals at Fort Walton and Panama City Beach (where I had contracts already in the works before I started the trip).

Ask me anything you want. I'll be driving around in the area as I settle in and will update things here as I learn more.

Cheers,
Lee
