### Annemarie Heise — 2018-05-25T05:31:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-242014>

> [Leland Stillman said:](/goto/post?id=241992)
>
> Dear everyone, I just completed a 3,500 mile road trip with my trusty AM-310 RF meter and thought you would all be interested in the results.
>
> Why did I take this road trip? Because I wasn't sure where I wanted to found a clinic/get to optimal and I was, at the time, living in the (to quote Jack) "EMF Chernobyl" of Denver, Colorado. I couldn't live there (I realized how it was affecting my performance and health) and I couldn't very well practice medicine in a place where my number 1 recommendation would be "move." Not exactly a sustainable business model. When Jack told a friend of mine and I we needed to both move, I thought he was indulging in hyperbole. I know now that he was entirely correct. Denver = unhackable. After reading enough of his work, I realized that he was entirely correct in choosing the Gulf South as the optimal location (at least for an American) to live.
>
> I drove from Denver through Kansas, OK, TX, LA, MS, AL, and Pensacola, Florida, then up the east coast to Savannah and Charleston, then back down to Tampa, Florida.
>
> What did I find?
> I didn't take exacting measurements, because I was (for the most part) looking for general trends. Here is a brief summary. If you have specific questions, feel free to ask. If this duplicates any findings or threads already on the forum, please point me in the right direction - I want to see what other people have found elsewhere.
> 1\. Highways are bad because of everyone else's devices and the towers are focused on these thoroughfares. I rarely, if ever, saw low reading while actually on the road.
> 2\. Even just a few minutes off the highway in a rural area (i.e. only a few houses or structures within sight) you start to find areas with undetectable levels (<0.02 V/m).
> 3\. In your average suburb, you will rarely find dead zones. Even in parks and vacant lots, I had trouble finding dead zones because everyone around you has a router that, these days, even has a built in "hot spot."
> 4\. Elevation is bad because they put up more towers.
> 5\. Trees are your friends.
> 6\. Flat areas can be an advantage IF it means there are fewer towers and more trees. Otherwise (like on the great plains), it just means the signal carries forever.
> 7\. Water is your friend. No one wants to live in a swamp, and wetlands are now protected by federal law, so development around them (or in them) is limited. This does present problems in the sense that it forces developers to build up/reach higher population densities (this was noticeable in a place like Charleston, which is full of rivers and swampland). Large lakes and other water features (like Pontchatrain) create massive areas without any EMF generation, essentially acting as giant buffers for you. This is why New Orleans is still a major city, but the levels are not so high. The sheer area of water and swampland limits population densities.
> 8\. Once you hit big cities, even if it's just the outlying strip mall, it becomes difficult to find dead zones. Levels range from low (0.02 v/m) to on the higher-end (~1 V/m) or, in places where radio towers, cell arrays, and habitations collide, way higher.
> 9\. Once you pass the outer suburbs and actually get into a city, you're in a soup of EMF. Readings below 0.5 V/m or 500 microwatts/m(squared) become rare. Everywhere, it is pulsating.
> 10\. Looking at maps for population density, I found that basically once population densities rise past 500/sq mile, it becomes hard to find dead zones. Below that, and you can typically find one around the next corner.
> 11\. What this basically boils down to is that pristine environments (or at least the best you can get without more sensitive measuring equipment) are 1) on the very outskirts of town (if you can see other houses, make sure it's not too many) or 2) in the middle of nowhere. The problem with number 2) being that, if you are in a place like Denver, the EMF will carry about 30 to 50 miles out from town. Making a commute quite impractical.
>
> Breakdown by city:
> 1\. New Orleans - I actually didn't go into the city, because I can always just ask Jack what it's like there. Where I was, in Madisonville, LA (north of Pontchatrain), levels were extremely low. Low population density + water + trees = low levels. Jack can weigh in on how the city of New Orleans itself is.
> 2\. Gulfport and Biloxi, MS - low population densities along the coast here (the area is less developed than that further east) + small cities (Gulfport and Biloxi are rather small) lead to low levels.
> 3\. Pensacola - not so bad, but you get higher readings around the port and the downtown area, equivalent with what I found in larger cities. Just a few minutes in the right direction, though, and the levels drop off rapidly, giving you plenty of options.
> 4\. East of Pensacola (Navarre beach, Highway 30A, Miramar Beach, Santa Rosa Beach) - nice, low levels around just about every corner off of the highway. Beautiful place to live. Still some affordable real estate in the area.
> 5\. Tallahassee - similar story as Pensacola
> 6\. Savannah - relatively low levels in most neighborhoods, but the ubiquity of wifi routers and wireless devices makes it hard to find a place with low levels of EMF within the city itself. Plenty of rural land outside the city is, I would imagine, quite low.
> 7\. Charleston - way more densely populated than I had anticipated. If you're willing to live a good 30 minutes outside the city (more lik 45 minutes or an hour with traffic), you can find a nice spot. There is land for sale in the area that will hopefully remain rural for some time (especially if you choose a spot next to swamps, rivers, or national forest, of which there is quite a lot).
> 8\. Tampa - levels were about half what they were in Denver, but still not great. Even at the beach, hundreds of yards from anything, levels are higher than I would like (think 1 microwatt/meter to 500 microwatts with a strong pulse).
>
> Where did I decide to go?
>
> Somewhere near Destin. Likely a little ways east, toward Panama City. The long term goal is to buy a nice piece of property and develop it into a clinic. For now, you can find me working in the hospitals at Fort Walton and Panama City Beach (where I had contracts already in the works before I started the trip).
>
> Ask me anything you want. I'll be driving around in the area as I settle in and will update things here as I learn more.
>
> Cheers,
> Lee

After much driving I decided to move to Naples Florida

### Tristan Summerfield — 2018-06-26T03:37:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-244324>

Thanks for sharing Leland. My wife and I are in the process of moving from Santa Barbara to a small coastal town in Florida and what you shared is very valuable information. East of Pensacola sounds nice. Annemarie, what has your experience been like in Naples thus far? Seems like South of Naples could be a great spot because of low population density. Jack, would you mind giving a list of your recommended spots or areas to live in Florida? Thanks everyone! Such an amazing community here.

### ObstreperousPolarBear — 2018-06-26T04:31:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-244327>

> [Tristan Summerfield said:](/goto/post?id=244324)
>
> Thanks for sharing Leland. My wife and I are in the process of moving from Santa Barbara to a small coastal town in Florida and what you shared is very valuable information. East of Pensacola sounds nice. Annemarie, what has your experience been like in Naples thus far? Seems like South of Naples could be a great spot because of low population density. Jack, would you mind giving a list of your recommended spots or areas to live in Florida? Thanks everyone! Such an amazing community here.

Jack's favorite is Navarre beach.

Understand a few things.

1\. Things change. The Panhandle is still the least settled part of Florida. It is amazing. I love it. However, it is rapidly developing. Population densities are rising. And land is expensive near the beach. Either buy inland and visit the beach (my preference) or carefully pick your site to be one that won't get overwhelmed by population density. Be wary of 1) shipping lanes and ports and 2) military bases (of which there are many).
2\. The above applies to everywhere in Florida that isn't a major city - Jacksonville, Miami, Tampa, etc.
3\. Buy equipment.
4\. Test your environment.

Plenty of opportunities economically, so you should have no problem finding a good place.

> 7\. Charleston - way more densely populated than I had anticipated. If you're willing to live a good 30 minutes outside the city (more lik 45 minutes or an hour with traffic), you can find a nice spot. There is land for sale in the area that will hopefully remain rural for some time (especially if you choose a spot next to swamps, rivers, or national forest, of which there is quite a lot).
> 8\. Tampa - levels were about half what they were in Denver, but still not great. Even at the beach, hundreds of yards from anything, levels are higher than I would like (think 1 microwatt/meter to 500 microwatts with a strong pulse).
>
> Where did I decide to go?
>
> Somewhere near Destin. Likely a little ways east, toward Panama City. The long term goal is to buy a nice piece of property and develop it into a clinic. For now, you can find me working in the hospitals at Fort Walton and Panama City Beach (where I had contracts already in the works before I started the trip).
>
> Ask me anything you want. I'll be driving around in the area as I settle in and will update things here as I learn more.
>
> Cheers,
> Lee

Thanks so much for sharing!

I'm curious what you type of vehicle you drove in??

### Inger — 2018-07-19T10:30:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-246173>

I had an Huyndai i30, 2008. Diesel, automatic. It had very little radiation! I loved that car! It was really a good and easy one. Now, as it had driven over 200 000 km I changed it into an Opel Vectra, Diesel, automatic, 2003. It has also quite little radiation, when I have the lights off. When I have them on, it has more. Thankfully you are allowed to drive lights off at daytime in Germany . But RF is great, only magnetic is elevated then. But the Huynday was even better, and great motor too, not much to repair and not expensive

I have a good friend that drives a Porsche Jeep... and we recently measured his car.. it was horrible!!! All was on max.. magnetic.. RF... He has an old Porsche cabriolet too and he now borrows my meters to measure if it has less but I am very sure, it has no tech at all . So he said he has to drive his old Porsche more..

### drezy — 2018-07-19T16:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-246194>

[@Gina R](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21555/)

Inger has your answer to:

> Anyone know how to (quickly) determine with regards to emf, etc, (and reliability) what the best vehicle is?

I think many people can tell you which vehicles to avoid, but to truly know which ones are good you have to measure them. I don't see any way around that.

Caveat: I'm a guy that takes meters to Home Depot to test out in store before purchases. Once you give up trying to look sane to the masses it gets easy.

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-07-21T19:29:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/3-500-mile-emf-road-trip.21381/post-246359>

> [Leland Stillman said:](/goto/post?id=241992)
>
> Dear everyone, I just completed a 3,500 mile road trip with my trusty AM-310 RF meter and thought you would all be interested in the results.
>
> Why did I take this road trip? Because I wasn't sure where I wanted to found a clinic/get to optimal and I was, at the time, living in the (to quote Jack) "EMF Chernobyl" of Denver, Colorado. I couldn't live there (I realized how it was affecting my performance and health) and I couldn't very well practice medicine in a place where my number 1 recommendation would be "move." Not exactly a sustainable business model. When Jack told a friend of mine and I we needed to both move, I thought he was indulging in hyperbole. I know now that he was entirely correct. Denver = unhackable. After reading enough of his work, I realized that he was entirely correct in choosing the Gulf South as the optimal location (at least for an American) to live.
>
> I drove from Denver through Kansas, OK, TX, LA, MS, AL, and Pensacola, Florida, then up the east coast to Savannah and Charleston, then back down to Tampa, Florida.
>
> What did I find?
> I didn't take exacting measurements, because I was (for the most part) looking for general trends. Here is a brief summary. If you have specific questions, feel free to ask. If this duplicates any findings or threads already on the forum, please point me in the right direction - I want to see what other people have found elsewhere.
> 1\. Highways are bad because of everyone else's devices and the towers are focused on these thoroughfares. I rarely, if ever, saw low reading while actually on the road.
> 2\. Even just a few minutes off the highway in a rural area (i.e. only a few houses or structures within sight) you start to find areas with undetectable levels (<0.02 V/m).
> 3\. In your average suburb, you will rarely find dead zones. Even in parks and vacant lots, I had trouble finding dead zones because everyone around you has a router that, these days, even has a built in "hot spot."
> 4\. Elevation is bad because they put up more towers.
> 5\. Trees are your friends.
> 6\. Flat areas can be an advantage IF it means there are fewer towers and more trees. Otherwise (like on the great plains), it just means the signal carries forever.
> 7\. Water is your friend. No one wants to live in a swamp, and wetlands are now protected by federal law, so development around them (or in them) is limited. This does present problems in the sense that it forces developers to build up/reach higher population densities (this was noticeable in a place like Charleston, which is full of rivers and swampland). Large lakes and other water features (like Pontchatrain) create massive areas without any EMF generation, essentially acting as giant buffers for you. This is why New Orleans is still a major city, but the levels are not so high. The sheer area of water and swampland limits population densities.
> 8\. Once you hit big cities, even if it's just the outlying strip mall, it becomes difficult to find dead zones. Levels range from low (0.02 v/m) to on the higher-end (~1 V/m) or, in places where radio towers, cell arrays, and habitations collide, way higher.
> 9\. Once you pass the outer suburbs and actually get into a city, you're in a soup of EMF. Readings below 0.5 V/m or 500 microwatts/m(squared) become rare. Everywhere, it is pulsating.
> 10\. Looking at maps for population density, I found that basically once population densities rise past 500/sq mile, it becomes hard to find dead zones. Below that, and you can typically find one around the next corner.
> 11\. What this basically boils down to is that pristine environments (or at least the best you can get without more sensitive measuring equipment) are 1) on the very outskirts of town (if you can see other houses, make sure it's not too many) or 2) in the middle of nowhere. The problem with number 2) being that, if you are in a place like Denver, the EMF will carry about 30 to 50 miles out from town. Making a commute quite impractical.
>
> Breakdown by city:
> 1\. New Orleans - I actually didn't go into the city, because I can always just ask Jack what it's like there. Where I was, in Madisonville, LA (north of Pontchatrain), levels were extremely low. Low population density + water + trees = low levels. Jack can weigh in on how the city of New Orleans itself is.
> 2\. Gulfport and Biloxi, MS - low population densities along the coast here (the area is less developed than that further east) + small cities (Gulfport and Biloxi are rather small) lead to low levels.
> 3\. Pensacola - not so bad, but you get higher readings around the port and the downtown area, equivalent with what I found in larger cities. Just a few minutes in the right direction, though, and the levels drop off rapidly, giving you plenty of options.
> 4\. East of Pensacola (Navarre beach, Highway 30A, Miramar Beach, Santa Rosa Beach) - nice, low levels around just about every corner off of the highway. Beautiful place to live. Still some affordable real estate in the area.
> 5\. Tallahassee - similar story as Pensacola
> 6\. Savannah - relatively low levels in most neighborhoods, but the ubiquity of wifi routers and wireless devices makes it hard to find a place with low levels of EMF within the city itself. Plenty of rural land outside the city is, I would imagine, quite low.
> 7\. Charleston - way more densely populated than I had anticipated. If you're willing to live a good 30 minutes outside the city (more lik 45 minutes or an hour with traffic), you can find a nice spot. There is land for sale in the area that will hopefully remain rural for some time (especially if you choose a spot next to swamps, rivers, or national forest, of which there is quite a lot).
> 8\. Tampa - levels were about half what they were in Denver, but still not great. Even at the beach, hundreds of yards from anything, levels are higher than I would like (think 1 microwatt/meter to 500 microwatts with a strong pulse).
>
> Where did I decide to go?
>
> Somewhere near Destin. Likely a little ways east, toward Panama City. The long term goal is to buy a nice piece of property and develop it into a clinic. For now, you can find me working in the hospitals at Fort Walton and Panama City Beach (where I had contracts already in the works before I started the trip).
>
> Ask me anything you want. I'll be driving around in the area as I settle in and will update things here as I learn more.
>
> Cheers,
> Lee

Thanks Lee for your detailed report.

Your observations about major highways being EMF death corridors is spot on. Folks should remember that fact when they relocate to more rural areas. If they still have to run into towns and cities for supplies, services, and visit friends/families then they still have to run the gauntlet of one death tower after another, to and from.

I wonder what the health effects might be if you are cocooned safely in an EMF quiet spot for weeks and then you subject your body periodically to hours or days of EMF hot zones in cities. Will some get sick after excursions to urban areas? I wonder.

Perhaps one day there will a huge demand and outcry for more 'EMF Quiet Zones' like the National Radio Quiet Zone (NRQZ) established by the FCC in West Virginia. The reason NRQZ was established was to protect radio telescopes there from interference from modern radio devices.

> 11\. What this basically boils down to is that pristine environments (or at least the best you can get without more sensitive measuring equipment) are 1) on the very outskirts of town (if you can see other houses, make sure it's not too many) or 2) in the middle of nowhere. The problem with number 2) being that, if you are in a place like Denver, the EMF will carry about 30 to 50 miles out from town. Making a commute quite impractical.
>
> Breakdown by city:
> 1\. New Orleans - I actually didn't go into the city, because I can always just ask Jack what it's like there. Where I was, in Madisonville, LA (north of Pontchatrain), levels were extremely low. Low population density + water + trees = low levels. Jack can weigh in on how the city of New Orleans itself is.
> 2\. Gulfport and Biloxi, MS - low population densities along the coast here (the area is less developed than that further east) + small cities (Gulfport and Biloxi are rather small) lead to low levels.
> 3\. Pensacola - not so bad, but you get higher readings around the port and the downtown area, equivalent with what I found in larger cities. Just a few minutes in the right direction, though, and the levels drop off rapidly, giving you plenty of options.
> 4\. East of Pensacola (Navarre beach, Highway 30A, Miramar Beach, Santa Rosa Beach) - nice, low levels around just about every corner off of the highway. Beautiful place to live. Still some affordable real estate in the area.
> 5\. Tallahassee - similar story as Pensacola
> 6\. Savannah - relatively low levels in most neighborhoods, but the ubiquity of wifi routers and wireless devices makes it hard to find a place with low levels of EMF within the city itself. Plenty of rural land outside the city is, I would imagine, quite low.
> 7\. Charleston - way more densely populated than I had anticipated. If you're willing to live a good 30 minutes outside the city (more lik 45 minutes or an hour with traffic), you can find a nice spot. There is land for sale in the area that will hopefully remain rural for some time (especially if you choose a spot next to swamps, rivers, or national forest, of which there is quite a lot).
> 8\. Tampa - levels were about half what they were in Denver, but still not great. Even at the beach, hundreds of yards from anything, levels are higher than I would like (think 1 microwatt/meter to 500 microwatts with a strong pulse).
>
> Where did I decide to go?
>
> Somewhere near Destin. Likely a little ways east, toward Panama City. The long term goal is to buy a nice piece of property and develop it into a clinic. For now, you can find me working in the hospitals at Fort Walton and Panama City Beach (where I had contracts already in the works before I started the trip).
>
> Ask me anything you want. I'll be driving around in the area as I settle in and will update things here as I learn more.
>
> Cheers,
> Lee

What readings did you get in Charleston?

**red cells (erythrocyte):** 6.67 ml/L (interval is 4.20-6)
**average red cells volume:** 61 fl (interval is 80-99)
**erythrocyte hemoglobin:** 20.40 pg (interval is 27-33.50)

Labs tell you very little. Your environment is what you react too. You have not even mentioned it, which I think on this site is ironic but likely where your problem lies.

I've been telling people the same story over and over again in different ways to get at deeper and deeper levels of understanding on this site. We are now at the most fundamental levels in blogs and on the forum, in using light frequency to get the message through to you. There is no deeper or smaller level to head too beyond this level. The metabolic trap door was and has been in the eye. The skin, gut, and lung surface must get the same light signal the eye does or you get ill and inflammation rises. I have been telling you the truth a long time, but too few ever really listen well. You have to learn to observe to learn why things happen. Inflammation is all about a proton disorder. Inflammation is the collateral damage of cellular chaos. Inflammation is related to pH. pH is a log scale of protons. Optimal health is an environmental decision and choice, it’s not a genetic lottery. The genome story is an idea from people printing money from half-truths.

**Why is change difficult for humans? With the eyes of a mole we see and judge our own dogmatic views and faults - with the eye of an eagle we judge the opinions of others. In this way, accept our persistent mediocrity we allow for ourself and dismiss ideas that might elevate us.

Your environment makes you ill.........it is that simple.

Now get learning about that.**

# Thread: Sue-UK Journal 2018 -2024
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 266
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sue-UK — 2018-05-26T12:16:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-242076>

Time for a fresh journal!

I seem to be doing much better, I'm very slowly losing weight, people who see me regularly don't seem to notice, because the difference between each meeting seems so small, but I met a friend I haven't seen for 18 months yesterday, and the difference between then and now was obvious.  I'm happy with the rate of fat loss, living in the cold lane over the winter it has felt like I'm a hibernating bear losing weight slowly, particularly during the overnight hibernation period. I'm never thirsty overnight as I have been in the past, and I don't wake up desperate for a drink of water. I'm drawn to drinking more in the evening, intuitively it feels like I'm craving drinking my first freeze well water to slightly deuterium deplete my blood before going to bed, so that excess deuterium in the fat I burn doesn't get dumped back where I don't want it. I don't need to get up in the night to pee, so I think my vasopression circadian rhythm is OK? And my environment is not stopping fat burning?

In the Somlyai book the mention of Aspergillus niger (p48) keeps jumping out at me. _..."which in a heavy water medium turns alabaster white which means that the fungus is unable to produce the pigment responsible for the black color."
_
So if I was/am deuterium loaded, deuterium depleting could be improving my melanin/alpha MSH pathway? I'll keep going ....I seem to be tanning much better, considering the number of days of bad weather .

We've had some good days of UV 5/6, but a lot of days of rain, but I just take it when its available, and don't fret the rest of the time. The no longer fretting about a few days of rain interested me. It's a definite mental shift.

Although its a study on rats using oral vitamin D3, [http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.429.8534&rep=rep1&type=pdf](http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.429.8534&rep=rep1&type=pdf) the percentage of vitamin D3 in the brains of supplemented rats following a period of D3 deprivation is interesting, particularly compared to the percentage going to and being released from the fat cells and skin. (table 1).

In a book about vitamin D, in a bit that talks about Parkinson's and dopamine, it talked about the area of the brain that is affected having a very high concentration of vitamin D receptors. But what jumped out at me was _"Intriguingly, dopamine can actually bind to the vitamin D receptor. "
_
Intuitively it makes sense that if the brain can't rely on having high vitamin D levels at the end of winter, or during hibernation, there would be survival value in something else stepping up to the brain's (deuterium normal) vitamin D receptor plate ....

### Phosphene — 2018-06-07T17:18:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-242932>

Greetings Sue!

While it’s of course awesome that you’re doing better and have started a new journal to reflect that, I have mixed feelings about the old one perhaps slipping into obscurity? Your journal was among several that I read voraciously when I first found this site, drawn back again and again by your brilliant insights, keen humor and sense of...otherworldlyness. Would be a shame for others to miss out on that opportunity. May I suggest adding a link to the old one? (Feel free to tell me to bugger off with this...)

### Joyfun — 2018-06-07T18:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-242939>

[@Sue-UK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9658/)
Hi Sue-UK,
Phosphene certainly piqued my interest on reading your older journal.
What worked for me losing weight when I was not even trying, was to eat my 2 meals outside AND my feet grounded. I went from 129.8 pounds ( 58.9 Kg) in April 18, 2017 to 118.8 pounds (53.9 Kg) in August 3, 2107. My weight stays between 117 and 118 pounds. My last meal ends by 3pm. I do eat a fruit serving at 5pm. I am 5 feet 4 inches tall (apx 165 cm).
A link sounds like a nice idea to the older journal.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-07T21:47:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-242957>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=242932)
>
> Greetings Sue!
>
> While it’s of course awesome that you’re doing better and have started a new journal to reflect that, I have mixed feelings about the old one perhaps slipping into obscurity? Your journal was among several that I read voraciously when I first found this site, drawn back again and again by your brilliant insights, keen humor and sense of...otherworldlyness. Would be a shame for others to miss out on that opportunity. May I suggest adding a link to the old one? (Feel free to tell me to bugger off with this...)

Thank you for your kind words, and thinking about it, it might be useful if I know I've posted some info in there somewhere, so [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-blogs-and-a-changing-journey.13824/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-blogs-and-a-changing-journey.13824/),  All my printed blogs and research is currently ummm in transit …

I'm living in 2 mobile homes on the edge of the field, near my old house. Love it, its in a T shape, with 2 doors connected by decking. The East Wing is the sitting room, kitchen area, a snug, a bathroom and an office and The West Wing has the living area as our bedroom, bathroom, and the dining area has the washing machine etc (not on at night), a room for my lights, and 2 bedrooms. In our bedroom my side of the bed is only a couple of feet away from a second outside door, and I've rigged it up so that with a couple of bricks stopping the door from either slamming shut or swinging open and hitting the exterior, I can have the night air blowing on my face. The birds are my alarm clock to get up for the sunrise. My bath is outside … Places to sunbathe, eat outside … bliss, its like going on a looong caravanning holiday.

We're not ready to sell our old house yet because the universe had something up its sleeve … the first floor apartment we already own had the tenant leave, and that was going to be our winter bolt hole if the weather forces a temporary migration, and until then it was going to be somewhere our student children could stay in when they came home. (i.e. they do their own washing cooking and cleaning, and no moaning about my turning off the lights or wanting to do a meal well before sunset etc ). But it would have been a squeeze if we'd needed it as a bolthole when they were there. But the apartment underneath came up for sale, and the deal was done before it hit the open market…Gotta love a bit of synchronicity..... we should pick up the keys tomorrow, and be able to furnish it with what's left in our old house and then clear the rest in one hit. Two two bed apartments is much cheaper than one 4 bed house - and when our house is sold we'll be mortgage free …. more winter holiday money.  And with my never needing to use the upstairs apartment, no extra washing, cooking or cleaning for them …. And if we want to go out in town for a meal or a drink without having to drive home, we can. In 3 years time when the youngest finishes full time education we can rethink it … Until then, 9 months minimum in the countryside, 3 months max of the apartment (with holidays booked in the 3 month time frame) …. gotta be worth a hack.

### caroline — 2018-06-08T11:35:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-242996>

well done Sue ....you rock!

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-08T11:44:27+0300

Hi Joyfun

That didn't work for me when I was leptin resistant, heavily glycated (which affects sun absorption), and when I couldn't burn fat effectively at night. I would read about people effortlessly losing weight, was doing what they were doing and breaking the ice in mid winter and freezing my arse off, and although there were improvements it took the mild deuterium depletion to really start moving the needle, and my summer lodgings are helping. Every time I move between the east and west wings, I get a sunlight snack.  At the latitude I live at, I expect my optimal weight to vary with the season, if I stay within a pound all year, its a circadian mismatch. I very rarely weigh myself, and now I've moved I don't even have a kitchen scales, or ml measuring things for liquids. I cook by eye smell and taste, the chunk, the dollop, the slosh and the sprinkle method.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-12T12:24:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-243234>

So the new info on the skin has had the cogs going ….. What about the skin on the eyelids …
I found a pic online of the anatomy of the eyelid skin, which is the thinnest skin in the body, but it has some SQ fat …. (information signalling for animals with fur who may have to almost close their eyes to protect the eye from freezing or UV reflection from the snow and ice - sense of smell more dominant than sight?)
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

(Source [https://www.centrallakesclinic.biz/eyelid-diseases/anatomy-of-the-eyelids.html](https://www.centrallakesclinic.biz/eyelid-diseases/anatomy-of-the-eyelids.html)).

I'm at a point with my damaged eye that I can't look to the left or right of the sun once its above the tree line (yellow/UV becoming dominant), because the reflex in my right pupil isn't working properly (I have developed a slight white film over part of my right pupil - I don't want a name for it....). I spotted it whilst peering at my skin in a magnifying mirror, DH couldn't see it from his distance to it peering over my shoulder at my reflection, but he did see it through a proper magnifying glass. I almost need diagonal eyelids but in the absence of those, for my n=1 I need to work differently.

I've also noticed that I am tanning very well, except that I've got white tan lines on my neck from looking down reading the blogs, articles and books. So I've started a hack where I take an ice cube and run it over my face and eyes to just cool the surfaces (don't want my eyelid fat to disappear if it turns out its even more important to a mammal that live in the northern hemisphere), and I massage my face and neck in the cold melt water. Then I'm leaning back in a chair to expose my neck fully, and closing my eyes and sunning my eyelids.

Another thing I've notice recently is that I'm using nicotine gum too much, its not for the nicotine (I could go days without it), but its the chewing. I'm wondering if I'm actually trying to generate current through the facial bones muscles and collagen …  If I do the ice cube facial, and get the sun on my neck and through closed eyes, I'm not interested in chewing gum … Interesting ….

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-13T13:12:39+0300

> [Joyfun said:](/goto/post?id=243303)
>
> Hi Sue-UK,
> I have been thinking about what you said about the eyelids. It's those little parts of us that all have an important function.

I was thinking about babies being born with loads of fat, and they sleep a lot .. Eyelids in sunshine, or in the cold of the den directing the stored fat to develop the brain?

I did an accidental hack last night .. I'd opened the door by my bed as usual, but pulled the curtain back a bit to let the natural summer night in. (No artificial lighting pollution) The door is east facing, and I stayed fast asleep for a couple of hours after sunrise. The sun wasn't visible, but I would have got indirect morning light on my closed lids. I woke when the sun was rising over the East Wing, so I just pushed the door open fully, and lay there sunbathing with my eyes closed, naked on the bed with my feet on my grounding mat. Awesome! With the short summer nights I've been getting less sleep, and I've got to say I feel better for the extra couple of hours sleep.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-18T12:48:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-243654>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=243308)
>
> I did an accidental hack last night .. I'd opened the door by my bed as usual, but pulled the curtain back a bit to let the natural summer night in. (No artificial lighting pollution) The door is east facing, and I stayed fast asleep for a couple of hours after sunrise. The sun wasn't visible, but I would have got indirect morning light on my closed lids. I woke when the sun was rising over the East Wing, so I just pushed the door open fully, and lay there sunbathing with my eyes closed, naked on the bed with my feet on my grounding mat. Awesome! With the short summer nights I've been getting less sleep, and I've got to say I feel better for the extra couple of hours sleep.

This is a comparison of the night time timings for my latitude between 21st June and 21st December.

June 21st (on British summer time, actual times one hour earlier).
**Astronomical twilight begins 01:00:01**
Nautical twilight begins 02:57:59
Dawn - civil twilight begins 04:09:32
**Sunrise: **04:56:12****
Sun at its highest (transit)13:12:57
**Sunset: **21:29:42****
Dusk - civil twilight ends 22:16:22
Nautical twilight ends 23:27:55
**Astronomical twilight ends 01:00:01**

December 21st (actual times).
**Astronomical twilight begins 06:09:29**
Nautical twilight begins 06:49:52
Dawn - civil twilight begins 07:32:33
**Sunrise: **08:12:09****
Sun at its highest (transit)12:09:21
**Sunset: **16:06:34****
Dusk - civil twilight ends 16:46:09
Nautical twilight ends 17:28:51
**Astronomical twilight ends 18:09:14**

At the moment I'm not naturally getting any complete darkness at night, in December from the end of astronomical twilight in the evening to the time it begins in the morning is 12 hours … Blackout curtains don't seem as effective now because its the dark and boring without the cold, and they block the cool night air..… From usually waking up when the birds start, or just before, I've slept past sunrise a couple of times in the last week, and was wondering about my circadian rhythm going out of whack, but on rereading Energy and Epigenetics 9, Quantum sleep: "_Two main mechanisms where melanopsin is used to regulate sleep in mammals are: 1. The circadian mechanism, which determines the optimal time for sleep. 2. The homeostatic system, which keeps track of how long the body is awake and asleep, and triggers **"sleep pressure"** when the body suffers from sleep deprivation."_ So as its acute and not chronic, I've been leaving the door right next to my bed open, and the curtain pulled back so that I get the cool from the night breeze in my lungs, and the morning light still hits my closed eyelids and my face. I'm rolling with it for the moment during what for me seems to be a difficult light cycle for optimal sleep ...

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-22T12:28:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-244033>

[@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/).

[https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD\_ID=1350448](https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=1350448)

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=243977)
>
> [@Sue-UK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9658/)
>
> Wondering if you might have any thoughts on this since you seem well versed in leptin, and other things.

I didn't want to sidetrack the thread you posted on, with what are musings, but in my own journal I will muse away.

The appetitive phase, consisting of foraging and food hoarding, is quite interesting … I know that when I was leptin resistant I was a real food hoarder … My fridge, freezer and store cupboards meant I could easily withstand a long siege … As I came out of leptin resistance, my shopping and food storage habits changed completely, I couldn't understand why I'd got so much …

I was watching a programme on squirrels last night, and a lot of the studies were observation of the squirrels in their natural habitat. Tree squirrels who are scatter hoarders grow larger brains apparently heading into winter, which then go back to normal size in the spring. They did a test whereby they chipped 300 nuts which the squirrels checked out and buried for the winter, and they retrieved 10% as unfound. Some would have been found by accident, but they reckoned on around a 90% accuracy rate, which for the number they bury, is one hell of a feat.... They believed the brain growth was to support memorising where the nuts were. It was a shame they didn't investigate whether the accuracy rate was greater in females, but a female polar bear conceives in the spring, and the oocyte doesn't get implanted until autumn, and if she is not holding enough fat, or can't den, she absorbs the oocyte, rather than waste energy on a pregnancy she can't sustain.

None of this is conscious, whether its to accumulate fat, or to store food (in my case both! ) its like survival programming running in the background, (via the leptin receptor?) changing anatomy, physiology, reproduction and behaviour, all dictated by the environment and the season the animal senses. Because she carries the next generation, I see the female survival programme as being much more sensitive. Its why I currently believe that telling women they need to exercise willpower, eat less or once a day, or aim for X pounds on the scale, exercise more etc is complete and utter shite. If the environment a woman senses leaves her chronically and pathologically leptin resistant (as opposed to an animal being acutely physiologically leptin resistant at the right time of year), she is fighting against her survival programming. Failure is predictable, yet both myself in the past and many women I've talked to, beat themselves up about it, self esteem plummets etc. Jack's teaching about not doing IF if you are leptin resistant was one of many nudges at the start of my journey that I was in the right place ….

Big time and made more out of control by poor environments.

I've starting thinking about how we all knew about but misinterpreted strong solar energy. Built into everyone I know is the understanding that if another person was working outside on a hot day they simply won't be hungry and there is no need to press them to eat. It's not like they have a stomach ache. It's just understood that they got "too hot" to sustain hunger. I feel like that's been right there staring me in the face my whole life.

### drezy — 2018-06-25T16:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-244295>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=244279)
>
> Am I nabbing some UV photons from starlight …

Cool question. I hadn't thought about that. I want to play...

There is a nice diagram that others frequently post that shows the rough percentages of UV-C,UV-B, and UV-A light transmission through the earths atmosphere. This is similar but not that picture (I need to save the other one soon):

So there is your crude transmission band. Seeing starlight and assuming that it is visible spectrum would, by definition, be egregious anthropocentrism.

Other stars emit UV and about the same transmission band exists for them( there may be minor day/night differences with ionized gases and all). The amount of UV photons compare to the sun would be minuscule, but how many times have we heard "non-linear effect"?

So, wanting to play, I'll see your stars and throw in sunlight reflected off of the other planets in our solar system. The first big question being is it time for me to stop pointing and laughing at astrologers? Maybe not.

When you think about it the subset of death metal fans that are also members of knitting clubs probably outnumbers the set of humans that sleep outside or at least get starlight (not through a closed window) on them while they sleep. Still maybe you could be the first quantum-bio-astrologer.

Good question Sue.

My mind immediately snapped to the [Dutch Famine](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21955503) and it's effect on the children in the womb at the time. Having hung around here long enough I don't just think of food or lack of food solely as a stressor. It's a source of environmental signaling, via electrons, and is best when matched to light (RPE to postrema and all that jazz). [Mars effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars_effect) anyone?

I'm not convinced that the sphinx is just looking at the sunrise. Its belly down too, as if it had been sleeping facing east under the stars, and raised his head when the sun rose. I was asleep, I am re-awakened. Watching east in the early a.m. light is awesome, as the sun slowly rises it begins to take out the other stars one by one (but one whose name I don't know because I'm still on lesson 1 of my quantum bio astrology course ) always holds out longer that its nearest companions, which makes me smile). Am I nabbing some actual photons from the stars or are they virtual photons, a bit like a placebo … I respond "as if " they are real. But even if the UV didn't penetrate the ozone layer …as we're playing, do "pilot waves" still get through.

I would suspect the ozone layer is more dynamic, perhaps thinning at the poles? Does it change diurnally or seasonally? Thinking of the artic animals that don't hibernate, den or live in burrows, enduring the harshest of winters in the open.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-26T21:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-244377>

> [WalterNL said:](/goto/post?id=244360)
>
> Well you bring up an interesting point; does total darkness exist in nature at all? Darkness as in no photons doesn't exist because we create our own light. Nights with clears skies have stars or the moon. Nights with cloud cover?

I see phosphenes in the dark, and behind closed lids, so if I'm awake I don't ever see total darkness, even in a blacked out room. But I have done experiments from astrological twilight in the morning through to +6 solar elevation, using just a CD and a black box spectroscope, pointing to the east. Unless the cloud is very dense and wall to wall, I get a reasonable idea of the changing frequencies and the intensity, even with cloud. Cloud cover at sunrise doesn't bother me like it used to, because I know the clock is getting enough photons to do what its supposed to do. If its very cloudy at sunrise I sometimes get the box out and have a look through it, its enough to raise dopamine if I'm thinking shit its cloudy again … And having taken flights at sunrise, a meditation I sometimes do is to sit grounded on the earth and take my consciousness above the clouds to have a look … I made myself some +6 solar elevation glasses for when it was wall to wall grey cloud in winter, but I don't have to use them to try and convince my SCN what time it is. I think I'm also getting information from the earth moving out of the magnetotail, which is perhaps how denning or animals facing complete darkness also keep track of time?

the last years I sleep right by the wide open window, no curtains.

I am addicted to watch the sky from my bed as I wake up… and also when I go to sleep.
If I have to sleep somewhere I cant do this, I feel a bit like in prison….

This came from In this study, [https://www.nature.com/articles/srep26756#f1](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep26756#f1). _" we collected spectroradiometric measurements of downwelling illumination as a function of solar elevation (time of day) in a rural location (Cherry Springs State Park, PA) and in a city location with light pollution (Philadelphia, PA)."
_
So my question is, if its correct that we don't get UVC from our own sun through the ozone layer, why does the reading for 200-300 nm in the astronomical and dark phases in the rural area seem to be above zero, when the city figure seems to be at zero?

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-29T12:29:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-244547>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=244427)
>
> This came from In this study, [https://www.nature.com/articles/srep26756#f1](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep26756#f1). _" we collected spectroradiometric measurements of downwelling illumination as a function of solar elevation (time of day) in a rural location (Cherry Springs State Park, PA) and in a city location with light pollution (Philadelphia, PA)."
> _
> So my question is, if its correct that we don't get UVC from our own sun through the ozone layer, why does the reading for 200-300 nm in the astronomical and dark phases in the rural area seem to be above zero, when the city figure seems to be at zero?

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=244455)
>
> [https://www.esrl.noaa.gov/csd/assessments/ozone/2010/twentyquestions/Q4.pdf](https://www.esrl.noaa.gov/csd/assessments/ozone/2010/twentyquestions/Q4.pdf) How is total ozone distributed over the globe?
>
> Interesting seasonal maps.

My musings so far …. From the paper: _The distribution of total ozone over the Earth varies with location on timescales that range from daily to seasonal."_
So if my thought experiment for the moment excludes seasonal global movement of ozone, in terms of daily variation at a particular location, if ozone is produced by UV sunlight reacting with oxygen to produce ozone, then presumably maximum "good ozone" is produced at midday rather than at midnight. So while the Cherry Springs figures show a low figure, it is still above zero during astronomical twilight and darkness. The Philadelphia city flatline figures for those times is possibly due to "bad ozone" from pollution.

How important is the low levels at night to nocturnal animals if they sleep in the dark during the day away from natural sunlight? If they sleep outside in a rural area, do humans "harvest" it on their skin (particularly closed eyelids) and through the lungs at night? The ultimate recycler of light seems to be the polar bear. A denning mother won't see daylight or starlight for months. So her white fur being made of a special crystalline structure that reflects any light emission back to be absorbed through her black skin may be her back door? If she's sleeping then any UV emitted from her eyes could be being reabsorbed by the blood running through her eyelids .. ?

Human eyelid skin interests me … its thin, it contains fat …Is there a higher concentration of melanopsin in the eyelid skin? Ho hum ..Still musing and digging …

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-29T18:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-244572>

On Davos, Switzerland, altitude 5120 ft.
(Modern Actinotherapy by Raymond H Beckett BA., 1955)
Sunshine at Davos, infrared over 7000 angstrom 41.3%, visible 7000 to 4000 angstrom, 57.4 %. Long wave UV 4000 to 3200 angstrom 1.2%, Erythemal UV (EUV)below 3200 angstrom 0.1%.
_"Figures represent midday, midsummer sunshine at Davos, where Dorno has conducted a unique series of measurements. The low percentage of EUV may surprise those who know Alpine sunshine, but taking into account the vast energy of sunshine (calculated as 6,000 horse-power per acre), even 0.1 means a considerable amount."_ (reference Dorno, C (1928) "Premiere Conference Internationale de La Lumiere", Paris, 59.

I wonder what the figures are now compared to around 1928, or the 2009 maps.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-30T12:22:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-244604>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=243234)
>
> I'm at a point with my damaged eye that I can't look to the left or right of the sun once its above the tree line (yellow/UV becoming dominant), because the reflex in my right pupil isn't working properly (I have developed a slight white film over part of my right pupil - I don't want a name for it....). I spotted it whilst peering at my skin in a magnifying mirror, DH couldn't see it from his distance to it peering over my shoulder at my reflection, but he did see it through a proper magnifying glass. I almost need diagonal eyelids but in the absence of those, **for my n=1 I need to work differently.**

So I'm working differently... I made the observation that at sunrise whilst outside looking at the sun, my eyes are comfortable, and that I was unconsciously doing head movements, shoulder shrugs and moving sideways in a pendulum movement similar to the Bates Method. Sunning the eyelids is also part of it. So I raked out my copy of Better Sight Without Glasses by Harry Benjamin yesterday which is based on the Bates method, and worked out a routine, but with the exercises all done outside whilst grounded. There's a good illustration showing a view of the eyeballs from above, showing muscles and arteries and the optic nerve, and what interests me was the infra orbital artery and the branch to the upper eyelid. So in a too strong (for me) summer sunlight, I have a route in.  The adjustment I've done is to the exercise called swinging, where you stand with feet 12 inches apart, hands loosely at the sides, and sway the whole body from side to side like a pendulum clock , raising each heel alternately, but not the whole foot. The whole body has to sway gently to and fro, with no bending from the waist and hips. You do it for a minute with eyes open, then a minute with eyes closed, and repeat. The books (first published 1929, my edition from 1984) suggests it should be done out of the window, but because I'm doing it outside in bright summer sunlight, I did the minute with eyes closed facing the sun in the east, and then turned round and did the minute with the eyes open facing west, with the sun on the head including the visual cortex. I also did the blinking, and palming my eyes exercise. I've tweaked it to do eye palming outside and grounded, but from the palming the eyes section, what jumped out was "_In this way the eyes are rested much more effectively than by any other method, and the more black the colour that is seen when palming, the more relaxed is the state of the eyes. "_ As I never see blackness behind closed eyes, how that progresses could be interesting …

Its free, uses natural light and doesn't screw with my circadian rhythm. Whilst the sunrise is so early that I can easily be outside 3 hours, fitting this hack in will be easy, and its something I can do after a session on the computer. Having come online to post this, I'm off to do it again. Gotta attend to my eyeballs.

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-06T17:43:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-245152>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=243654)
>
> This is a comparison of the night time timings for my latitude between 21st June and 21st December.
>
> June 21st (on British summer time, actual times one hour earlier).
> **Astronomical twilight begins 01:00:01**
> Nautical twilight begins 02:57:59
> Dawn - civil twilight begins 04:09:32
> **Sunrise: **04:56:12****
> Sun at its highest (transit)13:12:57
> **Sunset: **21:29:42****
> Dusk - civil twilight ends 22:16:22
> Nautical twilight ends 23:27:55
> **Astronomical twilight ends 01:00:01**
>
>
> December 21st (actual times).
> **Astronomical twilight begins 06:09:29**
> Nautical twilight begins 06:49:52
> Dawn - civil twilight begins 07:32:33
> **Sunrise: **08:12:09****
> Sun at its highest (transit)12:09:21
> **Sunset: **16:06:34****
> Dusk - civil twilight ends 16:46:09
> Nautical twilight ends 17:28:51
> **Astronomical twilight ends 18:09:14**
>
> At the moment I'm not naturally getting any complete darkness at night, in December from the end of astronomical twilight in the evening to the time it begins in the morning is 12 hours … Blackout curtains don't seem as effective now because its the dark and boring without the cold, and they block the cool night air..… From usually waking up when the birds start, or just before, I've slept past sunrise a couple of times in the last week, and was wondering about my circadian rhythm going out of whack, but on rereading Energy and Epigenetics 9, Quantum sleep: "_Two main mechanisms where melanopsin is used to regulate sleep in mammals are: 1. The circadian mechanism, which determines the optimal time for sleep. 2. The homeostatic system, which keeps track of how long the body is awake and asleep, and triggers **"sleep pressure"** when the body suffers from sleep deprivation."_ **So as its acute and not chronic, I've been leaving the door right next to my bed open, and the curtain pulled back so that I get the cool from the night breeze in my lungs, and the morning light still hits my closed eyelids and my face. I'm rolling with it for the moment during what for me seems to be a difficult light cycle for optimal sleep** ...

Looking back through the stats, it seems that the last time there was true darkness was 24th May (25 minutes) and the date its due back is 18th July, (also 25 minutes). Having rolled with it, I seem to be waking up now in time for the sunrise, so I get outside and watch the sunrise, and when the sun goes above the trees and yellow/uv is hitting, because its still so early I'm going back to bed, and usually have another hour or so's sleep with the sun on my face when it clears the top of the East Wing. Not quite camping, but I'm hoping my hack is giving me seasonal information, and the extra sleep is definitely welcome.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=244572)
>
> On Davos, Switzerland, altitude 5120 ft.
> (Modern Actinotherapy by Raymond H Beckett BA., 1955)
> Sunshine at Davos, infrared over 7000 angstrom 41.3%, visible 7000 to 4000 angstrom, 57.4 %. Long wave UV 4000 to 3200 angstrom 1.2%, Erythemal UV (EUV)below 3200 angstrom 0.1%.
> _"Figures represent midday, midsummer sunshine at Davos, where Dorno has conducted a unique series of measurements. The low percentage of EUV may surprise those who know Alpine sunshine, but taking into account the vast energy of sunshine (calculated as 6,000 horse-power per acre), even 0.1 means a considerable amount."_ (reference Dorno, C (1928) "Premiere Conference Internationale de La Lumiere", Paris, 59.
>
> I wonder what the figures are now compared to around 1928, or the 2009 maps.

So two references imply that in a natural environment, UVC is not stopped 100% by the ozone layer 100% of the time. From the Modern Actinotherapy book, the alpine lamps that were developed (because of the short Alpine summer season?) included UVC wavelengths. At the time of writing his book Into the Light, William Campbell Douglas MD was using UVC blood irradiation. Having been given the information just before going to press, he relates the work of Robert C Olney MD's use of UVA in the treatment of cancer. _"I have been employing ultraviolet C, while Dr Olney used ultraviolet A. Ultraviolet C is not the best frequency for treatment of cancer, though, as you will see, it does produce some beneficial results."_

William Campbell Douglas was in the process of engineering a new blood irradiation device to include wavelengths for cancer treatment, but went on to describe some of his results up to that point. One of his own cases was a woman with brain cancer secondary to breast cancer, (a no hope case). After 6 weeks of photooxidation therapy, there was no change in the brain scan, but the woman was doing very well until she went home for 10 days, and without the therapy, rapidly deteriorated, and died. During the improvement phase, her headaches became bearable and less frequent, she regained lost weight, a plantar wart on her right foot disappeared after having been there for 25 years, two moles disappeared from her left leg. After one week of therapy, her hair became shiny, and she reported a reduction in greyness. _"But the most remarkable change in JF was the improvement in her vision. She had to throw away her old glasses because they were "too strong". She needed no correction for distance vision and only a small correction for reading."_

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-07T15:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-245218>

This is the power of shortwave UV light. This is light YOU MUST MAKE from UVB and UVA light the sun provides. This was the key take-home of Vermont 2018.

Fireworks time: Did you know that naturally occurring deuterium in water and from photosynthesis is essential for the normal growth rate of cells? Do you know why? Deuterium is how cells make UVC light using mechanisms in their skin and skin arterioles. Naturally occurring deuterium is where the ELF-UV is created because it is needed to stimulate mitosis in the cell cycle to create cell division. The effect of ELF-UV light is talked about in detail in Roeland Van Wijk's book. How we make ELF-UVC light from deuterium in our blood plasma was covered in my Vermont 2018 talk. here:[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/8428617/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/8428617/)

I understood the take home of Vermont 2018, but my interest in looking back at the history of actinotherapy was that they were dealing with a lot of what could be described as "circling the drain" patients, using therapeutic devices that included UVC as well as other UV frequencies, mimicking the Alpine frequencies (where UVC was measured). After the Vermont talk I wondered if the successes of actinotherapy were linked to being able to reverse the drain circling to a point where the patient could begin to produce ELF-UVC endogenously, which perhaps the patient I mentioned from the book hadn't reached, so quickly went downhill after taking a break from treatment.

I have a question about sweat … My mild deuterium depletion and currently consistent days of UV8, my sweating pattern has improved. IIRC sweating depletes deuterium, how significant to the skin surface is strong sunlight on sweaty deuterium soaked skin as a source of ELF-UV? Or does it have a role in terms of resonance matching with the ELF-UVC from deuterium made in the blood plasma?

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-08T11:45:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-245288>

One big difference I've noticed is that despite the hot weather, I've not even thought about using ghee on my skin, like I did last year. It just doesn't need it, my skin seems to be holding its own. I'm definitely slowly losing fat from my belly and thighs. Aesthetically my thighs look worse at the moment, but from past experience (in the days I could burn fat easily) I go through phases of it looking worse, presumably because of not burning fat evenly. But its not cellulite or glycation, its like a bit of remodelling goes on, and the skin looks even again. But as SQ fat is an active organ, I'm not trying to force it into submission. With my previous crash diet history, (and watching my mother's SQ fat levels as she went downhill), that could bite me in arse.  Making the most of the good weather, and have 2 weeks planned for the end of October in Fuerteventura and another 2 weeks at the end of December. They are definites, but another towards the end of February is also on the cards. I don't want to live abroad permanently because I'm Mum and Barefoot Nana, and the thought of maintaining those relationships through skype and facetime and the occasional visit does nothing for my dopamine. But I'm happy living in the mobile homes, and if that allows me to get regularly to the sun over the winter, that my dopamine can handle.

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-09T11:00:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-245396>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=245291)
>
> Posting a link to Robert C Olney MD paper I've found re venous oxygenation with UV.
> [http://www.maebrussell.com/Health/Blocked Oxidation/Text.html](http://www.maebrussell.com/Health/Blocked%20Oxidation/Text.html)

The venous oxygen level increases in the study after UV are interesting. From the Vermont implications thread:

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=244563)
>
> When Mayer was bleeding sailors on these ships in tropical waters he was very alarmed to find out that the extracted blood was much redder than it was back in Germany. He initially thought that he had hit an artery and not a vein, but he soon found out from local physicians who practiced inside the tropics that it was "normal for venous blood to be redder" than it was in the colder Northern climates. This set up Mayer to begin one of the greatest mito-hacks in human history. Remember at this time humans had no idea cells had colonies of mitochondria. He did know that the great Lavoisier in France had proposed that animal respiration functioned to "produce heat" for tissues. He also knew that the change from red to blue blood from arteries to veins was due to the removal of oxygen from the blood for respiration. Thus, he was the first physician to realize that the more red blood was in the veins of a sailor in the tropics under higher UV conditions might be due to a "lowered need of respiration and heat production". Until Dr. Doug Wallace came along in mitochondrial studies to explain why mitochondrial haplotypes varied by latitude only Mayer linked it to more oxygen creation in the tropical environment and proved it by examining the patient's blood. This implies that anyone with a pulmonary disease tied to poor oxygenation, it might be very wise to visit the tropics often. It also means anyone with a mitochondrial disease needs to consider tropical light as their first-line therapy. Coupled haplotypes tend to have lower basal metabolic rates and they tend to live longest. Mathematics scaling laws link longevity to the basal metabolic rate for these reasons. Soon on the Patreon blog you'll learn precisely how pH and temperature fit the deuterium story. [https://atmos.washington.edu/~dennis/321/321\_Lecture\_23.pdf](https://atmos.washington.edu/~dennis/321/321_Lecture_23.pdf)

So my question is, with just normal breathing, and lots of sunlight on bare skin, can a better than the same time last year pulse oximeter reading give an indication that my skin is "breathing" better? Or that I'm better at producing endogenous ELF-UV?

Another really cool, especially as I have an easy and cheap way of watching the trend.  In the Van Wijk book on page 168, figure 9.5 on intermittent oxygen deficiency in cancer development, intermittent oxygen _deficiency_ leads to cancer development, so turning the chart upside down, would chronic low oxygen levels and intermittent _sufficiency_ do the same? With my previous chronically under optimal oxygen level, has my intuitively not wanting to do aerobic exercise or do deep breathing exercises been protective?

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-14T17:20:09+0300

From March 22nd 2018 entry in my old blog following a less than expected temperature in Fuerteventura

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=234820)
>
> I'd printed out [https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jpa/19/5/19\_5\_207/\_pdf](https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/jpa/19/5/19_5_207/_pdf) _The Turnover of Body Water as an Indicator of Health_ to read on the plane (Along with Elizabeth Blackburn's book The Telomere Effect). I was interested in the concept of the total body water turnover being higher in the young and in the healthy versus unhealthy, couldn't improve mine through sweating, but if CT offers a similar effect via the cold and increased urine, I just decided to go for it as much as possible, whilst getting as much UV as I could. (UV 6 for the clear sky hours).

Got this paper out again … _"Sigh et al (1989) reported that water turnover is 50 to 100% greater in tropical climate due to increased insensible water loss."_ With the use in the studies of D20 as a tracer, now that we're having a long spell of high UV, with my better sweating pattern, time for another read.

### drezy — 2018-07-14T18:39:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-245885>

Go past Sue!

FWIW most mornings I can sit on the east side of my property from sunrise until I sweat like a little piggy.

one of the the fringe benefits is visits by a hummingbird couple, 3 bunnies, and a momma fox, and my always faithful mockingbird. The bigger benefit is that I always feel great after this. I'm addicted to it at this point.

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-15T18:47:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-245925>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=245885)
>
> Go past Sue!
>
> FWIW most mornings I can sit on the east side of my property from sunrise until I sweat like a little piggy.
>
> one of the the fringe benefits is visits by a hummingbird couple, 3 bunnies, and a momma fox, and my always faithful mockingbird. The bigger benefit is that I always feel great after this. I'm addicted to it at this point.

It stays cooler until about 10 a.m. so I'm thinking about tweaking my hack to get the sunrise and then do a CT session before the heat kicks in. Finally moved my second CT tub from our old house yesterday - the metal, enamel lined seated one, now positioned facing east so I can still see the sun continuing to rise from just under the trees.

I'd stayed at DS1's house last night to babysit my 2 granddaughters, and walked them round to the mobile homes for breakfast outside. I'd got the CT tub ready for when DS1 collected them, for a bit of mitigation from a night of higher nnemf. Their house readings are still on the bottom green of my Cornet, but higher than the 0.0005 I get at home. That got my second bath move to the top of the priority list. DH got the tractor out …

The other bath gets the sun more, so I've been thinking its cool enough to stop sweating, but not cold enough for CT to deuterium deplete. So not such a good idea at this time of year if I'm trying to deuterium deplete. But the seated one was still nice and cold at 9.30 this morning, and it wouldn't take much effort to get colder. So I reckon that because heavy exercise isn't right for me at the moment, its worth tapping into the total body water turnover thing with CT before the heat hits, and then letting the heat do its magic on my newly CT'd respiratory proteins.

In the study the evaluation of water turnover for 8-14 days space flights compared to ground based is interesting. Wonder what Scott Kelly's was like after a year … or would be on a manned flight to Mars.

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-17T15:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-246050>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=245989)
>
> The lowest available deuterium depleted water is DDW-25.
> Is there a possibility that someone using DDW may deplete their deuterium to the point that their blood serum may have less than 150ppm?
> I would assume that such a case should be avoided even for cancer patients?
> Comments please?
>
> [View attachment 8306](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/8306/)

Having been rereading my books on actinotherapy in relation to infection, and trying to connect the deuterium dots , [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) question in the ask jack section has had the cogs going.

I've been intuitively wary of dropping my DDW water use below around 142 ppm from a first freeze. The question had me wondering if that intuitive whisper stems from, having deliberately kept herself thin all through her adult life, towards the end of her life my mum had lost her SQ fat stores, which I think is a storage site for deuterium? When her appetite went downhill, I think her natural deuterium losses exceeded her intake, she was emaciated, and that it is likely she had lower than 150 ppm blood serum levels. Add to that it was winter, in the nursing home hot lane, no deuterium programming by either natural sunlight or artificial UVA, and I think that without the stimulation of that to generate UVC to control bacteria and viruses, or the winter cold lane to slow them down, she became prone to an ultimately fatal infection. She was suspected of having cancer, but when it became clear that she wasn't going to survive the infection, it wasn't investigated. Although she was heading for the inevitable, I've wondered if a UVC haemo irradiation treatment might have changed the outcome at that particular moment in time.

We're having a lovely summer in the UK and I'm making the most of it, but if for example someone wasn't burning body fat properly, or they kept themselves deliberately thin for whatever reason, or were emaciated through illness, if they took full advantage of the sun by being outside, the good amount of UVA on a deliberately lowered ppm blood serum might be enough to protect them from infection. I've been wondering if that same lowered ppm deuterium blood serum level in the winter in the UK might be fatal in an already immune compromised person? And how much artificial UVA would be enough to mitigate that risk? If I was considering doing deuterium depletion for cancer, I'd want to know how I might be affecting my ability to produce endogenous UV to fight infection, and how I'd mitigate any risks. I may be missing something obvious  but in my defence at least I know I'm missing something ….

Thanks [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/)  Thought provoking question.

### Sue-UK — 2018-07-18T12:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-246118>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=246061)
>
> [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/max-on-ddw.21646/#post-246046](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/max-on-ddw.21646/#post-246046)
>
>
> [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/)
> [@Sue-UK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9658/)
> I have read and watched available Vermont info.
> Nothing ever is obvious to me, specially on this board.
> Would you mind giving me your understanding (to posed question) (please write it at 3 grade level)
>
> ...............

My understanding is that deuterium is not the enemy. It is how much and where it is, and how it is programmed that is important. I've never bought into the Cignature testing because I didn't see how the tests could tell me what was going on in individual organs, and I didn't think just depleting deuterium was enough, without understanding the detox pathways, or what would happen if blood levels got too low. The other thing I want to know is should deuterium accumulation change seasonally (for example to support breeding - thinking sperm, female orgasm) and what happens with deuterium in a hibernating animal.

I've been watching programmes about brown bears. In one programme, when the bear came out of hibernation it went for roots, then newly growing grass, then it chased down its prey, then came the salmon run, and then before hibernation it was gorging on berries. So it seems to have a seasonal cycle of deuterium collection, getting the DHA at the right time for seasonal membrane changes in advance of hibernation etc. When it came out of hibernation it went for deuterium loaded roots, (rather than chasing down prey) and when it went back into hibernation it would have been stuffed full of deuterium, presumably in the right places. (Physiological not pathological insulin resistance? )They don't pee or poo during hibernation, skin and lung elimination would be low, so higher deuterium appears to be important during the winter hibernation. Grounded, in the cold, in the absence of sunlight I think it must be being programmed endogenously, so I'm digging into the CT blogs again first, looking for clues … But if red light moves light hydrogen but not heavy, then it implies there's something else providing the right programming. (Is REM sleep important? And the liver? Seasonal changes in cell membranes? ) Bears that go into hibernation well fed and deuterium loaded, come out hungry but strong .. not starving, weak and infection prone.

I can almost see scenarios of seasonal variations of deuterium accumulation, deuterium obesity (pathological or physiological), and deuterium starvation/famine. In my own n=1 I don't think I've got the seasonal variation in accumulation, I'm probably still at a pathological deuterium accumulation level, even if its improving. My Mum had AD, suspected cancer, but her death certificate records infection as the cause of death. So whatever my problems, I've got to be sure I'm never at the deuterium starvation level, because if I am, and don't mitigate it if continuing to deuterium deplete is still the best course of action, it might be an infection that takes me out.
