### Sue-UK — 2023-04-01T13:32:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-320364>

Latest hack started on March 22nd using Kure water based on:

Transcript underneath the video of this interview with Dr Que Collins.

[https://melanieavalon.com/water/](https://melanieavalon.com/water/)

[https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0254619](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0254619)
**Effects of water stably-enriched with oxygen as a novel method of tissue oxygenation on mitochondrial function, and as adjuvant therapy for type 2 diabetes in a randomized placebo-controlled trial**
This is not using the Kure oxygenated water, and participants of the human trial didn't change any of their usual habits _("The average dissolved-oxygen (DO) level in ELO water ([Fig 2A](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0254619#pone-0254619-g002)) was significantly (p < 0.001) higher (16.40 ± 1.11 ppm), compared to tap water (8.31 ± 0.38 ppm)")_
_
[https://scialert.net/fulltext/?doi=jas.2022.207.210&org=11](https://scialert.net/fulltext/?doi=jas.2022.207.210&org=11)
**Oxygenated Water and Nano Engineered Water Benefits in Drinking Use and Food Processing: A Mini-Review**_
(This shows tap water at 5.7 mg/L tap water and 10-12 mg/l for fresh fountain water).
**
[https://www.hindawi.com/journals/jhe/2020/1323270/](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/jhe/2020/1323270/)
**Effects of Highly Oxygenated Water in a Hyperuricemia Rat Model****
_"The oxygen concentration of unoxygenated water was 3.7 mg/L, and after 3.5 h of oxygenation, the oxygen concentration rose to 38.5 mg/L. Subsequently, oxygen precipitation in the oxygenated water was measured at different temperatures and standing times as indicated in Tables [3](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/jhe/2020/1323270/tab3/) and [4](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/jhe/2020/1323270/tab4/), respectively. We did not observe a significant variation in oxygen concentration (37 mg/L to 32 mg/L) when the temperature was increased from 10 to 50°C. However, standing time played a more critical role in the amount of oxygen dissolved in the water. As indicated in Table [4](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/jhe/2020/1323270/tab4/), the oxygen concentration decreased from 29.2 mg/L to 16.8 mg/L when the standing time was 24 h."_

****[https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/21/15/5493](https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/21/15/5493)
Oxygenated Water Inhibits Adipogenesis and Attenuates Hepatic Steatosis in High-Fat Diet-Induced Obese Mice****
_"It was 100% saturation (40 mg/L) for the freshly prepared OW. The dissolved oxygen content decreased with time due to the diffusion of the dissolved oxygen into ambient air. It gradually decreased to 50% saturation (20 mg/L) in 12 h. The oxygen content further decreased to 30% (12 mg/L) saturation after another 12 h, and finally to 20% saturation (8 mg/L) in equilibrium with oxygen content in the air by the 40th hour after the initial OW preparation."_
So the human diabetes study was with roughly double the oxygen of unoxygenated water, with higher levels in other studies, and you could probably increase the oxygen levels of spring water by using a water fountain technique.

The Kure water apparently has 100 times the oxygen of normal water, marketed for things like energy, focus and exercise recovery, although I'm not sure that suddenly whamming that much oxygen in half a litre of water into the system in one hit is a good idea, deffo needs a bit of thought ............ but Que Collins mentioning it as a deuterium depleting tool is interesting .... I would suspect it might be something they would use to cut DDW.

DH's hack has had a more obvious result than mine so far ..... I think I can relate it to the timing of when he drinks it (about an hour before bed when he normally wouldn't drink for at least 2 hours before, so that's an added variable that Jack has talked about) and then having 100ml of the kure cut with 300ml spring water. Basically he sleeps too long and still snores ....thinks he sleeps well but since retiring last year I've noticed he often has a nap ... which has been a bit of a red flag to me. But this has changed to 8 to 8 and a half hours sleep, waking up naturally with the light stimulus, and no naps ... He's also naturally lost interest in an after dinner cup of tea. He's just gone away for a week and will continue the same thing, but he's up for a hack tweak when he gets back with only a minor eye roll ...

I always drink water just before bed, to the extent of sitting on the edge of the bed, drinking some water then getting in, although I drink most of my water earlier. My hack has been putting 100 ml with 400 ml of spring water, and just drinking normally throughout the day, including the pre-sleep drink, so roughly 2.5 litres overall. I usually sleep well anyway unless something is worrying me, such as the ongoing drama with the management company of the estate I live on  but that's gone from  to  and I'm more laid back about it, and its not affecting my sleep at all. Physically I feel a bit stronger, but its not easily quantified. My spO2 on a pulse oxymeter was already good, so can't really judge from that. One thing I have noticed is that I'm eating less and a couple of skintags that have been resistant to everything else I've done have started shrinking. The temptation is to increase the amount from 100 to 125 per half litre but with DH away I'm going to do a bit more IF and load up on seafood, which would be a variable anyway, so leaving any water tweak for now.

### Sue-UK — 2023-04-04T17:44:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-320402>

In the Que Collins transcript it says oxygen has an affinity about six or seven times the amount of affinity to deuterium than oxygen ... but I've been thinking about it in terms of light. We've had a couple of really nice sunny days, even if still a bit chilly. But drinking a half litre of 400ml spring water and 100ml oxygenated water first makes sunbathing a different experience. 2/3 naked, 1/3 kiniki covered. Out to get the light at the seafront around sunrise, then 3 hours sunbathing from 7-10, (proper time not British summer time ....) then in and out ...is quite a different experience,  but I don't think the particular effect can be because of deuterium depletion. From QE 36 I'm wondering if its because, having slept grounded all night, I have electrons .... and the addition of the extra oxygen to the spring water possibly reduces the proton ratio? so possibly if this improves the dialectric constant and refractive index it could mean I'm better able to absorb UV light ??

(+Melanin) 2H2 O (liquid) <-> 2H2 (gas) + O2 (gas) + 4e (Melanin+)

whereas the oxygen in the oxygenated water is starting in the middle  but neither seems complete, as they don't involve POMC ....?

Looking at it as

POMC-> MSH - > Melanin -> 2H2 O (liquid) <-> 2H2 (gas) + O2 (gas) + 4e <-Melanin<-MSH<-POMC

### JanSz — 2023-04-06T16:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-320426>

The regulation of water balance is essential for the maintenance of health and life. On average, a sedentary adult should drink between 1.5 L to 2.9 L of water per day, as water is the only liquid nutrient that is essential for body hydration.

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=320424)
>
> [https://www.researchgate.net/public...ere\_Do\_the\_CNS's\_High\_Oxygen\_Levels\_Come\_From](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/365998223_The_Circuit_of_Papez_Needs_Oxygen_to_Work_Properly_Where_Do_the_CNS's_High_Oxygen_Levels_Come_From)

There is a multitude of studies on body hydration.
Body hydration is highly non-descriptive and can easily be misunderstood.

About 100% of it when considering water assume that water comes to the body via drinking or with food.

Maybe some thinks of incoming water with the air we breathe.

Practically none considers metabolic water which volume or weight vise is the major amount of water body deals with.

Sedentary person:
Drinking water ---- about 2 liters/day
Breathing water ---- about 2 liters/day
Metabolic water ---- about 18 liters/day
................................................
Possibly one could look at drinking water as equivalent to oil in car's engine (that lubricates moving parts)
and
gasoline or diesel fuel ----->equivalent to metabolic water
\===========

So far, more thinking about the before it gets to aquaporin 8. Initial musings, long way to go with UB25 ....

"_You get higher biophoton emission rates when a steady stream of oxygen is present. All living cells have been shown to release bio-photons. Dead cells release no light. Sick cells release too much light."_

In terms of oxygenated water possibility.....??

_"UV light uses oxygen as its partner and water as its repository for energy storage in this evolutionary deal to build complexity. Life uses the DC electric power buried into the UV light frequency to build matter in tissues."

"When you run out of UV light storage in that tissue it fails. If you run out of it totally you die. The varied frequencies will lead to poor signaling and disease states since this is in flux. Simultaneously, your mitochondria will be chronically pseudohypoxic (low O2) and you will also not be able to use normal AM UV light cycles and nitrogen (NADH) to control carbon cycling in your cells."_

So its whether by not being able to use normal AM UV light, the mitochondria are increasing UV emission to try to partner with oxygen and regain control, and normalise biophoton emission. By increasing oxygen with oxygenated water, I'm wondering if that helps the mitochondria recover control using endogenous UV biophotons, and once control is re-established whether aquaporin 8 is the mechanism for excreting deuterium as D2O. As deuterium can't proton tunnel, if Que Collins is right about the increased affinity of oxygen to deuterium, maybe its taking it out as D2O via aquaporin 8? If control is re-established maybe it would mean being able to use normal AM UV again?

### Marko Pollo — 2023-04-18T18:21:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-320602>

Sue,
Very interesting. I have used two methods to super oxygenate water in past for growing yeast cultures and brewing compost tea. For the first, take a 3 or 5 gal Cornelious keg from home brew store or similar. Fill with water and cool down to 35 degrees or so as cold liquid will hold way more gas in suspension. Pressurize keg with O2 tank from welding or medical supply up to safe pressure. Shake keg and pressurize alternately to your desired pressure. Can be dispensed as needed by tap. Bubbly water without the carbonic acid.
Other method used an "air lift vortex brewer". Can be made in a bucket, I used an old soup kettle. I tested my O2 saturation with a meter and it was if I remember 3 times higher than one can achieve with air stone bubblers.
Good luck!

### Sue-UK — 2023-09-14T15:04:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sue-uk-journal-2018-2024.21390/post-326386>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=322102)

_

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-shocking.20489/#post-228644](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-shocking.20489/#post-228644)

"You want to test this idea in a mito-hack in your house? If you put aluminum foil in a microwave oven it will forst slicit a large electric spark with flames shooting about, giving off an orange halo glow. It’s not the metal that catches fire, but the aluminum oxide coating on the foil that does. When this film is superheated by microwave energy (same way it is on tar roads) the coating releases **oxygen** on the surface engorging the material to burn even hotter. This phenomenon is known as [**“field-activated combustion synthesis.”**](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0925838800014584) This ignition is the same type of plasma used to ignite a Duraflame log!!! The self-sustaining oxygen fuels the flames as had been seen with the ceramics roof tiles and the metals used to build — materials that do not burn in a normal forest fire. Bio-physics 101. "_

As well as aluminum reflecting UV, I've been wondering if, in terms of where melanin is in the pic, whether flooding oxygen in the blood would provide more "self sustaining oxygen" causing more damage/destruction to melanin containing aluminum that's exposed to microwave radiation.

I've been very cautious about flooding my system with highly oxygenated water, just using small amounts regularly along with silicon rich mineral water. Started with spring water and then switched. Trying to find a logical explanation for the intuition.

Using novel analytical technique developed at KI, the team of scientists led by Roman Zubarev found in seal bones twice as much deuterium as in sea water; extra deuterium cannot come from seals’ diet.

 news.ki.se

The collagen and semiconductor angle is interesting. The deuterium accumulation could come from diet if there's a pathway that conserves what is ingested and environmental pressures puts it where needed.

This is about tech semiconductors but could it relate to human semiconductors?

_"Moreover, deuterium and silicon bond together relatively quickly and strongly as a result of the synchronicity between the two elements.

Deuterium has been found to reduce the severity of “hot carrier effects” that diminish semiconductor reliability, while reducing stress-induced leakage currents."_

I don't think currently available tests would tell me what I want to know ..... To be useful to me I would also want to know what's in collagen and bone.

Amongst other things, aluminum is linked to osteoporosis, so exerting environmental pressure (such as distance running) on deuterium depleted or aluminum loaded bones could be a disaster. The general idea about aluminum is that silicon takes it out of the body, but I could also say that aluminum takes silicon out. At a certain point of silicon depletion, if silicon is the thing that takes deuterium into bone under certain environmental pressures (such as say weight bearing movement), the tests might show excess, normal or depleted deuterium in breath, blood or saliva, but it wouldn't show a deuterium deficit or aluminum excess in the structure of the bones and collagen semi conductors.

### [The overlooked benefits of hydrogen-producing bacteria](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9979208/#:~:text=According%20to%20a%20metagenomics%20survey,the%20other%2041%25%20are%20Bacteroides.)

Intestinal bacteria can be classified into “beneficial bacteria” and “harmful bacteria.” However, it is difficult to explain the mechanisms that make “beneficial bacteria” truly beneficial to human health. ...

 www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

_"Naked mole-rats have a 10-fold longer lifespan than normal rodents and have greater resistance to cancer. It has been reported that Bacteroides and Firmicutes predominate the intestinal bacteria of these mice. It is possible that hydrogen produced by Bacteroides and Firmicutes is involved in the longevity and cancer resistance of the naked mole-rats."_

what to eat for the healthy brain (answered by AI

**foods that are often recommended for brain health:

Fatty fish**: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids, which are major building blocks of the brain. Omega-3s play a role in memory and improving mood, as well as protecting against cognitive decline12.

**Coffee:** The caffeine and antioxidants in coffee can help support brain health by boosting alertness, improving mood, and sharpening concentration1.

B**lueberries:** These are high in antioxidants, which may delay brain aging and improve memory12.

**Turmeric:** Its active compound curcumin has been shown to cross the blood-brain barrier and has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefits1.

**Broccoli:** High in antioxidants and vitamin K, which is believed to support brain health1.

**Pumpkin Seeds:** Contain antioxidants and a rich source of magnesium, iron, zinc, and copper1.

**Dark Chocolate:** Contains flavonoids, caffeine, and antioxidants12.

**Nuts:** Especially walnuts, which have high levels of DHA, a type of Omega-3 fatty acid1.

**Oranges**: High in vitamin C, which is key for preventing mental decline1.

**Eggs:** Good source of several nutrients tied to brain health, including vitamins B6 and B12, folate, and choline1.

**Green Tea:** Besides caffeine, green tea contains an amino acid called L-theanine, which can cross the blood-brain barrier and increase the activity of the neurotransmitter GABA, which helps reduce anxiety and makes you feel more relaxed1.

boundary that does not allow any form of energy transfer, including heat and work,
**isolated boundary**
\=============

thermodynamics and adiabatic boundaries

In thermodynamics, a system is any three-dimensional region of physical space on which we wish to focus our attention.
The rest of the physical universe constitutes the surroundings of the system.
The boundary is the closed three-dimensional surface that encloses the system and separates it from the surroundings.
The boundary may (and usually does) coincide with real physical surfaces: the interface between two phases, the inner or outer surface of the wall of a flask or other vessel, and so on. Alternatively, part or all of the boundary may be an imagined intangible surface in space, unrelated to any physical structure.
[**An adiabatic boundary is a boundary that does not allow heat transfer**](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Physical_and_Theoretical_Chemistry_Textbook_Maps/DeVoes_Thermodynamics_and_Chemistry/02%3A_Systems_and_Their_Properties/2.01%3A_The_System_Surroundings_and_Boundary)[1](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Physical_and_Theoretical_Chemistry_Textbook_Maps/DeVoes_Thermodynamics_and_Chemistry/02%3A_Systems_and_Their_Properties/2.01%3A_The_System_Surroundings_and_Boundary).
\======
what is the **name of boundary that does not allow any energy transfer**

The type of boundary that does not allow any form of energy transfer, including heat and work,
is called an **isolated boundary**.
This concept is used in thermodynamics to describe an **isolated system**,
which is a system that does not interact with its surroundings in any way.
It’s important to note that perfectly isolated systems are largely theoretical, as in practice, it’s nearly impossible to completely prevent all forms of energy transfer.

### Jack Kruse — 2019-01-18T03:26:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-flotators-outdoor-only.21396/post-260660>

Seeing a floater early = a shit environment or a germ line problem from mom and/or dad.

A floater is a tiny cluster of cells or fleck of protein lodged in the **_vitreous humor_**. This is made from a very special type of collagen with unique piezoelectric and flexoelectric properties which interact with the photoelectric effect of the environment around the globe.

This clear, stable gel, which looks like a raw egg white, supports and fills the rear two-thirds of the eyeball (see pic below). The vitreous provides another accessory pathway for light coming into the eye through the lens. Muller cells are another.

The vitreous connects to the retina, the patch of light-sensitive cells along the back of the eye that captures images and sends them to the brain via the optic nerve.

What you see isn’t actually the floater itself, but the shadow cast it casts onto the retina. Floaters move as your eyes move. Their motion is tied to redox state of the environment around your and in the anterior chamber. They appear to zoom away when you try to look directly at them and drift slowly when your eyes stop moving.

The vitreous slowly shrinks with age as heteroplasmy rises, causing it to become a bit stringy with time. The strands cast shadows on the retina, causing floaters. About one-quarter of people have some vitreous shrinkage with floaters by their 60s; that rises to about two-thirds of 80-year-olds. Floaters also appear more often in people who are nearsighted/myopic, those who have had cataract surgery or a previous eye injury, and those with diabetes. All these people are low redox patients. Although most people tolerate floaters just fine, others feel that floaters affect their vision and disrupt their ability to read. [](http://hhp-blog.s3.amazonaws.com/2013/05/Normal-eye.jpg)

Flashes occur when the vitreous gel bumps, rubs, or tugs against the retina. Like floaters, flashes are generally harmless and require no treatment.

FUTURE CAST OF THIS PROBLEM:

Sometimes floaters and flashes signal a condition that can lead to vision loss.

The shrinking vitreous can tug on the retina and pull away from it. The same thing happens in the brain with age. The brain shrinks and atrophies and pulls away from the dura and you become prone to a bleeding subdural with minimal trauma. I call the shrinking vitreous the subdural phenomena of the eye because the retina is not part of the eye. It is part of the brain.

This event, called a posterior vitreous detachment, is common, and usually doesn’t threaten vision. In about one in six people, a posterior vitreous detachment causes the retina to tear. Fluid from inside the eye can then seep through the tear and separate the retina from the tissues that nourish it. This separation, called retinal detachment, can lead to permanent vision loss. It is becoming more common now in 5G cities in younger people. This is why the geoengineering shows up above. 5G and geoengineering tend to walk hand in hand.

Retinal tears and detachments are painless. Key warning signs include:

-   new onset of floaters and flashes of light in the eye
-   gradual shading of vision from one side (like a curtain being drawn)
-   the rapid decline in sharp, central vision. This occurs when the macula — the area of the retina responsible for central vision — detaches.

Retinal tears can be treated in several ways. We can use cold and lasers. Cold as in CT and lasers = light with large OAM and no flicker. Pinpoints of laser light can be used to fuse the retina to the back wall of the eye via photoablation = Laser photocoagulation. Extreme cold is also used in this condition via a procedure called cryopexy. Cold and lasers really act in the same way. This is a clue to the Black swan how cold and OAM are interrelated in quantum biology!!!

Cold and laser light can also be COMBINED with the injection of a gas bubble into the eye in a procedure called pneumatic retinopexy to repair a detached retina. There are two older operations also used for this called scleral buckling and vitrectomy to reattach a retina. Non-native EMF has a variety of interesting effects to the eye.

### AntonisK — 2025-01-29T11:50:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-flotators-outdoor-only.21396/post-345961>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=260660)
>
> Seeing a floater early = a shit environment or a germ line problem from mom and/or dad.
>
> A floater is a tiny cluster of cells or fleck of protein lodged in the **_vitreous humor_**. This is made from a very special type of collagen with unique piezoelectric and flexoelectric properties which interact with the photoelectric effect of the environment around the globe.
>
> This clear, stable gel, which looks like a raw egg white, supports and fills the rear two-thirds of the eyeball (see pic below). The vitreous provides another accessory pathway for light coming into the eye through the lens. Muller cells are another.
>
> The vitreous connects to the retina, the patch of light-sensitive cells along the back of the eye that captures images and sends them to the brain via the optic nerve.
>
> What you see isn’t actually the floater itself, but the shadow cast it casts onto the retina. Floaters move as your eyes move. Their motion is tied to redox state of the environment around your and in the anterior chamber. They appear to zoom away when you try to look directly at them and drift slowly when your eyes stop moving.
>
> The vitreous slowly shrinks with age as heteroplasmy rises, causing it to become a bit stringy with time. The strands cast shadows on the retina, causing floaters. About one-quarter of people have some vitreous shrinkage with floaters by their 60s; that rises to about two-thirds of 80-year-olds. Floaters also appear more often in people who are nearsighted/myopic, those who have had cataract surgery or a previous eye injury, and those with diabetes. All these people are low redox patients. Although most people tolerate floaters just fine, others feel that floaters affect their vision and disrupt their ability to read. [](http://hhp-blog.s3.amazonaws.com/2013/05/Normal-eye.jpg)
>
> Flashes occur when the vitreous gel bumps, rubs, or tugs against the retina. Like floaters, flashes are generally harmless and require no treatment.
>
> FUTURE CAST OF THIS PROBLEM:
>
> Sometimes floaters and flashes signal a condition that can lead to vision loss.
>
> The shrinking vitreous can tug on the retina and pull away from it. The same thing happens in the brain with age. The brain shrinks and atrophies and pulls away from the dura and you become prone to a bleeding subdural with minimal trauma. I call the shrinking vitreous the subdural phenomena of the eye because the retina is not part of the eye. It is part of the brain.
>
> This event, called a posterior vitreous detachment, is common, and usually doesn’t threaten vision. In about one in six people, a posterior vitreous detachment causes the retina to tear. Fluid from inside the eye can then seep through the tear and separate the retina from the tissues that nourish it. This separation, called retinal detachment, can lead to permanent vision loss. It is becoming more common now in 5G cities in younger people. This is why the geoengineering shows up above. 5G and geoengineering tend to walk hand in hand.
>
> Retinal tears and detachments are painless. Key warning signs include:
>
>
> -   new onset of floaters and flashes of light in the eye
> -   gradual shading of vision from one side (like a curtain being drawn)
> -   the rapid decline in sharp, central vision. This occurs when the macula — the area of the retina responsible for central vision — detaches.
>
> Retinal tears can be treated in several ways. We can use cold and lasers. Cold as in CT and lasers = light with large OAM and no flicker. Pinpoints of laser light can be used to fuse the retina to the back wall of the eye via photoablation = Laser photocoagulation. Extreme cold is also used in this condition via a procedure called cryopexy. Cold and lasers really act in the same way. This is a clue to the Black swan how cold and OAM are interrelated in quantum biology!!!
>
> Cold and laser light can also be COMBINED with the injection of a gas bubble into the eye in a procedure called pneumatic retinopexy to repair a detached retina. There are two older operations also used for this called scleral buckling and vitrectomy to reattach a retina. Non-native EMF has a variety of interesting effects to the eye.

I've had this same issue (partial vitreous detachment that was ready to tear the retina) and treated it with laser.
My question is, would this create any implication with the POMC pathway just like Lasik or similar?

### Sienna — 2018-05-29T01:50:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/siennas-optimal-journal-part-2.21402/post-242228>

Thought it might be okay to start a new journal, seem to be struggling to get Jack's attention to re-open my original optimal journal after updating my avatar. Below is the link to my old one for anyone new to my journey:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/siennas-optimal-journal.20438/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/siennas-optimal-journal.20438/)

I've bought 96 bottles of Qlarivia 25ppm water, they said it will get here by 30th May, however, I'm very skeptical as I've heard some aussies have waited a lot longer for theirs to arrive. It turned out cheaper than I expected though. Granted, it's not a lot but I don't drink a lot of water these days probably because of the high-fat diet; this could easily last me 3 months or more. Very interested to see how I go on it.

Somehow, my skin has gotten more irritated and I've gained more weight since starting my mitochondriac journey; it is frustrating to say the least. I'm really at my wit's end in figuring out what might be going on. Again, main hints I have are in the thermography screenshot, some major inflammation going on in my head and throat. So for now, I'm just trying to resolve my dental issues and see where that takes me and of course, getting as much sunlight as I can as winter comes closer. I've added oil pulling and painting iodine on my gums to the protocol. I'm also experimenting with brushing my teeth with emu oil + essential oils. I'm not 2 months yet into my protocol and from my readings, gingivitis/periodontitis takes time to completely resolve even when being absolutely diligent with the protocol, anywhere from 6 months to a year. However, you should be noticing improvements by the 3-month mark. I'm hoping that DDW will have some sort of action, even if very indirect, on helping this.

I do wonder though if my jaw misalignment is playing a significant role in all this. I'm hoping not because that is definitely not easy nor quick to fix, mainly because it is incredibly hard to find someone who can treat it without involving major surgery. I'm seeing an orthodontist tomorrow who might be able to treat it through with a new appliance. I hope I am a candidate for this treatment as the alternative is jaw surgery. I can't justify undergoing such an expensive, permanent and risky ordeal, I find it difficult to believe that getting healthy, living teeth removed and jaws broken would help my particular case. Fingers crossed this orthodontist aligns with my desired outcomes for the treatment.

# Thread: Usefulness of St. John's Wort induced photosensitivity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/usefulness-of-st-johns-wort-induced-photosensitivity.21410/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### YellowEmperorsNewElixir — 2018-05-29T23:49:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/usefulness-of-st-johns-wort-induced-photosensitivity.21410/post-242321>

_Hypericin phototoxicity induces different modes of cell death in melanoma and human skin cells | Request PDF_. Available from: [https://www.researchgate.net/public...f\_cell\_death\_in\_melanoma\_and\_human\_skin\_cells](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/5508885_Hypericin_phototoxicity_induces_different_modes_of_cell_death_in_melanoma_and_human_skin_cells) \[accessed May 29 2018\].

STUDY CITED:
Hypericin, the major component of St. John's Wort, absorbs light in the UV and visible ranges whereupon it becomes phototoxic through the production of reactive oxygen species. Although photodynamic mechanisms (i.e. through endogenous photosensitizers) play a role in UVA phototherapy for the treatment of skin disorders such as eczema and psoriasis, photodynamic therapy employing exogenous photosensitizers are currently being used only for the treatment of certain forms of non-melanoma skin cancers and actinic keratoses. There are few reports however on its use in treating melanomas. This in vitro study analyses the phototoxic effect of UVA (400-315 nm) - activated hypericin in human pigmented and unpigmented melanomas and immortalised keratinocytes and melanocytes. We show that neither hypericin exposure nor UV irradiation alone reduces cell viability. We show that an exposure to 1 microM UVA-activated hypericin does not bring about cell death, while 3 microM activated hypericin induces a necrotic mode of cell death in pigmented melanoma cells and melanocytes and an apoptotic mode of cell death in non-pigmented melanoma cells and keratinocytes. **_We hypothesis that the necrotic mode of cell death in the pigmented cells is possibly related to the presence of melanin-containing melanosomes in these cells and that the hypericin-induced increase in reactive oxygen species leads to an increase in permeability of melanosomes. This would result in toxic melanin precursors (of an indolic and phenolic nature) leaking into the cytoplasm which in turn leads to cell death. Hypericin localisation in the endoplasmic reticulum in these cells shown by fluorescent microscopy, further support a disruption in cellular processing and induction of cell death.\*_** In contrast, this study shows that cells that do not contain melanosomes (non-pigmented melanoma cells and keratinocytes) die by apoptosis. Further, using a mitochondrial-specific fluorescent dye, we show that intracellular accumulation of hypericin induces a mitochondrial-associated caspase-dependent apoptotic mode of cell death. This work suggests that **_UVA is effective in activating hypericin\*_** and that this phototoxicity may be considered as treatment option in some cases of lentigo maligna or lentigo maligna melanoma that are too large for surgical resection.
**
_\* Emphasis mine_**

Here is a study detailing the mechanism by which hypericin induces what they are calling "phototoxicity". However, this is surely to be a dose-dependent relationship. Could it be possible that lower doses of St. John's Wort could upregulate mitochondrial or melanin sensitivity in a way that is beneficial? What about apoptosis? Could this be effectively integrated for those engaging in hybrid tanning?

Calling all you quantum biologists. Thanks in advance.

### lakers — 2018-07-26T21:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/usefulness-of-st-johns-wort-induced-photosensitivity.21410/post-246768>

Bumping this, should one be weary of taking this supplement?

Hey folks,

For those who are eating Ketogenic and mostly carnivorous, do you take any additional measures to ensure sufficient electrolyte intake?

I'm currently adding salt to my food but not sure whether additional electrolytes may be required, e.g. magnesium/potassium?

Any thoughts and experiences would be of interest.

Thanks,

Phil

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=242408)
>
> No. I just add liberal salt pepper and turmeric.

Nice one Jack.

Do you know if potassium is made in the body from sodium via cold fusion (as per the description below), or is this not the case?

_Cells have an organelle that increases the voltage and serves like capacitor. This organelle is called the prismatic body. So if the cellular brain which is within the cellular membrane called the receptor does not find toxic elements within the extracellular liquid, it gives the order for potassium production. The prismatic body zaps sodium with high voltage, excites it and bonds it with an atom of oxygen. 11 electrons of sodium bond with 8 electrons of oxygen and 19 electron potassium is created. As more and more potassium is being produced, the intracellular pressure is rising and under that pressure toxic elements including heavy metals will be expelled from the cell. As particles and water are leaving the cell under the pressure, the cell will lose potassium as well. This is how potassium ends up in the blood circulation._

you may want to make sense of this, and formulate question that Jack may be willing to address.

I always think that we need everything (in just right amount).

My question always is about EPA, DHA, AA.
And their balance.
Excess EPA and DHA kills AA.

People who actually test their Fatty Acids very often find to have it high EPA and also often DHA high.
High=over laboratory range.
Low=below laboratory range
Note, Genova diagnostics, does not have high levels for the two.

People who get sick with neolithic diseases (and get tested) have DHA and specially EPA exorbitantly high.

The culprit seem to be excess of EPA. (in effort to go easy on DHA I do not mention it).
But everybody and his brother want more EPA.
To the point that new (I think prescription) medicine is advertised on my TV bragging that it is pure EPA (and they proudly brag about it).
\-----
Seafood ALWAYS have excess of EPA, but conversation is always about DHA.

A week or so ago,
I found analysis on oysters.
Looks like this is the only seafood (that I know off) that have much more DHA over less EPA.
Additionally,
oysters have large variety of phospholipids that are part of our membranes (including inner mitochondrial membrane that is different than all other.
So,
that could explain why [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) is so hot on oysters (and be understandable to me).

[@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/)
It's been 2 years that I collect my data and that's the reason that till now I haven't post my journal because I want to have a point of reference.
On the other hand I try to keep my testing to minimum mainly because if you get in that rabbit hole there is a possibility never get out,I'm trying to understand from the big things ( vit d,dhea,bun/creat,hs-crp ,glucose,HbA1c) the impact my environment has to my body.
Furthermore you know it better than me usually when you start a conversation with a doc about the things we discuss here in the forum and the approach in the blogs and webinars probably you get something of these
Recently I started (4 months ago) supplementing with MB ( 8 drops/day) till now I can attest an increased appetite,increased ability to stay in the sun,a serious decrease in the consumption of water that I drink and a serious increase in my BUN/creat ratio from 17 to 27.
Also I started a thread about how safe is 23&me testing and if there are other ways to obtain the data so to have deeper understanding on how ones function as I don,t consider very wise to give a sample of my DNA in any company. I would love to hear your view on the topic here or in the thread.

# Thread: Help re sun exposure
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-re-sun-exposure.21419/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Steph Roch — 2018-06-01T23:27:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-re-sun-exposure.21419/post-242525>

How can I explain concisely to a geneticist who insists that sun exposure is detrimental to health that it is essential for well-being? I explained some of what Jack has been saying about the necessity of light on skin, posted Dr. Wunsch's YouTube video entitled "Why the Sun is Necessary for Optimal Health" and her response is:

"No. I am a geneticist, and UV radiation causes mutations in your DNA. The melanin your body produces provides a small measure of protection from this, and your body has repair mechanisms to fix the mutations. But sometimes it doesn't, or fixes them badly. Every time you get more sun exposure, you increase the number of times that one of those mistakes happens, and if that mistake is in a cell cycle gene it can cause uncontrolled cell growth, which is cancer. It's like driving without wearing a seatbelt - knowing someone who managed to do that for years and by chance not have an accident doesn't change that wearing a seatbelt is still a really good idea."

Any help is greatly appreciated. Her analogy that sun exposure is like driving without a seatbelt seems utterly ridiculous to me. I am still in the process of learning myself. Thank you.

Update: This is what I replied to her from my knowledge. I hope that I got it all right:

"I really appreciate your comments. I am not trying to be a pain here but let me explain myself. We need sun exposure, and much more than we currently get. The sun (full spectrum) regulates pretty much all of the hormones in our body. It allows us to produce Vitamin D, which is oncoprotective. We need the sun for energy production in our mitochondria. Light basically charges our water battery in our cells, which means it regulates metabolism. All living organisms are based on energy from light that is stored in water. Sunlight induces biochemical reactions in our body. Vitamin D is synthesized under the influence of UV light. The process of photolysis is necessary to properly break down hormones such as adrenaline, testosterone, estrogen, growth hormone etc. in our bloodstream. Each of these hormones have absorption bands in the range of the solar spectrum. UV radiation is therefore essential to help regulate our endocrine system. It is a major circadian and seasonal stimulus. We need light to stimulate the SNC (central body clock) to synchronize all other cellular body clocks in our system. Vitamin D regulates organ function as well as melatonin. Your analogy of the seat belt does not make sense here. The sun clearly provides numerous essential benefits to our body. We need to build up our tolerance, based on seasonal shifts, but staying out of the sun is not going to help us steer clear of cancer. It would be like saying that we shouldn't breathe because we would be breathing in oxygen. Oxygen is the precursor molecule for Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) or free radical production. These are often the starting points for cancer activation in a cell. Should we then advise against oxygen? I think not. Sorry, but I am trying to understand exactly what you are saying...."

### drezy — 2018-06-02T01:05:34+0300

### Lahelada — 2018-06-02T06:12:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-re-sun-exposure.21419/post-242553>

A word to the wise,when talking to doctors arguing "what" is a losing battle. You can show as many papers for as against. She is not incorrect according to what she has read. Ask "WHY " You could ask why all cause mortality is lower with sun exposure, why Dr Hollwich found what he found, why we advise sun avoidance if we know that melatonin is made in the eye, why is everyone talking about melanin but not urocanic acid, why noone is talking about the lack of sulfation of cholesterol to make vit D ,why a mitochondria out of a cancerous cell switches on the oncogenic genes in a previously healthy cell when transplanted Do not make this a case of one upmanship though. Your doc friend as misguided as she seems to be in our eyes is trying to help.Keep communication open.

### JanSz — 2018-06-02T15:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-re-sun-exposure.21419/post-242555>

Your perspective on people comes from the cage you were held captive in.
Wise folks understand **there no facts**, only interpretations.
This is why one person's "crazy" is another person's reality.
This makes intuition and insanity relative.
It depends on "who" has "who" locked in 'what' cage.

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...read-here-at-jackkruse-com.17829/#post-196437](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/your-first-post-to-read-here-at-jackkruse-com.17829/#post-196437)

### karenr — 2018-06-02T16:06:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-re-sun-exposure.21419/post-242556>

It can be very tempting to engage in ego-driven discussions that aren’t useful. Even when topics of little consequence are on the table, I still often feel that drive to assert that I'm right. Now I'll step back and look at my motivation for that. And sometimes hold back from reacting to the other person right away rather than jumping in with my assertion. Because in that moment of choosing not to go along with the knee jerk reaction, I feel a sense of freedom. Ahh, not having to control everything, what a relief! Being right falls away, and being natural replaces it.

# Thread: Optimized Life Journey
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 62
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jill A. — 2018-06-02T05:58:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-242552>

Time to begin documenting this journey. It feels as if it is becoming an epic adventure into saving my own life.

About 5 mos to get here since joining. Like many Im sure I saw the truth of all of this but it has taken me time to change the way I think. Time to even feel up to the take off. Small steps.
I have known on some level that supplements and diet was not enough.
It has been the sun that has begun to revive me. I was stumbling around and decided to do just that one thing. The sun. Circadian reset. Now I cant get enough of the sun. The impact has been enough that I am feeling strong enough to employ the discipline of eating Paleo Rx.
DHA is important.
I am eating seafood. Oysters. Delicious.
Feet in the ocean and on the ground.
All blue light out of the house. I have grown to hate it. It feels so unnatural to me.
Drinking glacier water.
Looking at Quantlet.

I bought a Joovv. I enjoy its light. Need to understand it better. How do I use it to support me?
Looking at Magnetico.

I have begun testing. ordered some basics. not Great results again.
I have Epi-PaleoRx. Are the recommended tests in there still the most relevant or have there been changes written somewhere?

I feel I have made the shift from thinking about my healing being so much about food and more about my connection to my environment.
I have become a beachcomber. I am reading tides. I know when treasure arrives. I walk in the water. Working on going all the way in.
I have always loved the cold. But after some experimentation in the beginning I found I was not strong enough to handle it. It was last winter. I got chilled. I was too sick. Stronger now.
I believe I may be in the water all the way by the end of June.
Yesterday I wanted to get naked so bad. The sun was perfect. No chemtrails. Honestly, this has been one of the most moving times of my life. I am 64. I remember the sun. We werent allowed home till it went down.
Today I was able to get a good part of my breasts into the sun and also my upper back and neck. After doing a work session today I feel pretty pain free tonite.
The sun truly is a miracle. I have been pondering a life of most of the day spent in it. I will drop that one off in the Quantum field of infinite possibilities.

I am reading blogs and posts in the forum and listening to many you tube vids of Jack. Relistening. I pick up another piece each time I do. It all makes sense to me and feels right.

Today I was at the beach after an oyster, cod and coconut oil breakfast. I am struggling to stay away from the carbs because I actually have been getting dizzy and feel strange as my body tries to deal with the change. I got pretty shaky and did not feel well. It scares me. Perhaps I should add more fat. I will try tomorrow.
My tests show borderline Type 2, liver enzymes twice what they should be and Im surely inflamed. I am told my tests so far show I am on the road to heart disease.

I love the idea of not having to eat, like the trees. Jack spoke about it in an interview. I am so f@$#&^% sick of thinking about food. I was a bodybuilder in my twenties. Insane lifestyle and very regimented eating and lifestyle.

Something is wrong with me. I intend to understand it. I have been taking in a lot of material here but I am not good at putting it all together yet. my brain has begun to clear some from what Im doing so far. still not tracking like I should be however.

.Broke down nov 2015. c1-c2 disc compression, mild, stenosis at c4/5 ... severe pain and loss of feeling in my right outer hand, last 2 fingers. R diaphragm collapsed. couldnt work.
I have stomach aches ... for a long time now off and on.
Obesity ... I now weigh 230lbs. I have been 130lb competitive BB. Weight has gone up and down all my life.
I have not recovered fully from this crash but am able to work some.
Ive had sleep problems. It is correcting with circadian reset.
Most recently I have noticed my skin suddenly became extremely dry all over.
I was growing new skin tags on my face and chest.
Energy is up and down and if I work too much I go into pretty bad pain all over.
Ibuprofen abuse.
Overworking.Too much computer.No sunlight at all for a long time.
I feel a strange stiffness on my feet and calves,
High sex drive
I have not been able to come back. Its why I found Jack. I tried to pull myself out. I knew I needed something different.
No illness in my mother except Dementia 4 years ago.
Father died of pancreatic cancer and liver failure. Alchoholic.
All family illness's ... cancer, heart disease, diabetes, alchohol, some mental illness.

I have not been well my whole life. Never actually sick but just short periods of time when I felt like I was firing all cylinders. Ive been sick for a long time.

using Colostrum
vitC
D3
Magnesium
Progesterone cream
Estriol cream

### Jill A. — 2018-06-02T21:30:54+0300

I notice that since being in the sun everyday the skin tags on my face are diminished somewhat.
Wondering what the very dry skin, orange peeling affect is caused by. I will continue to research.
But overall my skin looks better.
I am developing a solar callous.
Up to my waist in the ocean. Hot sun, cold water.

I am controlling my mornings now. Trying to push start time to 10am as much as possible.

Tossed all underwire bras. Perhaps that is why my diaphragm collapsed. Pain in ribs.
The metal and my biology makes me an antennae.

Added more fat to breakfast. Still dizzy later on. Shaking. Wow. Metabolism is delicate right now. I feel inflamed in my gut... Up high

My Dr actually told me that disc compression and aches and gut problems were all normal part of aging.
In this environment it is.

### Jill A. — 2018-06-03T07:41:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-242582>

6\. Your BUN/creatine ratios are critical to follow in these battery of tests. You can layer on other tests like your urine-specific gravity, urinary and serum osmolarity as well to gain a greater understanding of where you are. When it is high, it means your proteins and mitochondria do not have reverse water micelles around them to transfer energy to and fro to allow for 100{a7b724a0454d92c70890dedf5ec22a026af4df067c7b55aa6009b4d34d5da3c6} energy efficiency. This ruins the zero entropy systems and causes you to have to make more ATP to open water-binding sites on protein. When ATP generation is low, you will crave carbs to offset this loss. That is also a cue your redox potential is off. We can see this in advanced cardiac testing using HRV/CAC scores.

Saving this. Piecing it together.

eat one time a day or less
eat small portions**

and you will have really good deal going.
plain water=tap water or cheap RO water

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-04T13:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-242677>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=242635)
>
> Hang in there. There are others with your experience and they will eventually come out of the woodworks.

> [Jill A. said:](/goto/post?id=242552)
>
> Today I was at the beach after an oyster, cod and coconut oil breakfast. I am struggling to stay away from the carbs because I actually have been getting dizzy and feel strange as my body tries to deal with the change. I got pretty shaky and did not feel well. It scares me. Perhaps I should add more fat. I will try tomorrow.

I found to start with that I needed a bigger breakfast of eggs cooked in butter lard or ghee, and fatty meat with a small amount of carbs. If they were seasonal and not eaten to excess, their glycemic index was of less importance, the protein and fat make the glycemic index of the meal as a whole much lower. I ate the protein and fat first, and then topped up with seasonal carbs. I would have lamb or pork chops, liver onions and bacon, lots of eggs ....seasonal roots and greens and I'd fill up, I could add a seafood starter ... As I understand it the first meal after you break a fast the energy goes to the peripheral tissues in preference to the fat cells. (I think there's maybe an evolutionary advantage for a hungry animal to have its first meal directed differently, so that it has the energy in the peripheral tissues to go after its next meal, or be active in its environment). Eaten within half hour of sunrise to help set the gut and liver circadian rhythm, and if I ate enough I didn't think about food for 4 or 5 hours, no shaking etc, and good energy levels.

> [Jill A. said:](/goto/post?id=242624)
>
> **_
> **_**_**_**_My Mother has Dementia. 90. No meds her whole life but I have watched her slide into this and now she is so frail. It didnt make sense to me. I understand so much more what has happened to her. I can track it. I do not intend to go out that way._**_**_**_**
> _**

I lost my mother to AD last year, and like your mum, no meds. My maternal grandmother also had dementia. I not only don't want to go out that way, but I don't want my kids to have to watch me go out that way, or accept that their own dementia fate is destined, or put their own lives on hold for years like both my mother did for her mother, or I did for her. The last couple of years of her life were a horrendous stress, and at best I was treading water but if I'd tried to eat once a day at that point, I would have made things worse, or sunk. My mother was my main stress but it could have been any other combination of stresses.

Jan posted

_"Carbs have to be divided into number of categories.
but when your weight is 230# and you are aiming at 130#
**you must stay out of high glycemic index carbs
eat one time a day or less
eat small portions**

**2-3x/day step on digital scale"**_

I have an alternative viewpoint.

If I eat one small meal a day, or less, I'm setting myself up for a self imposed eating disorder. Been there, done that, went from the large to the extra large t shirt..... There's a huge difference between a man's results of eating once a day to lose weight, and a woman. Women are wired differently when it comes to the environment, because of puberty, conception, pregnancy, childbirth and breastfeeding. Ignoring hunger cues chronically when I can't fat burn excess body fat, for whatever reason, is starvation, which I'm programmed to avoid. The stress of what my brain sees as undereating means I am more likely to store the meagre rations as fat, probably around my liver. My stress response to true or self imposed starvation, will be different to a man.

I'm not aiming for X# on the scale, I'm aiming to heal, and at 60 I can't afford to get it wrong. My mother kept herself thin all her adult life, regularly weighing herself and keeping herself at her "ideal" weight, but no thought of body composition. As she aged she lost so much SQ fat that the veins on her arms became prominent, that was the time when her AD escalated big time. SQ fat = a store of stem cells. I will happily keep a few extra pounds of subcutaneous fat, if it stops my brain degenerating. We burn fat at night, which is also when we regenerate. Deliberately starving myself during the day and using up stored stem cells and possibly losing muscle mass when I'm not in a regenerating phase, or I'm not accessing it at night properly, seems counterintuitive. If its genuine hunger or hunger caused by inflammation, I'm listening to the signals. I do CT to reduce inflammation, and I try not to eat within 6 hours of midnight, or after sunset, whichever comes first seasonally, and my sleep is a top priority.

I do intermittent fasting now, with a 6 hour eating window, to satiation, no small portions, but it was a progression until it was natural, effortless i.e. getting to a point where I can burn my own stored body fat. My 6 hour window satisfies my female programing if I've slept well. With a strong circadian rhythm, if I'm living in the cold lane of winter, or get enough summer sun, my stress levels are low and my sleep is good, I don't need as much food, and I think about it less. 3 or 4 days of rubbish weather or a bad night's sleep, and I can be back to 3 meals. It's still baby steps, and at 60 my progress is slow, but my brain and heart take priority. Gone are the days where I'm constantly thinking about food, or stepping on the scales 2 or 3 times a day... Hell I can lose "weight" during the day if I let myself become dehydrated or have a good dump, but it doesn't mean I'm improving ....

> If I understand correctly the goal should be circadian/Leptin reset using the sun. Grounding, CT and seafood, lots of DHA.
> I will try the supports you suggest. I need it all I think.
> Putting it together this week.
> Won't the iodine affect my thyroid along with better cortixol?

From the leptin blogs, intermittent fasting is contra indicated if someone is leptin resistant.
Exogenous T3 and your skin breaks out ..... sun on the gut and it feels better ... What is that telling you.

Whether its bad lab results or low temp, the question is ....why? What is the context? I don't do labs (don't need them to tell me when I feel like shit), I came to see low temp as my body reacting to the environment it faced. I was keeping excess fat until the environment changed, storing my stem cells until my environment improved. Being obese was a protective mechanism, delaying cancer and neurodegeneration. The other thing I came to understand from the blogs is that it is the brain that controls the thyroid. Fix that (circadian rhythm, light water magnetism, regaining leptin sensitivity, etc etc) and the thyroid has a chance to respond more naturally, in line with the season, and use my stem cells to advantage, rather than waste them to get to an "ideal" weight as fast as possible. Taking exogenous thyroid hormones or taking too much iodine could interfere with that, and circadian signalling. By getting my iodine from the epi paleo/leptin prescription route, and using a good sea salt, I'm getting it packaged with other synergistic proteins and nutrients, and my body is better able to read the signals, and tells me when to stop.

I'm not saying my understanding is completely right - but with dementia in my maternal line, its not a risk I'd take when the blog teachings are that if we are meant to make it, don't take it exogenously, its sun-plement, not supplement. I can't supplement my way out of a bad environment, or a good environment I'm not taking advantage of. Even if the ranges are similar, on an informational level, labs as a result of tinkering around with supplements are not the same as the same results in response to accessing the natural environment.

### Sue-UK — 2018-06-06T13:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-242827>

> [Jill A. said:](/goto/post?id=242820)
>
> I reread what you wrote and I wanted to say I noticed more closely your intuitive sense of what you need along with the application of the practices and processes to your symptoms.. There is an intuition to all of this.
>
> Seeing my mother this way is tough. In all my reading I can track her progression too. I see similarities. I see the similarities all over the nursing home. It scared the crap out of me.

It scared the crap out of me too, but once I got past that it scared the fight into me. My FIL was in a nursing home for about 10 months, (end stage CHF and vascular dementia and finally a brain tumor) and the month after he died, my mother moved in. Watching their decline and the differences in other residents was an eye opener. From my observation, the thinnest, frailer residents coming to live there had worse cognitive function. If they were able to walk they would often pace up and down the corridor, as if attempting to generate cognitive function from walking. The ones with more fat mass, even if they were wheelchair bound or diabetic etc, seemed to have better cognitive function. Having better cognitive function, they were able to make use of the social opportunities, which in turn contributed to better cognitive function. It wasn't always so cut and dried, someone who had been living alone and been lonely might improve cognitive function, even if seemingly frail. My FIL was an unsocial, difficult man, and my mother's fear of social interaction stemmed from her being deaf. It was the same nnemf hellhole for all the residents, but it didn't affect everyone equally or at the same rate.

One of the interesting residents was a woman on respite whilst her husband was in hospital. She had no womanly curves left, had her hair cut and dressed in a way that people thought she was a man. She started declining rapidly, a constant wanderer, and my mother and other female residents became frightened by her, particularly if they thought it was a man rifling through their personal belongings, or walked in on them when they were getting dressed. Her husband's condition meant she had to be moved to a specialist dementia unit, and in the few days before she went, the staff relented about discouraging smoking and one of them would take her outside before breakfast for a cigarette. She spent the morning calming sitting during the social activity on offer, no wandering …… The staff were surprised at the improvement, but thanks to my membership here, I wasn't ….

### Jill A. — 2018-06-07T19:50:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-242947>

So feeling pretty good this morning.
I started the HCL and a Probiotic thats very good.
Also, I read in the Forum somewhere that someone Made a morning drink of high quality Cacao, grassfed butter, Ghee, Coconut oil and a little Stevia.
Everything has been upsetting my stomach and I have been grumpy and shaky at times. I have tried this for 3 mornings and 2 afternoons.
Wow. It does not bother my stomach and I am so much more calm. Appetite very noticeably stabilized. Mood more stable. Its holding me for hours. Added some Oysters this morning to breakfast. Stomach still calm

Not really measuring nutrient amounts. I am staying away from that due to the anxiety I have around it still.
Being a BodyBuilder can be akin to a mental illness when you are forcing your body to do things its not meant to do. Like 12% BF for a female. That alone, for an extended time period can cause all kinds of weird behavior and depression. I would have a meltdown if I couldnt see the vein running down my stomach.
It is lonely too. Eating disorders, depression and metabolic syndrome is rampant among female BB. While it has been years and years I still fall into that obsessive bent when it comes to food and nutrients and scales etc. Do not want to go there with this.

I notice I am thinking more clearly, making better decisions, holding my ground better.
I am chasing the sun like a wild woman. I am turning a golden brown. My house if full of shells and rocks and driftwood. I have Redwood branches all over from the woods. I ordered a Geode.

I have renewed my relationship with the ocean. I remember it well. I lived 2 blocks from the beach, sand dunes as a teen. I surfed .... I was. there in it everyday. It was a healthy time.
Here is where I am now. Putting thoughts of a life here into the Quantum field. There are an infinite number of ways it could happen. I will not place limits on how it shows up.
When I broke and the usual stuff was not propping me up I began to let go of old paradigms. I asked.

The way showed up.
Feeling deep gratitude. I'm gonna be ok.

### Jill A. — 2018-07-23T07:31:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-246497>

It has been awhile. Processing.
I admit I was excited but something pulled me back and away from the group. This happens in personal life regularly. I no longer beat myself up for doing this. I am usually internalizing and efforting to put into action what I have learned . I require solitude. I have called it Critical Care BECAUSE IT IS!
I am surrounded lately by evidence of the effects of the environment and I see it so differently now.
At the rest home my mother struggles with Dementia. At 90 she is extremely frail but she is tough in there still. 73 yr old woman next to her dying of brain cancer. Working with hospice. The other is disconnected.
My neice, it was just reported has Vitiligo. Her Mother Innerstichalitis and feels like she is being choked sometimes, pain in her body. My brother hypothyroid and severe back spasms. Now kind of stoops. at 6/5 it looks heavy.
In their 40s and 50s it seems too early.
My son with Downs Syndrome, alopecia, exema, Toe Fungus, stigmstism overweight, etc.
And just thinking my other brother 12 years ago broke both fibia and Tibia, comound, He is now 70 and hia body has become painful and hard to live in. He was doing 15 mph. I wonder about that.
Me and my "personal 911"
Its a wonder we are all still cheerful! Ha!
How did Jack put it " when deviance becomes normalized" we just accept it.

My 34 year old son was diagnosed with schitzoprenia this week. What we thought was a drug problem was an ill person possibly self medicating as is common. With terrible results of course.
But I see other things involved now. A computer computer wizard, up all night on computer or doing security work. I understand why he chose it. He did not have to interact with people so much. Isolating. Very low Dopamine. Completely wrecked Circadian and other clocks.
In blue light all night. Little sun. For years. He is wrecked.

I am sensing a whisper of understanding protons and electrons, condensing, autophagy and our magnetic heart.
I am finding all of this information and awakening a great relief. It feels like freedom. I am in nature atleast 3 days per week, Most of the day in warm penetrating sun. My body loves it. I am starved. I recognize it now.
I did not follow through with testing and I have stopped most supplements. I think I had to go through a kind of reintroduction to myself in a natural state. Messed up. I decided to just trust the basics. I know this from a couple disciplines I have practiced.
Sun, light, water, magnetism, CT and I have been working on my daily DHA.
When I reaally started taking the sun and the ocean 3 to 4 days a week and laying in creeks with the sun shining on me, real change began.
I do not have the execution completely down . I am progressing.

Progress and challenge, I am very uncomfortable on many levels right now. I really see how my brain has not been working.

I f@#$ Love the sun. It is medicine. Im so brown and my house if full of all kinds of shells.
Developing a relationship with oysters. Dug this out of the mud on an old Oyster farm. Its huge. I have become a beach bum.

Will be listening to "skin in the game" tonite
My mind is clearing and my judgements about my life and how I should live are altering.
I am going to find out my Haplotype then have an appt with Dr Jack. Anyone else have a problem with some agency having your dna in a big data base? Alternatives?

### Jill A. — 2018-07-30T04:48:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimized-life-journey.21420/post-246953>

I understand this post below much better. Thanks Sue. I relate to it.

I am clearer about what I have been doing wrong and why. Interesting is that I have struggled with metabolic syndrome forever after bodybuilding days. I had a piece of the answer but it did not accurately point to the causes.
Nov 2016 I broke badly. Overnight. Literally. One day. For a long time I tried all my old strategies to get moving again. They did not work. I knew I was in a different kind of trouble.
Im beginning to be more consistent in my new disciplines with slips but this is not buying a new supplement or a new gimmick. It is a life change. I am changing my thinking. The more I practice and study execution improves. Immersion is necessary I think.
My eating is coming online at last.

For the last year I have felt like I am wearing socks. Its a tightness in the feet. Not painful but annoying. I thought it was because of my work. In my bare feet for many years doing it and very tight calves. I push through it as is my MO. So I looked at it as a symptom this week and researched it.
It is a symptom of Neuropathy.
Crap.
The win here is that I paid attention to it. There is a lot I have not been paying attention to. In many ways.

So then as I lay in a creek today letting the cool water ease the chronic pain in my right side I decided to research it also. Again I thought it was part of the "work injury" like the foot thing. In the middle of the forest I took out my phone and looked it up.
It could be liver pain. My enzyme output is 2x what it should be. It could be my liver.
It is almost 2 years since my crash. I have rested and babied my body a lot. Taking months off. The pain does not go away. I do have times when it is better but I feel its potential for flare up. I have a stomach ache everyday to some degree again with some very good days .
When I reach up into my R diaphragm it is very very tender. Pain at T-12, T-8 and disc compression at C1/2 with mild foramenal stenosis at C-4/5. Whole right side of my body is unstable. I had 18 clients a week now down to 5 with week long breaks. Sometimes I cant even do that. Good thing I am in demand.
My body aches. There is a heaviness in my legs and I lose power. I think I am inflamed. It is alarming.
The time in nature has brought me face to face with just how long I have been experiencing the slow crash and pushing through it all.

This last week I got 4 days with 3 to 5 hours of sun almost naked and 3 I was able to sun my breasts. I lay in a creek against the earth for hours, cold water, hot sun, magnetism from the earth. Sunrise and sunset everyday. The pain in my side got pretty bad for a few days. I thought with all the sun Ive been getting and fish Ive been eating and better sleep it would abate some. All blue light is out of my house. A couple incandescents for my son with Downs syndrome. He doesnt quite get it yet but enjoys the red light from the Joov. He bathes to it and loves the candles. He is at the beach and in the creek with me. He is falling asleep better but still wakes in the middle of the night. We both have a ways to go.

This is a time for truth about many things. A time to connect to who and what I really am and what "I" need to thrive. Answering with ruthless honesty the question of who I am being in this world and in this body. This truly is a change of the mind first. I have been hypnotized by a false narrative..... many. New beliefs and priorities are forming. Its hard and I feel uncomfortable in it. I dont feel good and so have times of weakness in my thinking and comprehension. I feel myself searching for old fixes of a damaging lifestyle. Homeostasis is a tough thing to overcome.
I am progressing but I get scared. The pain in my body discourages me. Cold water helps but it is still hard to get into it for me.. but I am beginning to dislike anything hotter than lukewarm.
My carb intake is naturally dropping as I eat more oysters and seafood and other proteins. This week I have resolved to get into ketosis at last.

I will have to search the site for any info on schizophrenia. Jan seems to know where everything is, I will ask him. Im sure everything I am experiencing is here.

The last 2 years have felt like a very bad plane landing.

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=242677)
>
> I found to start with that I needed a bigger breakfast of eggs cooked in butter lard or ghee, and fatty meat with a small amount of carbs. If they were seasonal and not eaten to excess, their glycemic index was of less importance, the protein and fat make the glycemic index of the meal as a whole much lower. I ate the protein and fat first, and then topped up with seasonal carbs. I would have lamb or pork chops, liver onions and bacon, lots of eggs ....seasonal roots and greens and I'd fill up, I could add a seafood starter ... As I understand it the first meal after you break a fast the energy goes to the peripheral tissues in preference to the fat cells. (I think there's maybe an evolutionary advantage for a hungry animal to have its first meal directed differently, so that it has the energy in the peripheral tissues to go after its next meal, or be active in its environment). Eaten within half hour of sunrise to help set the gut and liver circadian rhythm, and if I ate enough I didn't think about food for 4 or 5 hours, no shaking etc, and good energy levels.
>
> I lost my mother to AD last year, and like your mum, no meds. My maternal grandmother also had dementia. I not only don't want to go out that way, but I don't want my kids to have to watch me go out that way, or accept that their own dementia fate is destined, or put their own lives on hold for years like both my mother did for her mother, or I did for her. The last couple of years of her life were a horrendous stress, and at best I was treading water but if I'd tried to eat once a day at that point, I would have made things worse, or sunk. My mother was my main stress but it could have been any other combination of stresses.
>
>
>
> If I eat one small meal a day, or less, I'm setting myself up for a self imposed eating disorder. Been there, done that, went from the large to the extra large t shirt..... There's a huge difference between a man's results of eating once a day to lose weight, and a woman. Women are wired differently when it comes to the environment, because of puberty, conception, pregnancy, childbirth and breastfeeding. Ignoring hunger cues chronically when I can't fat burn excess body fat, for whatever reason, is starvation, which I'm programmed to avoid. The stress of what my brain sees as undereating means I am more likely to store the meagre rations as fat, probably around my liver. My stress response to true or self imposed starvation, will be different to a man.
>
> I'm not aiming for X# on the scale, I'm aiming to heal, and at 60 I can't afford to get it wrong. My mother kept herself thin all her adult life, regularly weighing herself and keeping herself at her "ideal" weight, but no thought of body composition. As she aged she lost so much SQ fat that the veins on her arms became prominent, that was the time when her AD escalated big time. SQ fat = a store of stem cells. I will happily keep a few extra pounds of subcutaneous fat, if it stops my brain degenerating. We burn fat at night, which is also when we regenerate. Deliberately starving myself during the day and using up stored stem cells and possibly losing muscle mass when I'm not in a regenerating phase, or I'm not accessing it at night properly, seems counterintuitive. If its genuine hunger or hunger caused by inflammation, I'm listening to the signals. I do CT to reduce inflammation, and I try not to eat within 6 hours of midnight, or after sunset, whichever comes first seasonally, and my sleep is a top priority.
>
> I do intermittent fasting now, with a 6 hour eating window, to satiation, no small portions, but it was a progression until it was natural, effortless i.e. getting to a point where I can burn my own stored body fat. My 6 hour window satisfies my female programing if I've slept well. With a strong circadian rhythm, if I'm living in the cold lane of winter, or get enough summer sun, my stress levels are low and my sleep is good, I don't need as much food, and I think about it less. 3 or 4 days of rubbish weather or a bad night's sleep, and I can be back to 3 meals. It's still baby steps, and at 60 my progress is slow, but my brain and heart take priority. Gone are the days where I'm constantly thinking about food, or stepping on the scales 2 or 3 times a day... Hell I can lose "weight" during the day if I let myself become dehydrated or have a good dump, but it doesn't mean I'm improving ....
