### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-17T13:46:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deep-implications-of-vermont-2018-are.21424/post-246038>

My Vermont video of 2018 just got more wind in the sails....Janel said, "If it was midnight in the lab, the brain would be forwarded a couple of hours to 2 a.m., which matches what scientists expected from previous research. But they found signs that the clocks in the gut, liver and pancreas were much further ahead as if it was noon."

The mounting research and recent Nobel given for work in circadian biology have researchers astounded by how important this is. If your ⏰genes are disrupted throughout your body, diseases like cancer, neurodegeneration, and diabetes will inevitably be the result.

☀️Melanopsin is a LIGHT sensitive protein in our body. It's in our eye, brain, and skin for starters. The light you are exposed to matters -A LOT! We evolved under sunlight and moonlight/fire for millions of years. Artificial light is still very new for us from an evolutionary perspective and the spectrum can and does differ greatly from what nature provides.

Meal timing is important - we are designed to eat during ☀️ and this has impacts on metabolism as well as our clock genes.

Focus on actions to align your circadian rhythm for optimal health and disease prevention. [http://www.cbc.ca/radio/quirks/night-shifts-put-your-brain-and-gut-clocks-out-of-whack-1.4744868](http://www.cbc.ca/radio/quirks/night-shifts-put-your-brain-and-gut-clocks-out-of-whack-1.4744868)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-08-09T16:36:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deep-implications-of-vermont-2018-are.21424/post-248017>

Why does blue light or nnEMF cause insulin resistance? Answer: Melanopsin.

The realization of just how far behind nature I was in 2003 described in a simple math analogy of my life. Consider the plight of any clinician my age now trying to change within this paradigm and you'll see how hard it truly is. I graduated from college in 1986. In the 31 years since then the amount of biological information has increased 16 fold; during these 4 decades, my capacity to absorb new information has declined at an accelerating rate according to neuroscientists (if you believe them) and now is at least 50% less than when I was a medical student. If one defines ignorance as the ratio of what is available to be known to what is known, there seems no alternative to the conclusion that my ignorance is at least 25 times as extensive as it was when I got my bachelor’s degree. Although I am sure that my unfortunate condition comes as no surprise to my students and younger colleagues, I personally find it somewhat depressing looking at the math. My depression is tempered, however, by the fact that all biologists, young or old, developing or senescing, face the same melancholy situation because of an interlocking set of circumstances. But does this situation stop me from being optimistic now that I am inside my sixth decade? No.

The result of any project is not an endpoint to your life unless you are actually going to die at the end of the project.

The outcome of a project or life event opens up a new door, path, or change.

And whether the project succeeds or fails by your standards doesn’t actually matter to the black swan. It still leads you to the next event in your life.

And while it is true that each outcome leads somewhere different, you don’t actually know whether the success or failure door is the better one to go through.

Each result in your life is just a fork in a path that is endlessly forking. Nature is a pattern of forced decision making pathways for living creatures. This is why life remains dynamic and evolution really exists. And it is impossible to predict where it is leading until you understand the steering currents present in the environment at that present moment.

We need to begin to treat bad fortune with the same equanimity as you treat good fortune. Bad or good information is just that........information. Use it properly and you will still win when you understand how the steering currents of nature works on us.

And go do something big. Focus. Work hard. Aim for an outcome, if you want. But know that it’s just a tool to use to motivate yourself, not something that will make or break your life. There will be plenty more projects and outcomes afterward for the black swan mitochondriac. this is why hard things become OUR way. Discomfort and bad outcomes are the wind in our sails that allow us to tack a different course in our journey to gain Optimal.

It is a polio-like disease, for those who don't know. Many ID docs have been complaining that polio-like diseases seem to be making a comeback.......they usually find out it is linked to other more obscure infectious agents. Hypervitaminosis effects of Vitamin A on the immune system are well known and published in medicine but nobody is linking the stimulus to the effect in environments known to cause hypermethylation and destruction of B12. Infectious diseases will spike in places where nnEMF is toxic. When this occurs the retinol will cause collateral B12 damage by lowering it. In children, this will lead to developmental delay in neurons, mental illness, weird brainstem tumors, anemia and many gut complaints that mimic a leaky brush border. B12 is needed in these kids for the growth of their grey matter in the brain and spinal cord. DNA methylation is an epigenetic mechanism that has been implicated in the pathogenesis of chronic inflammatory diseases as well as malignancies, by regulating the differentiation, apoptosis, proliferation, and activation of different cell types. This is achieved through alteration of gene expression and regulation of cellular phenotype. We know that phenotype links to mtDNA damage from Doug Wallace's work.........Now we have the data to link melanopsin dysfunction to free retinol which is a direct cell toxin for neurons. Do I believe this is the key to understanding the etiology in many motor neuron diseases like ALS and CTE too? Yep. Be prepared people. Since the recovery began in 2010, California’s net domestic out-migration, according to the American community survey, has almost tripled to 140,000 annually. Over that time, the state has lost half a million net migrants with the bulk of that coming from the Los Angeles-Orange County area.

In contrast, during the first years of the decade the Bay Area, particularly San Francisco, enjoyed a renaissance of in-migration, something not seen since before 2000. But that is changing and I think nnEMF is driving it. I also think this is why there is a huge culture change in California. True friendship is like fluorescence, it shines better when the situation darkens.

NEW BLOG OUT: Fluorescence is the emission of light by a substance that has absorbed light or other electromagnetic radiation. It is a form of luminescence. In most cases, the emitted light has a longer wavelength, and therefore lower energy, than the absorbed radiation. The most striking example of fluorescence occurs when the absorbed radiation is in the ultraviolet region of the spectrum, and thus invisible to the human eye, while the emitted light is in the visible region, which gives the fluorescent substance a distinct color that can be seen only when exposed to UV light. Fluorescent materials cease to glow nearly immediately when the radiation source stops, unlike phosphorescent materials, which continue to emit light for some time after.

Fluorescence has many practical applications, including mineralogy, gemology, medicine, chemical sensors (fluorescence spectroscopy), fluorescent labeling, dyes, biological detectors, cosmic-ray detection, and, most commonly, fluorescent lamps. Fluorescence also occurs frequently in nature in some minerals and in various biological states in many branches of the animal kingdom.

Here's a new one for Dr. Jack Kruse "I told you so" archives.
Blue Light at night (bALAN) raises blood glucose, alters insulin response and also increases hunger in DIURNAL rodents.
"Diurnal" means these animals in the study are naturally active during the daytime like humans and not active at nighttime (nocturnal) like the vast majority of rodents used in studies. So this is an important study because it relates more closely to you (diurnal humans).
Start taking artificial light at night seriously folks. You may think those weird colored glasses from RaOptics that some of us are wearing at night are funny but it is certainly no joke to your long term health.
[https://physoc.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.14814/phy2.14257](https://physoc.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.14814/phy2.14257)

Another study shows why docs like Zach Bush have zero understanding of how sunlight sculpts the microbiome. If he did he would never try to sell any human restore.......he's sell them on vacations to the sun. This is how Vitamin D is created in the body by UVB LIGHT exposure on the skin and NOT by oral supplementation. This ultimately leads to big improvements in gut bacteria populations. Looks like another win for Kruse Longevity Center in stomping out marketers for the public.
Skin Exposure to Narrow Band Ultraviolet (UVB) Light Modulates the Human Intestinal Microbiome
Microbiol., 24 October 2019
Participants who took vitaminD supplements and use the narrowband had no improvement in microbiome diversity.
Participants who used narrowband uvb light only had significant improvements.
Bacteria from several families were enriched in the group that did not take prior vitamin D supplements.
This is the first study to show changes in intestinal microbiome in response to Narrowband-UVB skin exposure.
This evidence suggests the existence of a novel skin-gut axis that could be used to promote intestinal homeostasis and health.

**Can airplane seat cameras spy on passengers?**

**On board cameras**

The fact that some aircraft seats have built-in cameras is not new knowledge.

Singapore Airlines' inflight entertainment system is manufactured by Panasonic Avionics, a US-based company that supplies IFE for many of the major airlines and French company Thales. Panasonic announced a while back that it's added cameras onto seat backs.

And in 2017, Panasonic Avionics announced a partnership with Tascent -- a biometrics and identity innovation company.

"The companies will combine Tascent's biometric identity devices, software and services with Panasonic Avionic Corporation's in-flight entertainment and communications systems to provide the data to the CIA and other law enforcement agencies. The camera will also record audio.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-and-5g-deaths-time-to-start-a-body-count-in-2018-19.21428/post-278318>

**BLUE LIGHT IS A STIMULANT THAT CREATES ROS/RNS NORMALLY**

**And stimulants are great. Most Americans drink a few cups of stimulants each morning** ☕️ **to get themselves up to face the daily grind.**

**But you know what's not great? Being stimulated 24 hours of every day by blue light. This ruins the dose response curve of the ROS/RNS. That is exactly what is occuring to modern humans because they live indoors and use tech screens to an excess. What else is different about this version of blue light? The blue light that wakes us up in the sun is NEVER present without 42% IR-A light which is red light. AM sunlight has 42% red light in it and only 1600K of blue light. This small stimulus of blue light is about to improve executive function of the prefrontal cortex. This blue light needs red light to control the oxidation ROS/RNS that blue light makes when it is present without red light in our light environment.**

**ALL CELLS contain ion channels in their outer (plasma) and inner (organelle) membranes. Ion channels, similar to other proteins, are targets of oxidative impact, which modulates ion fluxes across membranes. Subsequently, these ion currents affect electrical excitability, such as action potential discharge (in neurons, muscle, and receptor cells), alteration of the membrane resting potential, synaptic transmission, hormone secretion, muscle contraction or coordination of the cell cycle.**

**An important class of ion channels is the family of potassium (K+) channels, they are not only in charge of the membrane resting potential or the repolarization of the action potentials, but also control cell proliferation or transmitter/hormone release, to name a few. A subgroup of K+ channels are the so called calcium (Ca2+) activated K+ channels which need either an increase of Ca2+ at their intracellular face to open or a combination of Ca2+ and voltage to function properly. Maxi Ca2+ activated K+ channels, also named BK channels which constitute a subgroup of Ca2+ activated K+ channels.**

**DO you know where these ion channels exist in humans? They are found on the inner mitochondrial membrane. EXCESSIVE BLUE LIGHT exposure destroys these potassium ion channels to ruin signaling of cells that control the circadian mechanism and are associated with leptin and melanopsin. LET THAT SINK IN.**

**Mitochondria are a major source of ROS generation targeting BK channels. Blue light creates that stimulus when RED LIGHT IS ABSENT.**

**The inner membrane of mitochondria contains BK channels (mtBK) which appear essential in the production of ROS. mtBK channels appear to be inserted into the mitochondrial membrane with the toxin binding sites for charybdotoxin and iberiotoxin exposed to the mitochondrial intermembrane space. This can be accessed by using outside-out patch configuration of the inner mitochondrial membrane. Consequently the C-terminal tail domain including the Ca2+ binding site is localized to the mitochondrial matrix where the proton gradient exists. RED LIGHT MOVES PROTONS BEST. Blue light creates the most ROS. Do you understand why subtracting red light from blue creates mitochondrial diseases?**

**When you look at your computer screen or phone screen, your brain wakes up. It's alert. Because it hears "It's day time! Time to be focused and do human things!"**

**But guess when it's bad to hear that signal?**

**The other 14 hours of the day that the Sun wouldn't normally be sending such a signal to the brain.**

**We need a break from the stimulus. Otherwise we fry our circuits and get fatigued.**

**So take a break from the blue. Stop the intravenous coffee to your SCN and allow yourself to relax.**

**This is why I Chillax, with Ra Optics blue blockers when I face when red light is removed from the blue light stimulus. The discount code is OPTIMAL.**

### Jack Kruse — 2019-10-16T19:40:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-and-5g-deaths-time-to-start-a-body-count-in-2018-19.21428/post-280583>

Why is the pupillary response the most critical part of the cranial nerve exam diagnosing TBI
The ipRGCs are known to connect to many brain regions that regulate very different tasks. The cells tell one part of the brain how bright it is outside so that our pupil can rapidly close—in less than a second. When this does not happen with a TBI patient it alerts us there is a problem with the catecholamines like dopamine or with melanopsin damage in the retina due to the liberation of Vitamin A from this opsin. The same ipRGCs also connect to the master clock in the brain that regulates our sleep-wake cycle. However, it takes several minutes of bright light (blue) to make us fully awake. How the same ipRGCs do these very different tasks with different time scales was not clear until now based upon this study. Some clinicians have realized this for a long time and been using the pupillary light test with blue light and red light to see the damage in the eye clock in TBI cases for years.
The investigators found that the difference has to do with the way that light detected by the retina reaches the brain. By delivering the mini-SOG to the eyes of the mice, they were able to trace the signal to the part of the brain that constricts the pupil in response to light to identify the pathways.
It turned out these connections were much stronger—similar to water pouring out of a garden hose. Whereas the connection between the ipRGCs and the master clocks in the SCN in the retina were much weaker—more like drip irrigation. This is because the ipRGCs deliver the light signal to the circadian center in the eye clock phenomena through this slower drip system, it takes longer for any meaningful information to reach and reset the brain clock. It also implies since this system works slower in the retina in TBI’s the use of light has to be altered to reduce the symptoms of photophobia and post-concussive effects.
CITES:
[https://www.cell.com/cell-reports/fulltext/S2211-1247(19)31183-0](https://www.cell.com/cell-reports/fulltext/S2211-1247\(19\)31183-0)

Blue Light at night (bALAN) raises blood glucose, alters insulin response and also increases hunger in DIURNAL rodents.
"Diurnal" means these animals in the study are naturally active during the daytime like humans and not active at nighttime (nocturnal) like the vast majority of rodents used in studies. So this is an important study because it relates more closely to you (diurnal humans).
Start taking artificial light at night seriously folks. You may think those weird colored glasses from RaOptics that some of us are wearing at night are funny but it is certainly no joke to your long term health.

### Mike David — 2018-06-06T07:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/musings-of-a-mitochondriac.21434/post-242823>

The other day my wife and I had it out and I mean had it out about uv light. She claims her father and aunt got skin cancer from working long days on the farm when they were kids. They didn’t get cancer till they were in there 50’s. I myself have been burned bad by the sun in the past blisters and all. I told her there problem probably stems from their indoor lifestyles catching up to them. I wonder will I have negative effects down the road from being burned so badly. I developed a ton of freckles in these areas but I think Jack says this is to be able to absorb more uv. I spend at least three hours a day in the sun and I noticed I don’t burn like that anymore. Is it better to go into the shade at times to cool the skin down then back into the sun? Man it felt great to go toe to toe with someone who doesn’t believe a thing I say. I guess this is what it’s like being a mitochondriac in a blue lit microwave world. Well that’s all for now thanks Jack for having the balls to tell people when there wrong.

### drezy — 2018-06-06T17:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/musings-of-a-mitochondriac.21434/post-242849>

Morning full body exposure (shirts, pants, and whatever else off if you can) is your way to build a solar callous. A solar callous is both your sunscreen and solar panel.

Every relationship works different so you're the expert on how best to work with your wife. With enough time spent here you'll be able to search up "all cause mortality" and some of the melanoma studies from Sweden I think. Having these papers sometimes helps if the belief system of another person isn't too entrenched.

My wife's culture considers lighter skin to be better. Her physics degree helped when I showed her some of the papers, but I just got in the light and the CT tub and never bothered persuading much. I tanned up and body shaped nicely with all this and I think she started to feel a little left behind. Her coworkers are concerned for her these days...

Now she often tells my son that he's been in the cold too long because he's heating it up too much and she wants in.

I'd keep up and improve your routine. Often times these things sort themselves out. If she ever does join you down the road and tans up some, make sure your let her know how undeniably attractive she looks with a tan and be extra generous while she's tanned if you know what I mean. Good old Pavlov's theories still have gas in the tank.

Doesn’t get much better than this. Taking in the crisp mountain air at just the right time. The salmon and kale omelette is calling gotta take care of that.

### Mike David — 2018-06-21T23:50:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/musings-of-a-mitochondriac.21434/post-243995>

It’s funny the faces I get walking in the park pushing a stroller with three dogs in just shorts. Imagine there faces if I did it the proper way but there’s that whole getting arrested thing. I have the balls to do it in my backyard for like 10 minutes each day. I never use a laptop or computer just the phone and that’s on airplane mostly. Do you guys think I need to do the naked thing longer. It feels really weird. The wife thinks I’m completely nuts I just smile. I can now walk barefoot through the stone alley building up those calases on my feet nicely. Haven’t worn socks in a while crazy shit I used to have soft baby feet. Just use candles at night when I wake up I have to go through the house and turn all the lights off and the tv from the kids always leaving them on. I’m down to 210 lbs. from 230 lb. after the long low light cycle winter. Not stopping till I hit 190. My old playing weight. Other than walking I don’t do strenuous exercise just fart around in the yard. I have a little setup on the outside covered porch for those stinky rainy days. I don’t even look at the weather report anymore figured I’m going to be out there no matter what anyway. I haven’t mustered the courage to do CT yet working up to that. Just take a cold 3 min. Shower then like 5 min. Warm. I haven’t read more ever in my life starting to love it. Literally read one book in the last 15 years up until I found Jack. I tried like hell to debunk what Dr. Jack was saying on the podcasts the harder I tried the more right he was. Think I might need to buck up and ct if I want to hit 190lbs. Love having a great excuse to eat lovely fresh seafood. Nobody else in the house likes it and they complain about the smell . Sometimes I appease them and grill it except for when I get the raw oysters of course. Well I wish everyone luck on there own personal road to optimal.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-21T23:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/musings-of-a-mitochondriac.21434/post-243997>

I love the reports of your transformation.  Congratulations on all of the progress you've made!

I find it doesn't really matter if anyone else understands what we're doing. When it just feels right to the core of your being, and you are happy ~ you don't really give a shit anymore.

### Simon Wright — 2018-06-06T11:06:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/picking-the-best-of-a-bad-bunch.21436/post-242825>

Hi Guys (n Girls),

I have to pick the best of a bad bunch with lighting in my work environment.

I dont live in a first world country so I can often access high end solutions.

I need to change the lights in my clothing factory and associated office. They are currently CFL and after the years on this forum i have decided to splash the funds for my workers and myself.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. As everyone is well aware of the above spectral analysis I am swaying towards warm light LED.

I have 4 doors in the factory and alot of windows, i keep these open all day to allow natural sunlight to creep in.

Halogens aren't really practical as we are on the equator and it will be too warm.

If anyone else have any suggestions or points to add before i pull the trigger on this I would very much appreciate it?

THanks

Simon

### JanSz — 2018-06-12T15:13:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/picking-the-best-of-a-bad-bunch.21436/post-243244>

Before you spend money
figure out the frequencies and power your prospective lights have.

Any black light or actinic light should be better than nothing,
but
what I noted the black bulbs like on picture above
or other black lights
are mostly black.

The red Sperti looks to me much lighter and there is a lots of visible light.

For your commercial application
if you do not want to buy numerous Red Sperti ligts
then
consider long light tubes meant for tanning beds.

Again
frequencies are important
tanning beds emphasizes tanning effect
but we are worrying about wider frequency range.

[https://www.amazon.com/Midday-F71T1...668&sr=8-2-spons&keywords=fiji+sun+lamp&psc=1](https://www.amazon.com/Midday-F71T12-5-0-Tanning-Lamps/dp/B0731JC6S9/ref=sr_1_2_sspa?ie=UTF8&qid=1528802668&sr=8-2-spons&keywords=fiji+sun+lamp&psc=1)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-22T15:55:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/picking-the-best-of-a-bad-bunch.21436/post-244042>

**New blog out for the weekend of the beginning of summer:** Tryptophan and Methionine are the only two amino acids that have SINGLE codons in the human DNA code. This makes both of them unique compared to the other known 500 amino acids. Our DNA only codes from 20 amino acids. Methionine is NOT coded for. It has to be obtained from the environment. This makes it more unique. What does methionine do in humans that make it UNIQUE? Our start codon to begin all protein synthesis always codes for methionine in HUMANS. This means the methionine cycles are a pretty big controller of something important. Have you ever wonder what they control and why they exist? What if I told you methionine and tryptophan were two-time crystals that tick everytime sunlight in a season interacts with us. One is essential and made only in the environment and the other is made by us and it forms one key chemical that controls our mitochondrial DNA. [https://www.patreon.com/posts/19082581](https://www.patreon.com/posts/19082581)

# Thread: Optometrist exam experience

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Butters — 2018-06-06T12:53:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optometrist-exam-experience.21437/post-242826>

Hello,

I had an eye exam by an optometrist.
The exam was hold in almost pure blue light. Best was around any other room there was more natural light than in the refraction room.
His answer to my comment "this is very bright and garishly" - "everyone see that different" and on my question "do you have any other light for the test" - "we do that so since always".
Great, no wonder I had my problems to focus because blue lights scatters most. My eyes also were burning after a short time and I had to blink all the time.

Should I give a \*\*\*\* on the values and search for an other optometrist?
I think they know what they do there, so they can presribe stronger lenses to people.

What are your thoughts, experiences?

I sent my son, who is in fifth grade, to school with his blue blockers. They took them away when asked why they said they are a toy. How can I force there hand to allow my son to wear blue blockers in school? Any suggestions will be much appreciated.

### Karlos Amigos — 2018-06-06T20:08:57+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recent-bloods-for-toxic-and-essential-minerals-fatty-acids.21439/post-242856>

I have just got back some results from the The European Laboratory of Nutrients (ELN) [http://www.europeanlaboratory.nl](http://www.europeanlaboratory.nl)

I decided to have some tests that were not usually requested. No GP does these in the UK.

Any input would be appreciated.

Reference range on the right

**INTRACELLULAR CONCENTRATION**
**Zinc 15.3 mg/l** 10.5 - 13.7
**Zinc/Copper ratio 22.2** 9.0 - 16.0

**Ammonia (plasma)\* 47 µmol/L** 8 - 40

**ELEMENTS IN WHOLE BLOOD**
Sodium 1875 mg/l 1820 - 2050
**Potassium 1998 mg/l** 1670 - 1970
Calcium 54.5 mg/l 48.0 - 61.0
Magnesium 39.4 mg/l 34.0 - 48.0
Zinc 7.5 mg/l 5.3 - 6.5
Copper 1.0 mg/l 0.8 - 1.3
Selenium 0.178 mg/l 0.120 - 0.410
Manganese 8.8 µg/l 14.0 - 37.0
Chromium <0.1 µg/l 0.2 - 1.6
Cobalt 0.1 µg/l 0.1 - 0.7
Molybdenum 0.6 µg/l 0.4 - 1.9
**Vanadium 0.60 µg/l** 0.04 - 0.08

TOXIC ELEMENTS
Aluminum  µg/l <= 17.0
**Arsenic 9.0 µg/l** <= 3.1
Cadmium 0.13 µg/l <= 2.30
Gold <0.05 µg/L <= 0.30
**Mercury (Organic / Inorganic) 11.9 ng/mL** 0.0 - 2.0

ESSENTIAL FATTY ACIDS Essential fatty acids,
Omega 3 series
18:3Ω3 Alfa-linolenic acid (ALA) 0.07 % 0.10 - 0.40
20:3Ω3 Eicosatrienoic acid <0.01 % 0.00 - 0.10
**20:5Ω3 Eicosapent. acid (EPA) 0.45 %** 0.60 - 1.70
22:6Ω3 Docosahexaenoic acid 5.99 % 4.00 - 9.40

Omega 6 series
18:2Ω6 Linoleic acid 13.79 % 10.60 - 16.00
**18:3Ω6 Gamma linolenic acid 0.01 %** 0.10 - 0.30
20:2Ω6 Eicosadienoic acid 0.26 % 0.00 - 0.30
20:3Ω6 Dihomogammalinolenic acid 1.06 % 1.20 - 2.20
20:4Ω6 Arachidonic acid (AA) 18.62 % 14.20 - 19.20
22:4Ω6 Docosatetranoic acid 3.72 % 1.60 - 4.40

VITAMINS (MOL/L)
Vitamin A (as retinol)\* 1.61 µmol/L 2.20 - 4.00
Thiamin B1 (Thiamine, TPP) 170 nmol/l 148 - 297
Riboflavin (B2) 566 nmol/l 797 - 1860
**Niacin (B3) 63 µmol/L** 35 - 60
Pantothenic Acid (B5) 2.9 µmol/L 2.7 - 8.4
Vitamin B6 act.(P-5-P) 103 nmol/l 85 - 505

I have highlighted some of concern.

I do not take any artificial zinc at all. No supplements.
Any thoughts? Where would it be high in the cells? Why would vanadium and B3 be high?

# Thread: Shakshuka!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shakshuka.21442/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Phosphene — 2018-06-06T22:50:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shakshuka.21442/post-242863>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Or Shakshouka...however you want to spell it, this recipe for eggs poached in a chunky, spicy tomato sauce is just fab. I know tomatoes are a seasonal thing for many of us, but it’s almost the season for me so I stretched a little. Plus I had canned local tomatoes in my pantry just begging to be used, and we don’t have any (known) issues with nightshade veggies.

I made this almost as written, which is unusual since for me recipes are just suggestions. Only changes were nearly doubling the onions and garlic, a generous tablespoon of turmeric, a large can of salmon flaked in right before the eggs, plentiful cilantro sprinkled on top, and (sadly) no pita.

Ok so I guess it was pretty far from as written.  That’s the beauty of this recipe—it’s easily adapted for what’s available/allowed.

Enjoy!

[https://www.saveur.com/article/Recipes/Eggs-Poached-in-Tomato-Sauce](https://www.saveur.com/article/Recipes/Eggs-Poached-in-Tomato-Sauce)

### Lahelada — 2018-06-07T00:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shakshuka.21442/post-242868>

I LOVE Shakshuka. GReat recipe. Add curcumin and this dish rocks.

# Thread: Hoping to get some help: PCOS, Keto, Paleo, Weightloss
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hoping-to-get-some-help-pcos-keto-paleo-weightloss.21445/>
**Subforum:** Female Quantum Biology
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kittenjo — 2018-06-07T02:26:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hoping-to-get-some-help-pcos-keto-paleo-weightloss.21445/post-242879>

Hi,

This is going to be long and I apologize if it's not in the right thread.

I was diagnosed with PCOS 14 years ago (@ 21). At the time I was about 265 lbs and had given up on getting back to a healthy weight. At 25 after seeking help from MDs including endocrinologists I gave up, no one helped. I later found the hCG protocol and then the Leptin Reset, thank you Dr. Kruse. I managed to lose 80 lbs and keep it off for 6 yrs with a paleolithic diet but my PCOS symptoms didn't improve so about 2 years ago I was put on metformin and birth control. I started to gain weight rapidly. I had a car accident 6 months ago and with the immobility, and not eating paleo, I peaked at 270. I stopped taking the meds, because they were also causing other issues.

Since finishing my treatment (April 2018) I have now switched to a ketogenic diet with how's of losing the weight. I'm currently at 243 lbs but have seen no progress in over 1 month. I workout or 5 days a week : 3 lifting days and 2 cardio days.

I don't crave carbs or sugars, I can easily fast intermittently, and I have more energy now than ever before. But is it possible to become leptin resistant again? I just don't know which direction to go in right now. I don't want to further compromise my metabolism.

I live in South Florida, does anyone have MDs they can recommend to help me figure out what's going on? Which blood tests should I have done and what should I look for?

I also take the following supplements:

1\. multi
2\. vit d
3\. b complex
4\. b6
5\. b12
6\. folic
7\. coq10
8\. magnesium malate
9\. potassium
10\. collagen
11\. melatonin
12\. Chromium picolinate
13\. L-arginine
14\. DHA

TIA

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-07T02:30:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hoping-to-get-some-help-pcos-keto-paleo-weightloss.21445/post-242883>

Go read all the threads on my Dr. Jack Kruse FB page now. Your problem are described there and you need to fix your circadian mechanisms to fix it.

### DianGisnbergMD — 2018-07-02T16:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hoping-to-get-some-help-pcos-keto-paleo-weightloss.21445/post-244756>

Yes . Stop the folic acid and add methyl-folate
Run your Spectracell Micronutrient Test-so you will know exactly what to take
Add a myo- chiro- inositol supplement
Read the story of the PCOS woman at the end of the Epi-Paloe RX book and listen to Jack talk about Circadian Rhythm/ Carbs / and your ancestry

If you dont respect that you won't get better
I am an OB/Gyn and birth control masks the problem.

Listen to all Jacks you tube talks--you will heal!

# Thread: My biohacks

### Mike David — 2018-06-07T03:29:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-biohacks.21446/post-242887>

wake up with the sun go outside in backyard wearing just shorts for an hour.
Eat 3 egg omelet with 5 uncured bacon strips and spinach or kale right after. Then off on a two hour dog walk through the park. I wear my sneakers but I’m trying to figure out the right kind of shoes to have the shoe repair guy put leather soles on. The less I wear shoes the less my feet hurt funny how that works. My feet used to kill me in my work boots. Listen to podcasts on airplane mode now when I’m walking. Phone is in airplane much of the time now. Try to spend the afternoons outback playing with my young son and reading Jacks recommended books. Eat a burger or seafood for lunch then whatever the wife makes for dinner. I drink 3 bottles of mountain valley spring water a day. Clear the shelf off once or twice a week at the grocery store they love me. I have the uvex Skyper blue light blockers (recommended by consumer reports). It kills me that some companies say allowing a little blue is good . I went through the house with my new emf detector and promptly moved the WiFi to the basement also moved the babies crib and made sure we were safe where we slept. I’m an electrician so this was not hard to do just put labels on the right breakers and off they go. I still get the fish eye from the wife when I’m wearing the blue blockers. I do drink 12 ounces of coffee in the morning but that’s down from two pots a day! I love good chocolates and smoke a half a pack a day (working on that but it’s been cut in half so far). I like watching sports on tv but I cut way down on that too. Listen to the radio outback when not raining. Loving these long light cycles. Any suggestions for what to do in uv winter here in PA? Thinking of taking epsom salt baths in the backyard any thoughts on that? Feel great and sleep like a stone but sometimes when I poop it burns what’s up with that? Comments are much appreciated and any suggestions on possible improvements or more hacks are welcomed

### Joyfun — 2018-06-07T07:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-biohacks.21446/post-242893>

[@Mike David](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21512/)
Hi Mike David,
I was thinking about leather soled shoes and shoes you can put leather soles on. Since Dr. Kruse posted that metals are antennas, what about if there are hidden metal nails in the shoes? I realized this when I bought a used pair of men's leather top and leather soled shoes at a thrift store to use parts to make my own grounded shoes. Under the inside of the conductive lining I saw the nails that held the leather heel in place. All my shoes now are grounded shoes.

### Mike David — 2018-06-07T14:29:26+0300

### Joyfun — 2018-06-07T15:41:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-biohacks.21446/post-242923>

[@Mike David](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21512/)
Hi Mike,
I looked all over aliexpress and found some inexpensive shoes with a Vibram outsole:
[https://www.aliexpress.com/item/Out...652.html?spm=a2g0s.9042311.0.0.3da24c4dh9cPia](https://www.aliexpress.com/item/Outdoor-women-s-water-shoes-women-beach-swimming-men-shoe-for-fishing-shoes-diving-beach-aqua/32842637652.html?spm=a2g0s.9042311.0.0.3da24c4dh9cPia)

They are both for men and women. Just be sure the outsole says Vibram as some same looking shoes have rubber only outsoles.

To make it more comfortable when I took out the non-conductive inner lining, I purchased real sheep skin insoles to conduct the electrons to the feet.

I found another inexpensive Vibram outsole shoe at aliexpress:
[https://www.aliexpress.com/item/Lad...106.html?spm=a2g0s.9042311.0.0.27424c4dqXOqOT](https://www.aliexpress.com/item/Ladys-Outdoor-Sandals-Couple-Summer-Garden-Shoes-Woman-Light-Slip-Circle-Round-Toe-Beach-Slippers-Sandals/32854988106.html?spm=a2g0s.9042311.0.0.27424c4dqXOqOT)

They ran small making it necessary to cut out the toe area on top. It would be OK for men or women.

Google Vibram to confirm that it is conductive material made from rubber and carbon. I found a website that says this: [https://www.theearthingstore.com/blogs/articles/18103739-what-materials-are-conductive](https://www.theearthingstore.com/blogs/articles/18103739-what-materials-are-conductive)
"Rubber boots or soled shoes do not allow electrons to permeate through. There are specially made earthing shoes such as Pluggz that have black carbon and rubber targeted centre which are actually made to be conductive."

I got copper rivets from aliexpress and put them on the outsoles of 2 pairs of shoes. But, when 5G comes, copper in your shoes will not be better than nails in your shoes.

Good for you that you are wearing blue blockers and doing what you can.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-08T02:42:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garlic-bob-beck.21447/post-242983>

Real _knowledge_ is to know the extent of one's ignorance, but wisdom is not knowledge. Wisdom trumps knowledge all the time.

Wisdom and knowledge are not synonyms. Those who seek wisdom must seek many truths to gain insight. They must be tenacious. Knowledge is a part of ignorance that man arranges and classify. Knowledge has the shelf life of fish in a market. It is not sustainable and does not always lead to truth. If knowledge is information, wisdom is the understanding and application of that knowledge and insight is the awareness of the underlying essence of a truth.

Sadly we can gain a lifetime of knowledge, yet never see the wisdom in it. We can be wise but still miss the deeper meaning of facts. Today medicine is a good example of this.

There are many who know things among us but lacking in wisdom. Your mentors should be filled with wisdom because they will be the connectors of life.

Their insight will be indefatigable for you. **Not even ‘father time’ can destroy the natural truth of Mother Nature.

EStein keep your knowledge.......and we'll focus on what matters most.**

# Thread: Trying to make sure that CT is safe.

**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Steph Roch — 2018-06-09T03:19:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-make-sure-that-ct-is-safe.21449/post-243036>

Hi everyone,

I am really working hard at understanding all of this and I do understand so much more, thanks to the webinars and the reading I have done. I try to limit my time online (to avoid the blue light) and so I am in the process of printing out many of the blog posts to read through while sitting outside in the sun. Much better than staring at a screen, even with my super cool Blue Blockers from Matt Maruca!

**I am not trying to start anything negative and I don't mean to offend anyone, but can anyone help me out with this article that I found about the potential negative effects of CT? I really want to make sure that I do not harm myself in any way. I have been doing a lot of CT but I do not want to make any mistakes. Do any of you agree with what is being said here? I know it is long but any thoughts or comments would be greatly appreciated. Thank you!
**
H**ere is the article I found:**

Cold thermogenesis is becoming an increasingly popular trend in the biohacking community, not only for weight loss but also for everything from control of inflammation to regulation of blood sugar and pain management. It has been heralded a panacea and, in certain circles, lauded as the greatest health intervention of the 21st century. I do not wish to discourage those that might adopt CT as a tool to shape their health outcomes. I do, however, feel obligated to share some of the insights I have gathered over the years from both self-experimentation and my work with others. Hopefully, this will shed a small amount of light on what can only be described as an extremely complex topic.

Let's start by talking about the mechanics of thermogenesis. Just to be clear, we are referring to the non-shivering variety that people are using to convert white adipose tissue (WAT) to brown adipose tissue (BAT). In doing so, they are able to leverage the unique ability of BAT to uncouple protons from ATP synthesis as they move down their mitochondrial gradient. This allows the dissipation of energy as heat instead of being stored for use elsewhere in the body. This is, perhaps, the most important concept for us to understand. CT is making a tradeoff to mobilize fatty acids for heat energy by sacrificing ATP generation.

CT, first and foremost, is a stress response triggered by environmental challenge. All of its effects are mediated by an adaptation to some form of environmental shock. Cold stimuli are relayed through the peripheral nervous system to the hypothalamus which in turn signals the adrenal medulla, via the sympathetic nervous system, to release both norepinephrine and epinephrine into the blood stream. These stimulatory neurotransmitters, in turn, stimulate adrenergic receptors on brown or beige adipocytes which increases adenylate cyclase and cAMP levels. cAMP then provokes protein kinase A (PRKACA) to enter the nucleus, modulating the expression of various genes that eventually leads to the activation of UCP1, or mitochondrial uncoupling protein 1.

But let's step back a few paces. If, for whatever reason, you have limited ability to convert T4 to T3 from hypothyroidism, autoimmune Hashimoto's, or some other form of thyroid disorder, the acute thermogenic response of BAT to cold will be signficantly diminished. Many of the people I have encountered with thyroid problems continue to abuse themselves with CT because they have been falsely told that it will stimulate the hypothalamus to upregulate secretion of thyrotropin-stimulating hormone (TRH) and reverse their hypothyroidism (it most certainly does not). Meanwhile, they are torturing themselves, unable to generate heat, and waiting endlessly for their thyroid to "come back online". In rare cases, cold stimulation can lower pro-inflammatory cytokine signaling in the brain and reduce hypothalamic inflammation, thus leading to improved neuroendocrine signaling, but this will not happen if there are other underlying issues such as autoimmunity or, worse, polymorphisms in genes governing adenylate cyclase -- a problem that is more common than you would suspect.

The second problem I frequently see here is a limited ability to produce norepinephrine and, downstream, epinephrine (adrenaline). There are a myriad of reasons this could happen and we won't go into them here. The most common situation is genetic blocks in dopamine beta-hydroxylase (DBH) which mediates the conversion of L-DOPA to norepinephrine. It is a copper dependent enzymatic reaction, so low copper status could exacerbate the problem. Take this a step further, and there may also be issues in beta-adrenergic receptor expression or, in some cases, antibodies against those receptors, leading to varying degrees of orthostatic hypotension. Jumping into an ice bath for such individuals is not only ill-advised but decidedly dangerous and could severely complicate their health issues. Nonetheless, I have seen recommendations from Kruse et. al. directed to such individuals, berating them for not embracing the cold and "getting well". Those that frequent the forums will know exactly who I am talking about.

So what if the HPA-axis, thyroid, and adenylate cylase genes are all in order? Would that give you carte blanche to jump into CT with full force? Let's examine the process more deeply. Initially, if you are, to one degree or another, overweight, upregulation of UCP1 will release heat in adipocytes. There may be an initial period of weight loss, but this eventually plateaus as BAT concentrations reach homeostasis. This is where the trouble may potentially begin. You see, as this practice is engaged chronically, PRKACA is not only entering the nucleus to affect UCP1 expression, but it also begins to affect the MAPK signaling pathway as well. The MAPK (mitogen-activated protein kinase) cascade is a delicate system that mediates a wide variety of cellular functions, including cell proliferation, differentiation, and migration. Each MAPK kinase (MAPKK or activators), however, can be activated by more than one MAPKK kinase (or MAPKKK), increasing the complexity and diversity of MAPK signalling. I will tell you now that few proponents of CT truly understand this cascade. I have been studying it for over a decade, and there are subtleties that are yet elusive.

As adenylate cyclase is chronically activated by BAT adrenergic receptor stimulation, PRKACA will increase expression of MAP3K5. This leads to several downstream effects, not the least of which is increased activation of p38, another mitogen-activated protein kinase that responds to stimuli from cytokines, UV radiation, heat shock, and osmotic shock. p38 is also involved in the regulation of cell differentiation and, additionally, controls apoptosis and autophagy. The problem here is that abnormal activity of p38 has been implicated in a wide array of pathological events in tissues, including neuronal, bone, lung, cardiac, skeletal muscle, and red blood cells.

1) [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25217523](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F25217523&h=ATMKqFHFvdQptCmBj0mjd1BhSeyZDuneK-8bNPMBcDzRrVdGx8N7ADNUytO5jIYtpmM8wqGA5OAaAhyAJ9kZs3xUwzKrK6QB2qBZbUBtjrc0J7vMdpIkSuCmcKoj8litxqTboA)

This may be seen on tests as elevated NF-kB, even with daily CT which supposedly "reduces" inflammatory markers. And the cascade doesn't end here. p38 also enters the nucleus and increases expression of ATF2 (activating transcription factor 2). This results in an increase in fibroblast growth facter, specifically FGF21. FGF21 is involved in cell growth, morphogenesis, tissue repair, tumor growth and invasion.

2) [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23590285](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F23590285&h=ATOee-Rphawe--rbw5OVXqpnFuAkkKvX8kSp8dmOeZFbZEIRaozIeNr4BHZPLGGAPl3uHmM64swDMsIdU6YQj_9NeH_67g4sHLkZeaVOi59mAhi9zsaPzykO-pZ4sj905pcZbA)

The good news here is that FGF21, with repeated stimulation upstream from cAMP and p38, is eventually secreted from the brown or beige adipocyte and has effects on those same cells at both the FGF receptor (FGFR1) and the klotho beta membrane protein. Why is this important? Because it inhibits the mTORC1 complex, regulating lipolysis, autophagy, lipid accumulation and lipotoxicity.

3) [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4811695/](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpmc%2Farticles%2FPMC4811695%2F&h=ATOo9vutOxeHgolA1bae4Tat-smtx6F-5rAxi2EctSd6rZsdGE9Cfs8KF9lluxXAecmI7VCAiiqXdZW0ebGyvUA1CJ5SdZJeoffgvRTG2f5rVtxiZJtpL8N9YiaEwEhcJ3zXyg)

The general problem I see here is that the clinical studies which have observed the effects of cold exposure on mTOR signaling do not take all of these intermediaries into account. They only look at the end result -- mTORC1 / mTORC2 modulation. As I have stated above, chronic stimulation of this signaling cascade can have different effects for different people. For example, CT will have little effect on mTOR regulation if there are changes in the TSC1/2 or Rheb protein structures, which are responsible for downstream inhibition of mTORC1. Have I seen this clinically? Absolutely. And it manifests as a decreased ability to lose weight, regardless of cold exposure.

But inability to lose weight is not the major problem here. The problem is that chronic cold exposure will trigger this cascade not only in adipocytes. Anywhere there are beta adrenergic receptors, there will be the potential for MAPK initiation and mitochondrial uncoupling. And where else do we see these receptors? Adrenoceptor beta 3, the main receptor involved in thermogenesis, is also expressed in the brain, spinal cord, heart, small intestine, liver, lung, ovary, and testis.

4) [https://www.genecards.org/cgi-bin/carddisp.pl…](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.genecards.org%2Fcgi-bin%2Fcarddisp.pl%3Fgene%3DADRB3%26keywords%3DADRB3%23expression&h=ATOytrwXtcy7M7gJqDZNW-dhf0c8hHyjyaXow65xYVxD7_llyZwpin0Hk-6mZH6PE2MspTmgbdR03wHhKv3hOl95SXZGNTPNwequcPRHg3L4p-7hlpLXbNWwOkEWNSFZ8C1qsg)

Do you believe that systemic stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system via our skin surfaces can affect these organs? This is a very deep question, and you're not going to find direct answers in the literature. You have to put the pieces together as I have. This is a very complex issue and there are no "one size fits all answers". If you are engaging in CT on a regular basis, you need to understand what it is doing to you, specifically. And that means knowing your cAMP, MAPK, mTOR, and OXPHOS genetics up and down. The skeptics will tell you this is not possible because they are still tethered to clinical studies and epidemiological data. While I am not confortable discussing my approach to this issue in detail on social media, I will tell you that it is possible to know these pathways without having to depend on such data. I do it every day and have measured biomarkers to prove its efficacy.

There are many angles to the cold thermogenesis consideration. The conclusions I came to ~5 years ago was that the experts promoting CT for longevity did not have a firm grasp on the pathways I have mentioned above. Discussing the intricacies of these pathways and their interactions could easily consume a novel. Hopefully, I have given you some solid concepts to contemplate. As you meditate on these ideas, consider this: catecholamines such as norepinephrine and adrenaline play significant roles in the dispatch and migration of mesenchymal stem cells. You have a finite number of these cells, and upregulating their appropriation to damaged tissues and organs might seem to be a valid approach for longevity, but not if you are consistently incurring damage.

Chronic cold exposure, regardless of source, produces a stress response via the mechanisms described above. And while uncoupling mitochondria to generate heat may bring down the net reactive oxygen species concentration (which is higher in ATP-productive processes), there is still a measurable cascade of oxidative stress occurring in tissues systemically. This is not a problem if your genetic expression for superoxide dismutase, catalase, BAX, BAD, etc. are in perfectly good working order. But many people I see do not have this luxury. And measuring their 8-OHdG and lipid peroxide levels before and after prolonged CT has been very revealing. That said, do you know what it is doing to you, in your own particular case? Regardless of what you think, measure your cytokines, oxidative stress markers, and yes -- telomeres. You may be surprised at what you find. And know your genetics. They matter, regardless of what the "experts" out there are saying, all the while grossly oversimplifying biology for their own ends.

5) **[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25173883](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F25173883&h=ATNdYz6dfx0PBeH-AV7xesNFwy96XTSMFCpaV3BiejZnLX3SVAC1r2ORrOLW4XDqMq2J8Wxgp893bv0Z8s9E5KZg_49cw1u-TOQEgwcmghNqf4F1sr0rBEdjOFZZ_TxbyoHoJg)**
6) [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5815212/](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpmc%2Farticles%2FPMC5815212%2F&h=ATMDZD9-JXgxoYuN0wwH92wWYbd0RQ9bbDj8H75RCHToovOVApp0MoNVB6u6XzQJVBpY4dDSoPMNXWNnsy-CEqlMOngtjOMi7M3x7FP8ROuVtCUmQX_VMhmFXPQQb1c9vyxIhA)

> [Steph Roch said:](/goto/post?id=243036)
>
> [That said, do you know what it is doing to you, in your own particular case?](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F23590285&h=ATOee-Rphawe--rbw5OVXqpnFuAkkKvX8kSp8dmOeZFbZEIRaozIeNr4BHZPLGGAPl3uHmM64swDMsIdU6YQj_9NeH_67g4sHLkZeaVOi59mAhi9zsaPzykO-pZ4sj905pcZbA)

A tennis player after a 5 set match in the heat of the day will use an ice bath to bring down inflammation and pain - so its a tool to use after a flight or a day of being zapped by wifi etc that I couldn't avoid.

If in the winter I was living or working all day in artificial summer temperatures and light frequencies that I had no control over, my need for CT would be different. In the past I've tried to keep my BAT levels up all year, because I wanted to lose weight. But BAT is dynamic, I think it should naturally drop as spring moves to summer, when light is the dominant signal, and increase again during the autumn. I've had better results with cool thermogenesis rather than cold in the summer. Getting in the as it comes water in the outside tub as the warmth of the sun increases, cool the surfaces and then dry off barefoot in the sun. Rinse and repeat. Or stand with my hands and feet in the water, with the sun on my spine, as if I'm in the sea shallows, harvesting seafood … Or use a lump of ice like soap, standing in the sunshine ….. At this latitude with limited sunlight, it would make being inside in an icy bath a mismatch, and a waste of valuable photons!

I think there is also a gender difference - at the extreme end, a male polar bear lives on the icepack during winter. A female conceives in spring but doesn't become pregnant until autumn, and if she isn't holding enough fat or is unable to den, the oocyte doesn't implant …. Even for her uncoupled haplotype, her environment as a whole matters, cold can go from environmental challenge to too big a stress, with biological consequences. I think my "goldilocks zone" of CT varies seasonally, and has to be put in context with my environment as a whole, at that particular moment. My tolerance varies, and I've learned to watch for the signals, and if I'm doing deep CT caused by my natural environment, I use a timer, rather than risk over doing it. If I sense I need to get out earlier, I do. If I've followed the light cycles properly and I'm _living_ in the cold dark and boring lane in winter, I don't need to CT in water to maintain BAT. Last winter I was a woman in the cold, not a cold woman and I "denned" in cold, dark and boring for 10-12 hours a night. Next winter I will take denning up a notch, when its dark from 5 pm to 8 a.m.

I struggle with the science and the exact biochemical pathways, but I think the article misses the light and water quantum thermodynamics. The CT protocol is designed to be done in a particular way, in baby steps in terms of intensity and duration, and is a part of one leg of the 3 legged stool. I've learned that even with an uncoupled haplotype, its a valuable, flexible tool, but I can't CT myself out of a crap environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-11T19:12:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-make-sure-that-ct-is-safe.21449/post-243190>

Since the skin, eye, and gut are loaded with tyrosine laden melanin could this be why eating at abnormal times disrupts the biological clock of the skin in several species, including the daytime potency of an enzyme that protects against the sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation? Yep. Leptin Rx for the win yet again. [https://m.medicalxpress.com/news/2017-08-habits-affect-skin-sun.html](https://m.medicalxpress.com/news/2017-08-habits-affect-skin-sun.html)

_**Tryptophan**_

Tryptophan is significantly more fluorescent than tyrosine and phenylalanine. However, its fluorescent properties are solvent dependent, i.e. the spectrum shifts to shorter wavelengths and increases in intensity as the polarity of the solvent decreases. As such, tryptophan residues buried in hydrophobic domains of folded proteins exhibit a spectral shift of 10 to 20 nm.

Due to its greater absorptivity, higher quantum yield, and resonance energy transfer, the fluorescence spectrum of a protein containing the three amino acids usually resembles that of tryptophan.

**_Tyrosine_**

Tyrosine can be excited at wavelengths similar to that of tryptophan but will emit at a distinctly different wavelength. While it may be true that tyrosine is less fluorescent than tryptophan, it can provide significant signal since it is often present in large numbers in many proteins. Tyrosine fluorescence has been observed to be quenched by the presence of nearby tryptophan moieties either through a molecular resonance energy transfer or the ionization of its aromatic hydroxyl group. This is how sunlight 'informs' tyrosine what to do folks with information quanta. This is the essence of the QT series on Patreon. Anyone want to see how ignorant the skeptics really are. They have undressed themselves for all of you to see.

I am not even close to done burying them either.

Here are some important points to remember when measuring peptides using the A280 method.

-   Proteins of similar molecular weight can have different absorbance values due to the difference in tryptophan and tyrosine content.
-   UV absorbance is also affected by protein structure. Thus, conditions which affect structure (such as temperature, pH, ionic strength, or the presence of detergents) can affect the ability of aromatic residues to absorb light at 280 nm, and change the value of the protein’s extinction coefficient.
-   **The local environment of the aromatic amino acids can have an effect on their spectra.** How many of the skeptics know that the ph or temperature can change proteins and lipids made out of tyrosine? Do you think I knew this when I wrote CT 4 or CT 6? Think about your skeptics of CT and QB now folks. I am running them over with FACTS they do not know. Dunning Kruger 101. This means that tryptophan will have an emission peak at lower wavelengths when buried within the hydrophobic inner regions of a protein while tyrosine will often transfer its energy to adjacent tryptophan amino acids. Ionized tyrosinate, which forms when protons are lost from tyrosine as a result of increasing pH, will demonstrate wavelengths similar to tryptophan. It is about nuclear reaction where atoms of oxygen and hydrogen join and create water and heat.

So far, hydrogen and hydrogen isotope (deuterium) are discussed in regard to water.
But there are other isotopes that influence our biology.

Water is highly interesting and highly complicated subject.
Water made in matrix is not even liquid.
There are variety of interacting clusters.

# Thread: lowbluelights.com bulbs ???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowbluelights-com-bulbs.21452/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Anneh — 2018-06-09T21:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowbluelights-com-bulbs.21452/post-243087>

Hello everyone,

I am really hoping to get some help from you guys?

In terms of illumination in the evenings, it seems that people are discussing red lights and black lights (and candles and no lights). And from what I can tell Dr. Kruse also doesn't use them, so there must be a reason for that. But I am wondering why people are not using bulbs from lowbluelights.com, as they claim to prevent blue light spectrum, and they are linked to on the Jack Kruse website.

Thank you so much in advance for sharing your wisdom and experience.

Anne

### Anneh — 2018-06-10T07:38:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowbluelights-com-bulbs.21452/post-243132>

Ok, after a LOT more reading, I am going to assume it's because whatever blue blocking capacity they have, the lux is just too high in them. (I just measured all the different ones I own with an app and the lux is quite high on the regular bulbs. Even on the nightlight and the booklight it is potentially high enough to disrupt melatonin, although I'm still not completely sure about that?.)

I noticed a big difference in my head (migraines and eye pain) from using these bulbs. I simply can't have white lights on in the evening -- they make me sick, so these bulbs have been a big improvement that way. However, the lowbluelight.com bulbs are still quite bright, and I have not noticed any improvement in sleep from using them.

So onward and upward as I continue to reduce and modify the light in my environment at night.

### shiran — 2018-06-10T09:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowbluelights-com-bulbs.21452/post-243133>

hey
It is important at night to protect the eye with eyeglasses and skin with clothing
Artificial light is not optimal only sunlight is optimal and correct for us
I personally and since I do not live alone uses the
[https://lowbluelights.com/product/12-led-gooseneck-usb-light-copy/](https://lowbluelights.com/product/12-led-gooseneck-usb-light-copy/)
I adjust and reduce the light to a minimum possible by a long press behind..
When I put on my UVEX glasses on The light from the lamp does not change and it means that there is no blue light in it
Me and the children also go to sleep very early an hour after sunset so I do not have much dealing with the darkness just going to sleep is the best for us..

### Martha Ray — 2019-10-02T18:29:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/find-members-in-your-area.21453/post-279415>

> [Jason Yun said:](/goto/post?id=278653)
>
> Martha could of used this a while ago. my dad went through quad bypass surgery on 11th and dumbass doctor has now doubled his statins. Been trying to get him off for more then 5 years.

Hi Jason Yun,
My husband finally got off his statin when one of his buddies came hobbling into their gym on a walker and said that statins had crippled him. I'd been praying he get off as that prescription was sickening me every time I saw it sitting on the kitchen table. So the grace of God was at work so I hope something influences your dad consider tapering down or getting off. Statins are notorious for taking folks cognition down. Half of my family are full out conventional allopatic doctor worshipers. Whatever the doctor says... I can't teach, bully, trick, coerce or move them and it is really not my place too. They are not yet open. It's painful to watch...with much learning comes much suffering. Living what I believe to be an example and praying a lot works for me and I have seen God work on some of them from different influences.
I gave my husband's cardiologist the book...Is you cardiologist killing you? by Dr. Sherry Rogers, [https://www.amazon.com/Is-Your-Cardiologist-Killing-You/dp/B001U9WFUS](https://www.amazon.com/Is-Your-Cardiologist-Killing-You/dp/B001U9WFUS). I cried when I gave it to him as the title is pretty bad so he knows it was hard but important to me to share it with him. Next appointment I had to ask re it and he said he read but was not impressed enough with the references...funny thing though I think his nurse and staff have gotten friendlier toward the message. Dr. Rogers is blood and guts straightforward and the book makes a lot of good points.
Will pray for your Dad only if ok with you. For his protection and some intervention for his best health. Just let me know.
My hope is for both of you to be well and prosper.

### Ryan Carter — 2023-06-02T03:42:54+0300

>
> Hi Ryan, me and my family might pop over in July. Keen to hear some recommendations and tips. We work on the same field as you, would be cool to connect.

Amazing. I will be returning in mid-July from North Africa / Europe trip.
It is a massive country - i have yet to explore the north or the Carribean side. July is the rainy season but there's plenty of sunshine
Ometepe - volcano summit climb
Possible visits Granada - Leon - both volcanos - Corn Island trip for a few days to the Carribean island.
Managua is grim/major city vibe - it can be dangerous. But the rest of the country is very safe when applying common sense. Spanish language skills are important further go out of tourist areas.

Renting is best on Facebook groups as oppose to using Airbnb.

I am in San Juan Del Sur. Welcome to message me, and happy to meet up if our paths collide.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-10T04:59:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicide-and-kids-the-link-is-nnemf-from-screens-and-devices.21455/post-243118>

Scott said, "At the heart of the EMF issue are "shifted behaviors" and our value of nature. We have to be honest what we allow into our lives due to our behaviors, but also what's coming into our lives without our permission. If nnEMFs were visible to the naked eye, there would probably not be an EMF Warriors because the public would know what's up with their environments and demand Industry to change. Most people don't want to hear this about what they allow into their lives, because convenience with tech typically trumps a decision to get outside, get colder or feel some discomfort. Case in point: I've seen a lot of people complain when the thermostat is set to 66F vs. 68F.

Over the past 50 years, we've become a society of comfort-loving creatures, looking down into screens and stumbling on sidewalks to create a 2nd chin. In profound irony, many of us will prioritize our tech-time over our own families and friends that are there with us "in the moment." Neglect is a real thing, so when a child sees the role-model parent continually checking devices, the deeper and longer-term influences are typically ignored. The ways we manage our time is often wrapped up in this equation. Those people that setting aside a rough window of time when it is consumed are also defining the rough windows of time when real human interaction is consumed.

Those of us that are awake see it in front of us. Our modern behaviors can blend together all windows of the day. Unless we define the "off limits" times of a day and try to hold to them, our time can be easily mismanaged. On my personal list, the window after dusk is exactly this "off limits" time frame. I will brake that window if the importance is great enough, so it's more like a "guideline" rather than a military rule. Why post-dusk? Because it helps circadian rhythm and also helps us wind-down for the night and also share actual in-person conversations to bring more meaning back to life.

In our knowing that nature can heal, calm and slow down time for our lives, I also like to set aside windows of time for her as well and not blend technology into the mix while I'm in nature. This also allows a person to look inward and listen to one's mental chatter for thoughts, creativity and resolutions rather than focusing on something outside of us. If we are not making enough time for ourselves to self-reflect, it eventually catches up. The overuse of technology may be the greatest barrier we face today, even when we are doing it to spend time with the people we care about online. But if we are mindful to setup windows with nature and self-reflect, balance can be found.

Here is a test I've done in the past. Log the amount of time I spend in front of all screens in a day versus the time spent away from a screen. Does this look balanced? (Similarly, how much time indoors vs. outdoors, how many hours in closer proximity to magnetic/electric fields and RF/Microwaves vs. not). Now parents, do this for your kids. How many hours of screen time do they get vs. outdoor time? In 1978 for me as a kid, it was about 3 hours of outdoor time on average and about 2 hours of screen time (due to the TV).

About 20 years ago when my Electro-sensitivities were at their worst, I was spending about 14 hours of screen time per day (due to 10+ hours at work, then my behaviors with tech at home), about 1 hour of nature at best behind sunglasses and sunscreen. It all might be fine and well for a few weeks or months, but fast-forward these types of behaviors long enough, I expect problems to show up because I've lived it. I can only imagine what our ancestors from 1818 would think about our modern lives 200 years later."

The dramatic increase in the use of cellular telephones has generated concern about possible negative effects of radiofrequency and microwave signals delivered to the brain. When cell phones are placed against the head it opens the blood-brain barrier to things that should not get in. Moreover, if you are afflicted with 4G and 5G electrosensitivity it also allows you to have a leaky brain and retinal barrier. This is usually associated with poor redox potentials in cells. People who are already ill will be afflicted by this. I fully expect this problem to show up in the cities with the first rollouts of 5G. Think NYC, Austin, LA, and Denver/Colorado Springs/Washington DC/ Vegas/ SF, San Diego. Be aware of it. Dr. Allan Frey, biophysicist, and engineering psychologist. His work has changed the scientific approach to the subject of radiation and made it an important topic for other scientists. What has he done? It is important to know what the blood-brain barrier (BBB) is. By definition, it is a highly selective semipermeable membrane (meaning it allows certain molecules or ions to pass through it by diffusion) barrier that separates the circulating blood from the brain. He found out that this kind of radiation could break the blood-brain barrier and harm the circulatory system. After long-term exposure, bacteria and toxins could enter the brain environment and affect the functioning of the nerve cells. Frey used a relatively simple method to describe his work.

The circulatory system of rats was injected with a fluorescent dye. As we know, the brain is protected from taking such substances due to the blood-brain barrier so it remained pink-grey while different organs were fluorescing. Then, animals were exposed to microwave radiation, after a few minutes the dye got through the barrier of the brain. It happened because even at low-frequency signals could open the protective barrier. Doctor Allan Frey’s studies were reported in the Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences in 1975. That is how long we have known about it. Gadolinium deposition is another consequence of a blue-lit microwaved environment. [https://appliedradiology.com/articles/an-overview-of-gadolinium-deposition-disease](https://appliedradiology.com/articles/an-overview-of-gadolinium-deposition-disease)

Many of you know I believe obesity is an inflammatory disease of the brain that begins in the retina and skin due to melanopsin defects in retinol due to an altered spectrum of light that causes us to begin to collect deuterium in our retina to expand the choroid and subcutaneous fat. This is mediated by extracellular matrix changes in a protein called MMP-2. Once the melanopsin defect occurs the altered retinol proteins destroy the basement membrane of tissues to cause damage that allow deuterium get into places in cells it just should not be. MMP-2 has another effect. It changes the physical-chemical structure of leptin and these changes do not allow leptin to enter the hypothalamus at night.

The same thing happens in out gut and respiratory tree too in humans. Where does a cavernoma (CCM's) begin? It begins with the light we choose to live under. This disease begins pathophysiology may surprise some of you non-Black Swans. It begins both in our eyes and gut flora. How does this happen? There are many ways to obesity and this is why variation exists in this condition. Suboptimal bacteria filled with deuterium which contain bacterial toxins called lipopolysaccharides (LPS) which are found in bacterial cell membranes. This is the chemicals that increase MMP-2 in the extracellular matrix. Since deuterium is an isotope of H+ and H+ is linked to the pH scale which defines inflammation you begin to see how deuterium is playing its key role. As gut LPS rises due to the deuterium in the bacteria and our enterocytes people get fat and get cavernomas in their brain. I have seen this disease many times as a neurosurgeon. This disease has been shown to cause a rise in serum leptin levels. Once leptin levels are raised high enough, it stimulates SOCS 3 signaling in the hypothalamus to cause Leptin resistance because of the changes in the leptin molecule. Scientists have recently found this mechanism in nocturnal mammals but they have not studied the effect in diurnal humans yet because we just learned 8 months ago that melanopsin is in the skin of humans. [https://neurosciencenews.com/leptin-obesity-overeating-9739/](https://neurosciencenews.com/leptin-obesity-overeating-9739/)

Deuterium fractionation easily set this off and the most severe cases I believe are tied to deuteration of the leptin receptor with marked altered T1 imaging due to the kinetic isotope effect of deuterium on T1 relaxation. The excess deuterium destroys the short and long loop of DHA recycling in the retina and this leads to a slowing down of the circadian timing mechanism in the SCN/eye clock as the choroid thickens via the central retinal pathways. This pathway also affects the hypothalamus where the leptin receptor is located. The overweight seems to physiologically 'guard' their elevated weight once the anterior visual pathways and gut injury have transpired. In many mammal models of diet-induced obesity, leptin resistance is seen initially at and within the signaling through the vagal afferents into the area postrema in the brain stem. I believe the amount of deuterium clogging the ATPase is the key science is missing. This blunts the actions of satiety incretin hormones centrally and causes low brain dopamine levels especially in the frontal lobes where the anterior visual pathways have a great influence on signaling. We can see this in T1 images on MRI if we understand how images are made. Most radiologists and clinicians do not understand the deuterium effect on T1 relaxation. These things, along with the gastrointestinal bacterial/deuterium-triggered SOCS3 signaling, are all implicated in the etiology of human obesity. In humans, man-made dietary fats (deuterium bomb) and fructose (another deuterium bomb) are capable of increasing systemic lipopolysaccharide and SOCS3 signaling. Protein seems immune to this signaling of SOCS3 because animal proteins are naturally depleted of deuterium. This is why protein/fat and AM light exposure of the eye are keys to disease reversals in my Leptin Rx. In this article below, we can see how gut level LPS leads to another brain condition called cerebral cavernous malformations (CCMs). They are clusters of dilated, thin-walled blood vessels that can lead to seizures or stroke when blood leaks into the surrounding brain tissue. I'll post an MRI pic of it in the comments. This condition should be a sign to the clinician that the brain-gut axis is under attack BY DEUTERIUM and there is a problem in both organ systems that can lead to systemic effects of inflammation and mitochondrial damage. The most interesting part of this story is how there is an environmental link with nnEMF and blue light and the gut bacteria. The link is deuterium in all the cases. Bacteria can act like fruits and be deuterium bombs and not many people realize it yet. This seems to have been missed by researchers and by this article below. Researchers have been studying a well-established mouse model that forms a significant number of CCM's following the injection of a drug to induce gene deletion. It turns out that drug is also a deuterium bomb. However, when the animals were relocated to a new facility, the frequency of lesion formation decreased to almost zero because of the light change. They never made mention of the change in the light environment of the animals but this factor cannot be overlooked by any Black Swan. Endothelial TLR4 and the ECM MMP-2 stimulated from the microbiome drive cerebral cavernous malformations and I believe man-made laboratory light was the stimulus that really set this process off. It points out how a light environment can affect a process in the gut because of how melanopsin damage controls the peripheral gut clocks via the Bazan effect I spoke about in Vermont in 2018 in June. This is not a baffling condition to a Black Swan mitochondriac at all. It is another deuterium laden disease linked to melanopsin damage in the skin and eye. [https://www.nytimes.com/2017/05/10/...mdesk&kwp\_0=619317&kwp\_4=2201430&kwp\_1=919892](https://www.nytimes.com/2017/05/10/health/brain-defect-gut-bacteria-microbiome.html?mc=aud_dev&mcid=keywee&mccr=domdesk&kwp_0=619317&kwp_4=2201430&kwp_1=919892)

What people seem to not know is we have known that deuterium is very toxic to bacteria. So when they are loaded with it they die fast and then the deuterium is liberated to our enterocytes and this causes CCM's. Any time I see a CCM I know the person deuterium fractions in the gut and brain are way above 130 ppm. ......We've know deuterium is toxic a long time. 1964 paper in Nature.
