I don't know how accurate the Cornet 88T is with LF and ELF. i want to get a separate meter for measuring Low frequencies. Do you know how I'd ground my keyboard and mouse?

Anyone know anything about having a car bluetooth removed. Should I just bring it to a shop? or is it something pretty simpl I can do. I've looked up online about having it removed, and everything just shows how to get Bluetooth in your car lol. I drive a lot, and it reads pretty high with my cornet.

### Katie Durham — 2019-08-30T15:46:29+0300

Wow! 30 minutes later, I'm now wired! The mouse/keyboard aren't setting off my meter at all (RF range). Kind of junky equipment but I'll get used to it.

The IT guy who brought it up had some familiarity because a friend of his is a 5g tower installer and is getting zapped. I demonstrated my meter for the IT guy, who I hope appreciates better what we're expected to endure here at work.

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-08-30T19:04:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rf-meter.21546/post-276881>

I don't know how accurate the Cornet 88T is with LF and ELF. i want to get a separate meter for measuring Low frequencies. Do you know how I'd ground my keyboard and mouse?
>
> Anyone know anything about having a car bluetooth removed. Should I just bring it to a shop? or is it something pretty simpl I can do. I've looked up online about having it removed, and everything just shows how to get Bluetooth in your car lol. I drive a lot, and it reads pretty high with my cornet.

I am somewhat skeptical of products that claim to measure RF and ELF in a combo meter. I use an RF meter and Gaussmeter separately to measure these fields.

Assuming your computer is a laptop, you could try shielding it with ELF shielding materials like Harapad and Wraptop to reduce EMF exposure (similar shielding products are available for desktop computers too).

[http://www.emfwise.com](http://www.emfwise.com) has helpful info on grounding computers and other electrical devices (but if you have dirty electricity in house you may inadvertently bring in stray voltage this way). Also, even if you have a wired (ethernet) connection to computer (using no Wi-Fry router) it is possible your computer has original factory computer settings set to enable Wi-Fry (i.e., your computer has its own internal Wi-Fry antenna and unless it is disabled it is contantly barking out RF microwaves vainly to try to connect to an external Wi-Fry source).

It actually my work computer, but I believe its connected via Ethernet cable because the RF readings aren't high in my office. ### Alex97232 — 2019-09-01T20:08:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rf-meter.21546/post-276966>

> [Nrelax11 said:](/goto/post?id=276897)
>
> The usually peaks at 0.45 mW/m2 and gauss 0.6mG

Hi. Consider that .45 milliWatts converts to 450 microWatts to compare your measurements
with various studies that have been done:
\_The Bioinitiative 2012 recommendation for power density limit is 3-6 microWatts/m2.
\_SBM 2008 notes "Severe Concern" for sleeping areas measuring 10-1,000 microWatts/m2.
\_The Oberfranken study evaluated medical complaints of 356 people with long term radiation
exposure in their homes. Above 100 microWatts/m2, only 5-6% of the people did NOT have
adverse health effects.
Quoting Drezy: "Once you see, you can't unsee".

### Nrelax11 — 2019-09-01T20:30:10+0300

# Thread: Hair?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CarolandNick — 2018-07-02T10:43:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244740>

I have been following Jack for nearly a year now. reading and researching in my spare time. I still feel like a "beginner" - there is just so much information here, and its takes time to process it. This may have been asked before - but what is the point of the hair on our heads? This is the only hair on our body that continues to grow, and can get really long. I have read in some cultures this indicates some kind of power... it is generally considered to be providing some kind of protection for the head, but as I am having my mind blown by so much I read here I have stopped taking things at face value ;-)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-02T16:07:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244754>

they are an antenna for EMF that comes from your hair follicle. The very same hair follicle where your melanocytes on your skin keep your stem cells for melanocytes.....People with vitiligo and alopecia have circadian disruption of the hair follicle and their ability to use the sun well.

### Trev Douglas — 2018-07-02T16:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244772>

I have wondered about body hair...and how some are naturally hairy and others not.
I am naturally fairly hairy chested and wondered if it is a hinderance to harvesting sunlight, or the perfect moderator for extending sun time...a natural solar callous optimizer!

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-02T16:51:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244775>

More time you spend in sun more hair grows........when you lose hair it is because your cells are STARVED for light from our star.

This is why diabetics lose their hair from blue light and why their skin changes and why when they get back in the sun hair sweating and skin thickness all change.

### CarolandNick — 2018-07-04T08:50:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244936>

What about for us ladies? I personally don't relish the thought of more body hair  , but I have never been the girl that was obsessive about removing it either. I am ok with it how it is. Sugaring/waxing always felt wrong to me .... the pain surely isn't good for us... but there is a cultural pressure to remove it. What are we doing to ourselves?
What is the reason women are (usually) less hairy than men?

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-04T14:44:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244942>

Nature doesn't really have any concern for vanity and neither do I. You can do and believe what you like. My task is to bring the science data to you, it is not designed to make you like what it means.

### WalterNL — 2018-07-04T16:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-244952>

There was a time when people used products with loads of heavy metals as cosmetics. People do weird things out of vanity.

### Meli — 2018-07-16T00:32:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-245940>

Many people start graying in their thirties and they say it is genetic. Is graying genetic? If one is a mitochondriac out in the sun alot at what age would graying be normal?

Can I expect my hair to become brown again with alot of sun? It is about one quarter gray. Why does it gray?

### Jenny S — 2018-07-16T05:17:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-245965>

Why do men grow beards & not females?

### Inger — 2018-07-16T10:35:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-245968>

> [Melinda Hood said:](/goto/post?id=245940)
>
> Many people start graying in their thirties and they say it is genetic. Is graying genetic? If one is a mitochondriac out in the sun alot at what age would graying be normal?
>
> Can I expect my hair to become brown again with alot of sun? It is about one quarter gray. Why does it gray?

My guess is, it is partly genetic, but we can delay it with being naked in the sun a lot?
My bosses wife is all gray and she is my age, but she colors her hair so you cant tell. She is almost never outside. I have a few gray/white hair you cant really see...my sister that is a few years younger has more gray and she is never naked in the sun because of religious dogma, but she do is outside a bit though but no way enough.
My dad became gray/white (dad is very white skinned, freckles and red hair, mum is dark skinned and brown hair) in his 30ies and my mum is still brown haired. My dad worked shift work...mum was a stay home mum, always liked the sun - but I do guess genetics too plays a role. I am curious how fast I will turn white!

### Patrick K — 2018-07-17T16:11:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair.21548/post-246056>

Yup...I know grey is a sign of the stem cells no longer producing the pigment needed for color (melanin i believe)....somewhere I read too much hydrogen peroxyde and not enough catalyse.

Either genetics (expressed because of ) stress and environnmental factors are responsible.

# Thread: New beginning
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-beginning.21550/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tara — 2018-07-02T21:24:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-beginning.21550/post-244791>

Looking back I think my story starts way back when I was 12. I started having severe constipation issues. I literally wouldn't go for a month, take laxatives or some other prescription meds and would get some relief. I still have a lot of issues with this and what shocks me is most doctors don't think it's a big deal. (they should try it some time!).

Fast forward to age 17 and I became anorexic. After a year of struggling with anorexia I switched to bulimia and have always ever since struggled with eating issues.

Around age 25 my spouse and I had our first child. When she was born, she didn't sleep, didn't have a good suck reflex (so was failure to thrive), nonstop cranky for a good two years. I'm sure it sounds like I'm exaggerating but I really do mean we never slept. Even when my husband and I see pictures from that time frame we get sick to our stomachs. it was the hardest years of our lives. I do believe lack of sleep has caused a lot of the issues my body has faced.

Since then I have miscarried two children and also have an amazingly healthy son.

Around 5 years ago I started having all over body pain. They diagnosed fibromyalgia. But then I had extreme fatigue, unusual rashes in the sun, Raynaud's, horrible memory issues, intolerance to exercise (exercising seemed to throw me into a flare). After running blooodwork I was diagnosed with systemic scleroderma.

In the meantime, my daughter (who has williams syndrome), began having trouble with blood pressure, abdominal pain, leg pains etc. So they ran some tests and she has a large amount of vessels that are narrowed (2 of the 3 feeding her abdomen are severely stenotic, both renal arteries are stenotic). She's on blood pressure meds and seizure meds and anxiety meds. More than anything I want her loving her life again and living a good quality life.

We've started on the letpin Rx and are sitting in the AM sun (but also getting in the sunlight throughout the day), grounding (I love it because now she looks at me and asks if I want to go ground), and next we are focusing on food.

My vit d was extremely low as was my potassium when I had my labs done back in February. So searching on the Vit D was probably what led me to this website.

At this point I am off the pain medicine the doctors had me on (hydrocodne and tramadol) and I took myself off of the plaquenil.

I look forward to journaling and being able to look back and see how far I've come. Thanks to everyone else for doing journals as well - it's inspiring and motitvating to read!

### Tara — 2018-07-10T20:49:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-beginning.21550/post-245560>

I seem to have an addiction to diet soda. I'm not having issues with getting sunlight, eating correctly, etc but the diet soda is holding me back. Can someone give me some links to what diet soda/artificial sweetener is doing to my body (I think once I read everything it will scare me out of it).

### Phosphene — 2018-07-10T23:24:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-beginning.21550/post-245566>

Welcome Tara! Here’s a good place to start.

[https://jackkruse.com/msg-your-gut-and-your-brain-post-trauma/](https://jackkruse.com/msg-your-gut-and-your-brain-post-trauma/)

Can you try replacing the diet soda with sparkling water? Maybe flavor it with lemon, mint or cucumber, and a drop of liquid stevia at first? I know it’s hard to wean off the sweet stuff but you can do it! Try to identify what it is, exactly, about the soda that draws you—the flavor, texture (carbonation), cold, caffeine? Are you just thirsty or hungry and chugging a big glass of plain water will fix it? Good luck!

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-11T02:55:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-beginning.21550/post-245574>

# Thread: Mitochondrial Heteroplasmy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-heteroplasmy.21555/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jason Coates — 2018-07-03T09:41:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-heteroplasmy.21555/post-244832>

[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-04797-2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-04797-2)

Vital mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) populations exist in cells and may consist of heteroplasmic mixtures of mtDNA types. The evolution of these heteroplasmic populations through development, ageing, and generations is central to genetic diseases, but is poorly understood in mammals. Here we dissect these population dynamics using a dataset of unprecedented size and temporal span, comprising 1947 single-cell oocyte and 899 somatic measurements of heteroplasmy change throughout lifetimes and generations in two genetically distinct mouse models. We provide a novel and detailed quantitative characterisation of the linear increase in heteroplasmy variance throughout mammalian life courses in oocytes and pups. We find that differences in mean heteroplasmy are induced between generations, and the heteroplasmy of germline and somatic precursors diverge early in development, with a haplotype-specific direction of segregation. We develop stochastic theory predicting the implications of these dynamics for ageing and disease manifestation and discuss its application to human mtDNA dynamics.

### Jason Coates — 2018-07-03T09:42:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-heteroplasmy.21555/post-244833>

A mathematical model that can predict the nature of the mitochondrial DNA a child will inherit from its mother was part of a study that also found that maternal age counts in mitochondrial health, too.

This part of the study, in mice, showed that the older the mother, the higher is the risk that mutated, disease-causing mitochondrial DNA will be passed along to offspring.

The study, “[Large-scale genetic analysis reveals mammalian mtDNA heteroplasmy dynamics and variance increase through lifetimes and generations](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-04797-2),” was published in the journal _[Nature Communications](https://www.nature.com/ncomms/)_.

Most cells have multiple mitochondria, which house the mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) — an integral component of the cell’s energy-producing machine.

mtDNA can acquire mutations over time, which can lead to different mtDNA sequences within different mitochondria of the same cell. This phenomenon, called heteroplasmy, occurs when a cell has some mitochondria with a mutation in its mtDNA and some that does not.

Heteroplasmy can influence the inheritance and onset of serious mitochondrial diseases, which develop due to mutations in key mitochondrial genes.

People inherit their mitochondria from their mothers. If the mother has heteroplasmy, it can be difficult to determine how this will affect an unborn child.

In particular, scientists don’t know how heteroplasmy changes over time in women, and whether the risk of transmitting a disease-carrying mitochondrial mutation increases or decreases.

Researchers in Europe analyzed mtDNA segregation in two mouse lines with heteroplasmy, using mothers with a range of ages. Measurements were then taken from 1,947 oocytes \[immature egg cells\] and 899 pups.

Using this extensive data, they were able to create a mathematical model that was descriptive of mtDNA inheritance — they transfer of mitochondrial DNA from mother to offspring, taking maternal age into account.
[https://mitochondrialdiseasenews.co...ail&utm\_term=0\_fdcf314ce5-8ecfca31ec-71855073](https://mitochondrialdiseasenews.com/2018/06/29/older-moms-more-likely-to-pass-mutated-mitochrondrial-dna-to-offspring-study-finds/?utm_source=Mito+Disease&utm_campaign=8ecfca31ec-RSS_EMAIL_CAMPAIGN&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_fdcf314ce5-8ecfca31ec-71855073)

So then, I would like to know if taking it sublingually is best. I am looking to relieve symptoms of decreased short term memory, heightened stress response, and some remaining inability to stay asleep longer than 6 hours at times, especially mid cycle. BTW, I am 51 yo, perimenopausal. Thanks so much for any input!!

Hi, round2...I am very glad to encounter new folks on the forum, like you, who are having success with the Epi-PaleoRx approach to life. The reason that I am writing this note is to confirm your comment that wild yams are not the 'real deal'. Let me add that claims for the effectiveness of wild yam cream are based on the fact that wild yams contain a precursor of steroid hormones called diosgenin. However, diosgenin itself has no hormonal activity and can’t be converted in the human body into anything that does.

Thanks for all the great replies, everyone. My labs, drawn day 18 of cycle (luteal phase), were: progesterone 4.7 ng/ml (normal luteal range here is 2.6-21.5); and estradiol 94 pg/ml (normal range here is 56-214 approx.). So, I am in the low end of normal for both. I get 50 for the Pg:E2 ratio, not anything near the 100-500 goal, so it seems my progesterone level needs to more than double, if keeping E2 the same. I will ask my doctor for the Prometrium.
Does anyone have advice on the best way to take this as Jack says above? Is it advisable to take the micronized powder (from capsule), mix it with olive oil, and inject; to apply the powder alone; or is a suppository necessary? multiple online sites say it is "sparingly soluble in vegetable oil." thanks for any advice on this!

Pg:E2 ratio, not anything near the 100-500 goal

Look up your estrogen ranges (56-214)pg/mL (likely blood was tested)
and
within the sample report (1.3-3.3)pg/mL
They are different, blood vs saliva.
\=====================
Be careful about that ratio.
As far as I can tel it applies only to saliva testing.
And the testing laboratory will figure that ratio for you.

Great listen thanks again Dr. Kruse. Loved hearing how Dr. Wallace got his start in mitochondrial research an original thinker I think Dr. Kruse would define as genius. Just getting started in understanding all this but here is my takeaways. Mitochondria control nuclear dna based on energy availability (well covered by Dr. Kruse but very important). As mitochondria energy decreased parts of the body that consume the most energy are affected first (brain being #1). The mitochondria power plant output differs with different mitochondria dna. Dr. Wallace said complex 2 is least important would like to know your guys take on this thought I heard somewhere that complex 1 could be completely bypassed possibly related to the PPP. ROS from inefficient mitochondria affects mytophogy causing nuclear genome changes. Energy output from mitochondria affects your ability to handle stress (Ican second that). Pink-1 and Parkan (not sure if theseare the exact right names or spelling) are proteins that control mytophogy from the nuclear side. Are these epigenetically controlled by MTOR? Restricting diet by 40-60% increases MTOR by 30%. What are some things that influence MTOR positively? Some mitochondria dna defects are weeded out by nature bynot allowing inefficienct eggs to procreate. An uncoupled mitochondria are better at cold adapting.Training at high altitude helps performance at low altitude (I’ll know I’m doing good when I can explainthis in detail). Uncoupled mitochondria handle fat better than coupled. Certain mitochondria dna adapt better to our modern environment than others. Diggingthe connection with vaccines Dr. Kruse provided as sad as it is.

“When jarred, unavoidably, by circumstance, revert at once to yourself, and don’t lose the rhythm more than you can help. You’ll have a better group of harmony if you keep ongoing back to it.” This reversion back to the circadian mechanism is the biases nature requires........we forget this all too easily.

This is why vaccines need to be studied like we study drugs.........we do not realize the mode of delivery may be the real problem with respect to melanopsin. This is why settled science should be fought when new data shows up that makes us ask better questions that need answers.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-05T17:45:17+0300

[Debra Gambrell](https://www.facebook.com/debra.gambrell?fref=ufi&rc=p) said (anesthesia), "We now have a situation where children's hospitals will not do surgery unless a child is fully vaccinated before the surgery. As little as ten years ago we used to ask when the most recent vaccination was given because we felt a two-week minimum window was important between vaccines and anesthesia. They have thrown that idea out of the window and now vaccinate during surgery. (Implied consent for all "biologics.")

A few years ago I looked into the half-life of sevoflurane (a halogenated fluorocarbon) and learned it was found in the blood three days after surgery.

From studies over 25 years ago, we know that the combination of fluoride and aluminum easily gets by the BBB. But no one in the OR talks to anyone outside of the OR so this vaccination during surgery crap will continue.

My two cents to her comment: Our real problem in medicine now is that the thinking docs are under attack by the non-thinking docs and their evidence-based recipes based on flawed thinking. I just had to block one of those docs on Twitter this week. She is a leftist OB who thinks this topic is 100% settled. She has no Earthly idea how the melanopsin paper changes the questions we should be asking researchers to answer to see if our beliefs are plumb with nature's circadian cycles. That blocked doctor was Jen Gunter on Twitter.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-05T19:16:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measure-ketones.21563/post-245049>

Blood ketone measurement is by far the most accurate method of testing.

The meters themselves are not that expensive ~ it's the cost of the strips that can be prohibitive for many.

[@shiran](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20240/) ... Best wishes for your friend or family member.

### shiran — 2018-07-07T15:34:27+0300

### Marko Pollo — 2018-07-07T16:35:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measure-ketones.21563/post-245226>

Check out Ketonix. They sell a ketone breath meter that works as advertised. Non invasive way to get feedback when going Keto and a way to see how different changes affect ketone levels. Site has good info. I don’t use mine often now, but when I started Keto I liked seeing how my ketone charged during the day and climbed over time.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-07T17:31:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measure-ketones.21563/post-245231>

> [Marko Pollo said:](/goto/post?id=245226)
>
> Check out Ketonix. They sell a ketone breath meter that works as advertised. Non invasive way to get feedback when going Keto and a way to see how different changes affect ketone levels. Site has good info. I don’t use mine often now, but when I started Keto I liked seeing how my ketone charged during the day and climbed over time.

Had one when they first came out years ago ~ and yes, it was interesting. But for anyone who wants/needs true accuracy, blood ketone measurement is the way to go. Not knowing what sort of situation we are talking about here ... I'm assuming health condition ~ and I would not recommend the Ketonix under those circumstances.

### shiran — 2018-07-08T07:12:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measure-ketones.21563/post-245282>

### Jack Kruse — 2018-07-06T02:53:51+0300

### Sean Waters — 2018-07-06T14:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-moving-for-my-health-would-like-to-live-with-people-from-this-site.21567/post-245132>

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seans-optimal-journal.20757/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seans-optimal-journal.20757/)

Check out my journal mate... the last couple pages are most relevant to your situation.

I'm 25, live in a city, had battles with autoimmunity (Crohns and eczema)~ plus fatigue and depression.

I moved to Mexico in March for 2 months. Now i'm back in London better than ever. Check it out - it might save you a trip.

I do believe we all have to walk our own paths, so if you do want a roommate in yucatan mexico for a few months - give [@shah78](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1152/) a message or join the Quantum Healing in the Yucatan facebook group and post a message asking for a room like i did... Shah78, if you PM me i will give you his full name so you can fb message him direct, he reached out to me on there and i stayed for 2 months to figure things out - it wasn't what i expected, and it didn't "heal me" as i though, but it raised my dopamine so much that it enlightened me to know what i need. I missed my family and friends out there so much, i agree with Jack 100%. Beware, it may not heal you, but it will show you what you need.
